AWS CLI Complete Documentation - anihpj Django Project

Comprehensive AWS CLI reference with practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project with its jobpost app (Skills, Author, Location, User, and JobPost models)

AWS CLI v2 Django 5.2.5 Python 3.11+ Docker & pytest Cloud Integration

🚀 AWS CLI Complete Documentation for anihpj Django Project

📚 Table of Contents

🎯 Navigate through comprehensive AWS services... Each section includes practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project and its jobpost app models (Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost) with real AWS CLI commands, Docker deployments, and pytest integration.

⚡ Compute Services

AWS Compute services provide the infrastructure to run your anihpj Django application. This section covers EC2 for virtual machines, Lambda for serverless functions, Elastic Beanstalk for managed deployments, and Fargate for containerized workloads. Learn how to deploy and manage your jobpost application models (Skills, Author, Location, User, JobPost) across different compute platforms.

💡 Django Focus: This documentation shows practical examples of deploying the anihpj Django project to AWS Compute services using the AWS CLI, Docker containers, and pytest for testing the jobpost app models.

Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)

Virtual servers in the cloud for deploying Django applications

📋 EC2 Overview

Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) provides resizable compute capacity in the cloud. It allows you to deploy your anihpj Django application on virtual machines with full control over networking, storage, and security. EC2 instances run your jobpost application with models for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost data.

Core EC2 Operations for Django Deployment

run-instances - Launch new EC2 instances
describe-instances - Get instance information
start-instances - Start stopped instances
stop-instances - Stop running instances
terminate-instances - Delete instances
reboot-instances - Restart instances
create-security-group - Create firewall rules
authorize-security-group-ingress - Allow inbound traffic

run-instances

Launches one or more EC2 instances for your Django application. This creates new virtual machine instances in AWS that will host your jobpost application with Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models.

Syntax
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id {ami-id} \
  --count {count} \
  --instance-type {instance-type} \
  --key-name {key-name} \
  --security-group-ids {security-group-id} \
  --subnet-id {subnet-id} \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value={value}}]'
Parameters Description
  • --image-id (string, required): The ID of the AMI (Amazon Machine Image) to use. Example: ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 for Amazon Linux 2 with Docker pre-installed
  • --count (integer): Number of instances to launch. Default: 1. For Django app with HA, use 2-3
  • --instance-type (string): Type of instance (t2.micro, t2.small, t3.medium, etc.). For development use t2.micro (free tier), for production use t3.medium or larger
  • --key-name (string, required): Name of the EC2 Key Pair for SSH access
  • --security-group-ids (list): List of security group IDs to apply to the instance
  • --subnet-id (string): VPC Subnet ID where instance will be launched
  • --tag-specifications (string): Tags to apply to resources (Key=Name,Value=anihpj-django-server)
  • --user-data (string): Base64-encoded user data script to run on instance startup (for installing Docker, Python, Django)
Return Value
  • Instances (Array): Information about the newly created instances
  • InstanceId (string): Unique identifier for the instance
  • State (string): Current state (pending, running, stopped, terminated)
  • PublicIpAddress (string): Public IP address for accessing the instance
5 Detailed EC2 Examples
Example 1: Launch t2.micro Django Development Server

Launch a single t2.micro EC2 instance for development and testing of the anihpj jobpost application. This is ideal for development environments and uses the free tier.

#!/bin/bash
# Launch t2.micro development instance
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --count 1 \
  --instance-type t2.micro \
  --key-name anihpj-dev-key \
  --security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-jobpost-dev},{Key=Environment,Value=development}]' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Instances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "ImageId": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0", "InstanceType": "t2.micro", "State": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "LaunchTime": "2025-01-11T14:23:45.000Z", "PublicIpAddress": "54.123.45.67", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.1.50", "SecurityGroups": [ { "GroupId": "sg-0123456789abcdef0", "GroupName": "django-app-sg" } ], "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-dev" }, { "Key": "Environment", "Value": "development" } ] } ] }
# Django Model Representation (after EC2 instance ready) # This instance will run Django with these models: from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Instance deployment status instance_deployment = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'public_ip': '54.123.45.67', 'private_ip': '10.0.1.50', 'instance_type': 't2.micro', 'status': 'pending', 'models_available': ['Skills', 'User', 'Author', 'Location', 'JobPost'] } # Django apps running on this instance: # - jobpost.apps.JobpostConfig (main app) # - Contains models: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # - Running on port 8000 # Query: Check if models are migrated Skills.objects.all() # Should return empty initially User.objects.all() # Should return empty initially Author.objects.all() # Should return empty initially Location.objects.all() # Should return empty initially JobPost.objects.all() # Should return empty initially
Example 2: Launch t3.medium Production Django Server with User Data

Launch a t3.medium EC2 instance configured with Docker, Python, Django, and PostgreSQL for production deployment of the anihpj jobpost application. Includes automatic setup script.

#!/bin/bash
# Create user data script for automatic setup
USER_DATA=$(cat <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# Update system packages
yum update -y

# Install Docker
amazon-linux-extras install docker -y
systemctl start docker
systemctl enable docker
usermod -a -G docker ec2-user

# Install Python 3.11 and pip
yum install python3.11 python3.11-pip -y

# Install PostgreSQL client
yum install postgresql -y

# Clone Django project
cd /home/ec2-user
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/anihpj-jobpost.git
cd anihpj-jobpost

# Install Django dependencies
pip3.11 install -r requirements.txt

# Run migrations for Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models
python manage.py migrate

# Collect static files
python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Start Django development server
python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000 &
EOF
)

# Base64 encode user data
USER_DATA_B64=$(echo -n "$USER_DATA" | base64)

# Launch t3.medium production instance
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --count 1 \
  --instance-type t3.medium \
  --key-name anihpj-prod-key \
  --security-group-ids sg-0987654321abcdef0 \
  --subnet-id subnet-0987654321abcdef0 \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=anihpj-ec2-role \
  --user-data "$USER_DATA_B64" \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-jobpost-prod},{Key=Environment,Value=production},{Key=App,Value=jobpost}]' \
  --monitoring Enabled=true \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Instances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "ImageId": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0", "InstanceType": "t3.medium", "State": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "LaunchTime": "2025-01-11T14:35:22.000Z", "PublicIpAddress": "54.234.56.78", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.2.100", "Monitoring": { "State": "enabled" }, "IamInstanceProfile": { "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:instance-profile/anihpj-ec2-role" }, "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-prod" }, { "Key": "Environment", "Value": "production" }, { "Key": "App", "Value": "jobpost" } ] } ] }
# Django Production Instance Setup Status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser # After user data script executes, the instance will have: production_config = { 'instance_id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'instance_type': 't3.medium', 'public_ip': '54.234.56.78', 'private_ip': '10.0.2.100', 'monitoring': 'enabled', 'software_stack': [ 'Docker Engine', 'Python 3.11', 'Django 5.2.5', 'PostgreSQL Client', 'Git' ], 'models_migrated': True } # Create initial admin user (via migration script) admin_user = DjangoUser.objects.create_superuser( username='admin', email='admin@anihpj.com', password='secure_password' ) # Create sample Skills skill1 = Skills.objects.create(name='Python', level='Advanced') skill2 = Skills.objects.create(name='Django', level='Advanced') skill3 = Skills.objects.create(name='AWS', level='Intermediate') print(f"Created {Skills.objects.count()} skills") # Output: Created 3 skills # Create sample Location location = Location.objects.create( city='San Francisco', state='California', country='USA', zip_code='94105' ) print(f"Locations in database: {Location.objects.count()}") # Output: Locations in database: 1 # Create sample Author author = Author.objects.create( user=admin_user, bio='Senior Python Developer', location=location ) print(f"Authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: Authors: 1
Example 3: Launch 2 t3.small Instances for HA Django Cluster

Launch two t3.small instances in different availability zones for high availability deployment of the jobpost application with load balancing.

#!/bin/bash
# Launch 2 t3.small instances for HA cluster
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --count 2 \
  --instance-type t3.small \
  --key-name anihpj-ha-key \
  --security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-jobpost-ha},{Key=Environment,Value=production},{Key=Cluster,Value=primary},{Key=App,Value=jobpost}]' \
  --placement 'AvailabilityZone=us-east-1a' \
  --monitoring Enabled=true \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --output json > ha-instances.json

# Also launch one more in different AZ
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --count 1 \
  --instance-type t3.small \
  --key-name anihpj-ha-key \
  --security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-jobpost-ha-backup},{Key=Environment,Value=production},{Key=Cluster,Value=backup}]' \
  --placement 'AvailabilityZone=us-east-1b' \
  --monitoring Enabled=true \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Instances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4", "InstanceType": "t3.small", "State": { "Name": "pending" }, "PublicIpAddress": "54.145.67.89", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.1.25", "Placement": { "AvailabilityZone": "us-east-1a" }, "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-ha" }, { "Key": "Cluster", "Value": "primary" } ] }, { "InstanceId": "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi", "InstanceType": "t3.small", "State": { "Name": "pending" }, "PublicIpAddress": "54.156.78.90", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.1.26", "Placement": { "AvailabilityZone": "us-east-1a" }, "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-ha" }, { "Key": "Cluster", "Value": "primary" } ] } ] } # Secondary instance in different AZ: { "Instances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw", "InstanceType": "t3.small", "State": { "Name": "pending" }, "PublicIpAddress": "54.234.90.12", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.2.30", "Placement": { "AvailabilityZone": "us-east-1b" }, "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-ha-backup" }, { "Key": "Cluster", "Value": "backup" } ] } ] }
# HA Cluster Django Model Distribution from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # HA Cluster configuration cluster_nodes = { 'primary_az': { 'instances': [ 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', # anihpj-jobpost-ha (10.0.1.25) 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi' # anihpj-jobpost-ha (10.0.1.26) ], 'public_ips': ['54.145.67.89', '54.156.78.90'], 'availability_zone': 'us-east-1a', 'role': 'primary' }, 'backup_az': { 'instances': [ 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw' # anihpj-jobpost-ha-backup (10.0.2.30) ], 'public_ips': ['54.234.90.12'], 'availability_zone': 'us-east-1b', 'role': 'backup' } } # Distributed database replication # Primary instances sync with backup for failover # Example queries across the cluster: all_skills = Skills.objects.all().values_list('id', 'name', 'level') # Output on all 3 instances: (initially empty) # Create location across cluster locations = Location.objects.bulk_create([ Location(city='New York', state='NY', country='USA'), Location(city='Los Angeles', state='CA', country='USA'), Location(city='Chicago', state='IL', country='USA'), ]) print(f"Created {len(locations)} locations") # Output: Created 3 locations # Check replication status on backup backup_locations = Location.objects.all().count() print(f"Backup node has {backup_locations} locations") # Output: Backup node has 3 locations
Example 4: Launch GPU Instance for ML Model Training

Launch a GPU-enabled instance (g4dn.xlarge) for training machine learning models on job recommendations and skill matching for the jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Launch GPU instance for ML training
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --count 1 \
  --instance-type g4dn.xlarge \
  --key-name anihpj-ml-key \
  --security-group-ids sg-0456789012abcdef0 \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=anihpj-ml-role \
  --block-device-mappings '[{DeviceName:/dev/xvda,Ebs:{VolumeSize=100,VolumeType=gp3,DeleteOnTermination=true}}]' \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-ml-trainer},{Key=Purpose,Value=skill-matching-ml},{Key=GPU,Value=enabled}]' \
  --monitoring Enabled=true \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for instance to be in running state
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids i-0p9o8n7m6l5k4j3i2 --region us-east-1
{ "Instances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0p9o8n7m6l5k4j3i2", "InstanceType": "g4dn.xlarge", "State": { "Name": "pending" }, "PublicIpAddress": "54.245.123.45", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.3.50", "BlockDeviceMappings": [ { "DeviceName": "/dev/xvda", "Ebs": { "VolumeId": "vol-0123456789abcdef0", "VolumeSize": 100, "VolumeType": "gp3", "Status": "attaching" } } ], "GpuDevices": [ { "Count": 1, "Name": "NVIDIA Tesla T4", "Memory": 16000 } ], "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-ml-trainer" }, { "Key": "Purpose", "Value": "skill-matching-ml" }, { "Key": "GPU", "Value": "enabled" } ] } ] } # Instance is starting up with: # - GPU: NVIDIA Tesla T4 (16GB VRAM) # - vCPUs: 4 # - Memory: 16 GiB # - Storage: 100 GiB gp3 SSD
# ML Training Instance for Job Recommendation Model from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q # ML Instance training data preparation training_config = { 'instance_id': 'i-0p9o8n7m6l5k4j3i2', 'instance_type': 'g4dn.xlarge', 'gpu': 'NVIDIA Tesla T4 (16GB)', 'training_purpose': 'Job recommendation ML model', 'models_used': ['Skills', 'User', 'JobPost', 'Author'] } # Prepare training data from Django models # Get all skills for feature encoding all_skills = Skills.objects.all() print(f"Total skills in dataset: {all_skills.count()}") # Output: Total skills in dataset: 0 # Get all job posts for training labels job_posts = JobPost.objects.prefetch_related('author', 'location', 'required_skills').all() print(f"Available job posts for training: {job_posts.count()}") # Output: Available job posts for training: 0 # Prepare job posts by location (geographic features for ML) jobs_by_location = JobPost.objects.values('location__city').annotate(count=Count('id')).order_by('-count') print("Jobs by location distribution:") for loc in jobs_by_location: print(f" {loc['location__city']}: {loc['count']} jobs") # Output: (empty initially) # Prepare skills frequency in job posts skill_frequency = Skills.objects.annotate(job_count=Count('jobpost')).order_by('-job_count') print("\nMost required skills:") for skill in skill_frequency[:10]: print(f" {skill.name}: {skill.job_count} jobs") # Output: (empty initially) # ML feature engineering from User and Author data authors_with_skills = Author.objects.filter(skills__isnull=False).distinct().count() print(f"\nAuthors with skills: {authors_with_skills}") # Output: Authors with skills: 0
Example 5: Launch Spot Instance with Custom VPC and Networking

Launch a spot instance for cost-effective development of jobpost application with custom VPC, security groups, and network interface configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Create custom VPC for jobpost app (if not exists)
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 --region us-east-1 --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)

# Create subnet
SUBNET_ID=$(aws ec2 create-subnet --vpc-id $VPC_ID --cidr-block 10.0.1.0/24 --region us-east-1 --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# Create security group for Django application
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name anihpj-jobpost-sg \
  --description "Security group for anihpj jobpost Django app" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'GroupId' \
  --output text)

# Allow SSH access
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 22 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --region us-east-1

# Allow HTTP and HTTPS
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 80 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --region us-east-1

aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 443 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --region us-east-1

# Allow Django development server (8000)
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 8000 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --region us-east-1

# Launch spot instance
aws ec2 request-spot-instances \
  --spot-price "0.05" \
  --instance-count 1 \
  --type "one-time" \
  --launch-specification '{
    "ImageId":"ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
    "InstanceType":"t2.small",
    "KeyName":"anihpj-dev-key",
    "SecurityGroupIds":["'$SG_ID'"],
    "SubnetId":"'$SUBNET_ID'",
    "Monitoring":{"Enabled":true},
    "TagSpecifications":[{
      "ResourceType":"instance",
      "Tags":[
        {"Key":"Name","Value":"anihpj-jobpost-spot"},
        {"Key":"App","Value":"jobpost"},
        {"Key":"Type","Value":"spot"}
      ]
    }]
  }' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "SpotInstanceRequests": [ { "SpotInstanceRequestId": "sir-0abc123def456", "State": "open", "Status": { "Code": "pending-evaluation", "Message": "Your Spot request is being evaluated." }, "LaunchSpecification": { "ImageId": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0", "InstanceType": "t2.small", "KeyName": "anihpj-dev-key", "SecurityGroups": [ { "GroupId": "sg-0123456789abcdef0" } ] }, "CreateTime": "2025-01-11T14:52:10.000Z", "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-spot" }, { "Key": "App", "Value": "jobpost" } ] } ] } # Spot request processing output: # # SpotInstanceRequest Status: # ID: sir-0abc123def456 # State: open (request submitted) # Status: pending-evaluation (AWS evaluating availability) # Price: $0.05/hour (vs on-demand ~$0.23/hour) # # VPC and Networking created: # VPC CIDR: 10.0.0.0/16 # Subnet: 10.0.1.0/24 in us-east-1a # Security Groups configured for SSH (22), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), Django (8000)
# Spot Instance Django Environment Setup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.conf import settings # Spot instance configuration (cost-optimized) spot_config = { 'spot_request_id': 'sir-0abc123def456', 'instance_type': 't2.small', 'price_per_hour': 0.05, 'cost_vs_ondemand': 'Saves ~$0.18/hour (78% cheaper)', 'expected_monthly_cost': 36.50, # ~150 hours/month 'availability': 'May be interrupted by AWS' } # VPC and networking configuration vpc_config = { 'vpc_id': '10.0.0.0/16', 'subnet': '10.0.1.0/24', 'availability_zone': 'us-east-1a', 'security_groups': { 'ssh': 'port 22 (0.0.0.0/0)', 'http': 'port 80 (0.0.0.0/0)', 'https': 'port 443 (0.0.0.0/0)', 'django_dev': 'port 8000 (0.0.0.0/0)' } } # Django application configuration for spot instance # Once instance is running, these models will be available: Skills.objects.all() # Skills model ready # Output: Author.objects.select_related('user', 'location').all() # Authors with relationships # Output: Location.objects.all() # Job locations # Output: JobPost.objects.filter(author__isnull=False).count() # Valid job postings # Output: 0 # Cost tracking query (add to models if needed) cost_tracking = { 'instance_type': 't2.small', 'launch_time': '2025-01-11T14:52:10.000Z', 'hourly_cost': 0.05, 'monthly_estimate': 36.50, 'django_apps': ['jobpost'], 'models': ['Skills', 'User', 'Author', 'Location', 'JobPost'] }

describe-instances

Retrieves detailed information about EC2 instances. Shows status, IP addresses, security groups, and other configuration details for managing your Django application instances. Essential for monitoring, debugging, and managing your jobpost application deployment.

Syntax
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids {instance-id} \
  --filters "Name=tag:Name,Values={tag-value}" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,State.Name,PublicIpAddress,PrivateIpAddress,InstanceType]' \
  --output table
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list): Specific instance IDs to describe. Optional - if omitted, returns all instances. Example: i-0987654321fedcba0
  • --filters (list): Filter instances by various criteria. Common filters: instance-state-name (running, stopped, terminated), tag:Name (tag value), vpc-id, subnet-id, security-group-id
  • --query (string): JMESPath query to extract specific fields from output. Example: 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,State.Name]'
  • --output (string): Output format - text, json, table. Recommended: json for parsing, table for viewing
  • --max-results (integer): Maximum number of results to return (1-1000). Default: all instances
  • --next-token (string): Token for pagination when results exceed max-results
Return Value
  • Reservations (Array): Container for instances grouped by reservation
  • Instances (Array): List of EC2 instances with details
  • InstanceId (string): Unique identifier for the instance
  • State (Object): Current state (pending, running, stopping, stopped, shutting-down, terminated)
  • PublicIpAddress (string): Public IP address (if assigned)
  • PrivateIpAddress (string): Private IP within VPC
  • Tags (Array): Key-value pairs for organizing instances
5 Detailed EC2 describe-instances Examples
Example 1: List All Running Django Instances with Key Details

Query all running EC2 instances tagged as anihpj-jobpost and display essential information for monitoring deployed Django applications.

#!/bin/bash
# List all running anihpj Django instances
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
             "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name,PublicIpAddress,PrivateIpAddress,LaunchTime,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
--------------------------------------------------------------------------- | Describing EC2 Instances --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | INSTANCEID | INSTANCETYPE | STATE | PUBLIC IP | PRIVATE IP | LAUNCH TIME | NAME |--------------------------------------------------------------------------- | i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | t3.small | running | 54.145.67.89 | 10.0.1.25 | 2025-01-11T14:35:22.000Z | anihpj-jobpost-ha | i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi | t3.small | running | 54.156.78.90 | 10.0.1.26 | 2025-01-11T14:35:45.000Z | anihpj-jobpost-ha | i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw | t3.small | running | 54.234.90.12 | 10.0.2.30 | 2025-01-11T14:36:10.000Z | anihpj-jobpost-ha-backup ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Django Query: Map EC2 instances to jobpost models from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q # EC2 instance inventory from describe-instances ec2_instances = { 'running': [ {'id': 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'type': 't3.small', 'ip': '54.145.67.89', 'private': '10.0.1.25'}, {'id': 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi', 'type': 't3.small', 'ip': '54.156.78.90', 'private': '10.0.1.26'}, {'id': 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw', 'type': 't3.small', 'ip': '54.234.90.12', 'private': '10.0.2.30'} ], 'total_running': 3 } print(f"Total running instances: {ec2_instances['total_running']}") # Output: Total running instances: 3 # Each instance runs Django with these models: for instance in ec2_instances['running']: print(f"Instance {instance['id']} ({instance['ip']}) running:") print(f" - Skills model on port 8000") print(f" - User authentication service") print(f" - Author content processing") print(f" - Location job postings") print(f" - JobPost aggregation") # Query: Count skills in database across all instances (replicated) total_skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nTotal skills in jobpost database: {total_skills}") # Output: Total skills in jobpost database: 0 # Query: Count authors with job postings authors_with_jobs = Author.objects.filter(jobpost__isnull=False).distinct().count() print(f"Authors with job postings: {authors_with_jobs}") # Output: Authors with job postings: 0 # Query: Count total job posts across locations locations_with_jobs = Location.objects.filter(jobpost__isnull=False).distinct().count() print(f"Locations with job postings: {locations_with_jobs}") # Output: Locations with job postings: 0
Example 2: Describe Specific Development Instance in JSON Format

Get comprehensive details of a specific development instance in JSON format for programmatic parsing and configuration management.

#!/bin/bash
# Get detailed JSON output for specific development instance
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids i-0987654321fedcba0 \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0]' \
  --output json \
  --region us-east-1 > dev-instance-details.json

# Display the JSON file
cat dev-instance-details.json
{ "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "ImageId": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0", "InstanceType": "t2.micro", "State": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" }, "PublicIpAddress": "54.123.45.67", "PrivateIpAddress": "10.0.1.50", "SubnetId": "subnet-0123456789abcdef0", "VpcId": "vpc-0123456789abcdef0", "SecurityGroups": [ { "GroupId": "sg-0123456789abcdef0", "GroupName": "django-app-sg" } ], "KeyName": "anihpj-dev-key", "LaunchTime": "2025-01-11T14:23:45.000Z", "Monitoring": { "State": "disabled" }, "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-jobpost-dev" }, { "Key": "Environment", "Value": "development" } ] }
# Development Instance Configuration Mapping to Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser # Instance configuration loaded from describe-instances dev_instance = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'image_id': 'ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0', 'instance_type': 't2.micro', 'state': 'running', 'public_ip': '54.123.45.67', 'private_ip': '10.0.1.50', 'subnet': 'subnet-0123456789abcdef0', 'vpc': 'vpc-0123456789abcdef0', 'security_group': 'sg-0123456789abcdef0', 'key_name': 'anihpj-dev-key', 'launch_time': '2025-01-11T14:23:45.000Z', 'environment': 'development' } # Django configuration on this instance: print(f"Django development server on {dev_instance['public_ip']}:8000") # Output: Django development server on 54.123.45.67:8000 # Create test user on development instance test_user = DjangoUser( username='dev_tester', email='dev@anihpj.local', is_staff=True, is_superuser=False ) print(f"Development instance ready for testing jobpost models") # Test Skills model Skills.objects.create(name='Testing', level='Beginner') print(f"Skills created: {Skills.objects.count()}") # Output: Skills created: 1 # Test Author model author = Author.objects.create(user=test_user, bio='Development tester') print(f"Authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: Authors: 1 # Test JobPost model creation location = Location.objects.create(city='Dev Local', country='Local') job = JobPost.objects.create( title='Test Job', author=author, location=location, status='draft' ) print(f"Draft job posts: {JobPost.objects.filter(status='draft').count()}") # Output: Draft job posts: 1
Example 3: Filter Instances by Multiple Criteria and Export

Use advanced filtering to find instances matching specific tags and states, then export details for inventory management.

#!/bin/bash
# Filter instances by environment and instance type
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=production" \
             "Name=instance-type,Values=t3.medium,t3.small" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name,PublicIpAddress,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0],Tags[?Key==`Environment`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output json | jq '.[] | .[]' > production-instances.json

# Get instance count
INSTANCE_COUNT=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=production" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query 'length(Reservations[*].Instances[*][])'  \
  --output text)

echo "Total production instances running: $INSTANCE_COUNT"
# Filtered results for production anihpj instances: [ { "instance_id": "i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "instance_type": "t3.medium", "state": "running", "public_ip": "54.234.56.78", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-prod", "environment": "production" }, { "instance_id": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4", "instance_type": "t3.small", "state": "running", "public_ip": "54.145.67.89", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-ha", "environment": "production" }, { "instance_id": "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi", "instance_type": "t3.small", "state": "running", "public_ip": "54.156.78.90", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-ha", "environment": "production" } ] Total production instances running: 3
# Production inventory analysis with Django models from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q from datetime import datetime # Production instances inventory production_instances = [ {'id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'type': 't3.medium', 'ip': '54.234.56.78', 'name': 'anihpj-jobpost-prod'}, {'id': 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'type': 't3.small', 'ip': '54.145.67.89', 'name': 'anihpj-jobpost-ha'}, {'id': 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi', 'type': 't3.small', 'ip': '54.156.78.90', 'name': 'anihpj-jobpost-ha'} ] print(f"Production inventory: {len(production_instances)} instances") # Output: Production inventory: 3 instances # Production database metrics across all instances all_authors = Author.objects.filter(user__is_active=True).count() active_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count() total_locations = Location.objects.values('id').distinct().count() print(f"Active authors: {all_authors}") # Output: Active authors: 0 print(f"Published job posts: {active_jobs}") # Output: Published job posts: 0 print(f"Unique locations: {total_locations}") # Output: Unique locations: 0 # Production capacity query production_capacity = { 'total_instances': len(production_instances), 'storage_type': 'EBS gp3', 'max_throughput_mbps': 1000, 'max_iops': 20000, 'instance_breakdown': { 't3.medium': 1, # Primary database and app server 't3.small': 2 # HA replicas } } print(f"\nProduction capacity configured: {production_capacity}") # Output: Production capacity configured: {...}
Example 4: Monitor Instance Status and Health Checks

Get detailed status information including system status checks, instance status checks, and resource utilization for all running instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Get instance status checks for all running jobpost instances
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,State.Name,InstanceType,[SystemStatus.Status,InstanceStatus.Status],PrivateIpAddress]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1

# Alternative: Get status with more detail
aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[*].[InstanceId,InstanceStatus.Status,SystemStatus.Status,InstanceState.Name]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
------------------------------------------------------------------------ | Instance Status Check Results ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | INSTANCEID | STATE | TYPE | SYSTEM | INSTANCE | IP ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | running | t3.small | ok | ok | 10.0.1.25 | i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi | running | t3.small | ok | ok | 10.0.1.26 | i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw | running | t3.small | ok | ok | 10.0.2.30 ------------------------------------------------------------------------ Instance Status Check Output: INSTANCEID | INSTANCE STATUS | SYSTEM STATUS | STATE i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | ok | ok | running i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi | ok | ok | running i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw | ok | ok | running
# Instance health monitoring mapped to Django metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q, Count, F import json # Instance health status from EC2 instance_health = [ { 'instance_id': 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'ip': '10.0.1.25', 'instance_status': 'ok', 'system_status': 'ok', 'state': 'running' }, { 'instance_id': 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi', 'ip': '10.0.1.26', 'instance_status': 'ok', 'system_status': 'ok', 'state': 'running' }, { 'instance_id': 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw', 'ip': '10.0.2.30', 'instance_status': 'ok', 'system_status': 'ok', 'state': 'running' } ] # Check Django application health on each instance all_healthy = all(inst['instance_status'] == 'ok' and inst['system_status'] == 'ok' for inst in instance_health) print(f"All instances healthy: {all_healthy}") # Output: All instances healthy: True # Application level metrics users_active = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() jobs_total = JobPost.objects.count() skills_available = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nApplication metrics:") print(f" Active users: {users_active}") # Output: Active users: 0 print(f" Total job posts: {jobs_total}") # Output: Total job posts: 0 print(f" Available skills: {skills_available}") # Output: Available skills: 0 # Health summary health_report = { 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(), 'instances_healthy': sum(1 for i in instance_health if i['instance_status'] == 'ok'), 'total_instances': len(instance_health), 'app_data': { 'users': users_active, 'jobs': jobs_total, 'skills': skills_available } } print(f"\nHealth report: {json.dumps(health_report, indent=2)}")
Example 5: List Stopped Instances and Plan Recovery

Find all stopped instances for the jobpost application and gather information needed to restart them and verify data integrity.

#!/bin/bash
# Find all stopped jobpost instances
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=stopped" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name,PublicIpAddress,PrivateIpAddress,StateTransitionReason,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1

# Get cost savings from stopped instances
STOPPED_COUNT=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=stopped" \
  --query 'length(Reservations[*].Instances[*][])' \
  --output text)

# Estimate savings (t3.small costs ~$0.0376/hour, stopped = free)
MONTHLY_SAVINGS=$(echo "scale=2; $STOPPED_COUNT * 30 * 24 * 0.0376" | bc)

echo "Stopped instances: $STOPPED_COUNT"
echo "Estimated monthly storage cost: $MONTHLY_SAVINGS (storage only, compute is $0)"

# Show detailed info for restart planning
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:App,Values=jobpost" \
             "Name=instance-state-name,Values=stopped" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,StateTransitionReason,Tags]' \
  --output json > stopped-instances-recovery.json
Stopped instances: 2 Estimated monthly storage cost: 18.05 (storage only, compute is $0) Instance Recovery Planning: [ { "instance_id": "i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4", "instance_type": "t3.small", "state_reason": "User initiated (2025-01-10 13:45:30 GMT)", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-backup-1", "tags": { "Environment": "backup", "App": "jobpost" } }, { "instance_id": "i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4", "instance_type": "t3.small", "state_reason": "User initiated (2025-01-09 09:22:15 GMT)", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-backup-2", "tags": { "Environment": "backup", "App": "jobpost" } } ]
# Recovery planning with Django data consistency checks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser from django.db import connections from django.db.models import Count import datetime # Stopped instances from EC2 describe stopped_instances = [ { 'instance_id': 'i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4', 'type': 't3.small', 'stopped_since': '2025-01-10T13:45:30Z', 'name': 'anihpj-jobpost-backup-1' }, { 'instance_id': 'i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4', 'type': 't3.small', 'stopped_since': '2025-01-09T09:22:15Z', 'name': 'anihpj-jobpost-backup-2' } ] print(f"Stopped instances requiring recovery: {len(stopped_instances)}") # Output: Stopped instances requiring recovery: 2 # Pre-recovery data consistency check database_state = { 'users': User.objects.count(), 'authors': Author.objects.count(), 'locations': Location.objects.all().values('id').distinct().count(), 'jobs': JobPost.objects.count(), 'skills': Skills.objects.count() } print(f"Database state before recovery:") for model_name, count in database_state.items(): print(f" {model_name.capitalize()}: {count} records") # Output: # Users: 0 records # Authors: 0 records # Locations: 0 records # Jobs: 0 records # Skills: 0 records # Recovery checklist for each instance for instance in stopped_instances: print(f"\n{instance['name']} Recovery Checklist:") print(f" ✓ Verify database connection after restart") print(f" ✓ Check data consistency across models") print(f" ✓ Validate user sessions if any") print(f" ✓ Test jobpost app endpoints") print(f" ✓ Verify static files are accessible") # Expected cost savings while stopped print(f"\nCost Savings Analysis:") print(f" Storage cost (running): ~$0.10/month per instance") print(f" Compute savings: Full savings on stopped instances") print(f" Total stopped: {len(stopped_instances)} instances") print(f" Estimated monthly savings: ${len(stopped_instances) * 0.10:.2f}")

start-instances

Starts one or more stopped EC2 instances. Useful for resuming suspended Django application instances without terminating them. Restores the instance to running state and resumes billing for compute time.

Syntax
aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids {instance-id} \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list, required): One or more instance IDs to start. Example: i-0987654321fedcba0. Can accept multiple IDs separated by spaces
  • --region (string): AWS region where instances are located. Example: us-east-1, eu-west-1
  • --additional-info (string): Additional information about the start request (rarely used)
Return Value
  • StartingInstances (Array): List of instances being started
  • InstanceId (string): ID of the instance
  • CurrentState (Object): State transitioning to running
  • PreviousState (Object): State before start (was stopped)
5 Detailed EC2 start-instances Examples
Example 1: Start Single Development Instance

Resume a stopped development instance for testing the jobpost application after maintenance window.

#!/bin/bash
# Start single development instance
aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids i-0987654321fedcba0 \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for instance to be running
aws ec2 wait instance-running \
  --instance-ids i-0987654321fedcba0 \
  --region us-east-1

# Get the public IP after start
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids i-0987654321fedcba0 \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].[PublicIpAddress,PrivateIpAddress,State.Name]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
{ "StartingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } } ] } # Instance transitioning to running state... # Waiting for instance to reach running state... # Instance details after startup: PUBLICIPADDRESS | PRIVATEIPADDRESS | STATE 54.123.45.67 | 10.0.1.50 | running
# Development instance startup and Django initialization from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser from django.core.management import call_command import time # Instance start event instance_start = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'previous_state': 'stopped', 'current_state': 'pending', 'public_ip': '54.123.45.67', 'private_ip': '10.0.1.50', 'startup_time_seconds': 45 } print(f"Instance {instance_start['instance_id']} starting...") time.sleep(instance_start['startup_time_seconds']) print(f"Instance now running at {instance_start['public_ip']}") # Output: Instance now running at 54.123.45.67 # Django initialization after instance restart # Run migrations to ensure database is up to date print("Running Django migrations...") call_command('migrate', verbosity=0) # Check database connectivity try: user_count = User.objects.count() print(f"Database connected. User count: {user_count}") # Output: Database connected. User count: 0 except Exception as e: print(f"Database error: {e}") # Verify jobpost app models are accessible print("Jobpost app models status:") print(f" Skills: {Skills.objects.count()} records") # Output: Skills: 0 records print(f" Authors: {Author.objects.count()} records") # Output: Authors: 0 records print(f" Locations: {Location.objects.count()} records") # Output: Locations: 0 records print(f" JobPosts: {JobPost.objects.count()} records") # Output: JobPosts: 0 records # Development server ready print(f"\nDevelopment instance ready for Django testing") print(f"Access Django at: http://{instance_start['public_ip']}:8000")
Example 2: Start Multiple Backup Instances Simultaneously

Resume multiple stopped backup instances to create a failover cluster for the jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Start multiple backup instances at once
INSTANCE_IDS="i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4"

aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_IDS \
  --region us-east-1

echo "Starting instances: $INSTANCE_IDS"

# Wait for all instances to be running
for instance_id in $INSTANCE_IDS; do
  echo "Waiting for $instance_id to start..."
  aws ec2 wait instance-running \
    --instance-ids $instance_id \
    --region us-east-1
  echo "✓ $instance_id is running"
done

# Display startup summary
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_IDS \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,State.Name,PublicIpAddress,LaunchTime]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
{ "StartingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } } ] } Starting instances: i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 Waiting for i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 to start... ✓ i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 is running Waiting for i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 to start... ✓ i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 is running INSTANCEID | STATE | PUBLICIPADDRESS | LAUNCHTIME i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 | running | 54.234.123.45 | 2025-01-10T13:45:30Z i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 | running | 54.245.234.56 | 2025-01-09T09:22:15Z
# Backup cluster startup and data synchronization from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db import connections from django.db.models import Count import asyncio # Backup instances being started backup_instances = [ {'id': 'i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4', 'ip': '54.234.123.45', 'name': 'backup-1'}, {'id': 'i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4', 'ip': '54.245.234.56', 'name': 'backup-2'} ] print(f"Starting {len(backup_instances)} backup instances for jobpost app...") # Output: Starting 2 backup instances for jobpost app... # After startup, verify data replication for backup in backup_instances: print(f"\nVerifying {backup['name']} ({backup['ip']}):") # Check model counts to verify replication skills_count = Skills.objects.count() authors_count = Author.objects.count() jobs_count = JobPost.objects.count() print(f" Skills replicated: {skills_count}") # Output: Skills replicated: 0 print(f" Authors replicated: {authors_count}") # Output: Authors replicated: 0 print(f" Jobs replicated: {jobs_count}") # Output: Jobs replicated: 0 # Cluster status after startup cluster_status = { 'total_backup_nodes': len(backup_instances), 'expected_data_consistency': True, 'replication_lag_seconds': 0, 'models_synced': ['Skills', 'User', 'Author', 'Location', 'JobPost'] } print(f"\nBackup cluster status:") print(f" Total nodes: {cluster_status['total_backup_nodes']}") print(f" Data consistent: {cluster_status['expected_data_consistency']}") print(f" Models synced: {', '.join(cluster_status['models_synced'])}")
Example 3: Start Instances and Update DNS Records

Resume stopped production instances and automatically update Route 53 DNS to point to new IP addresses.

#!/bin/bash
# Start production instances and update DNS
PROD_INSTANCES="i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4"
HOSTED_ZONE_ID="Z1234567890ABC"
DOMAIN="jobpost-api.example.com"

# Start instances
aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids $PROD_INSTANCES \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for startup
for id in $PROD_INSTANCES; do
  aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids $id --region us-east-1
done

# Get new public IPs
PUBLIC_IPS=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $PROD_INSTANCES \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].PublicIpAddress' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

# Update DNS records
echo "Instances restarted. Public IPs: $PUBLIC_IPS"
echo "DNS records would be updated with new IPs"

# Verify instances are ready
aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --instance-ids $PROD_INSTANCES \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[*].[InstanceId,InstanceStatus.Status,SystemStatus.Status]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
{ "StartingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } } ] } Instances restarted. Public IPs: 54.234.56.78 54.145.67.89 DNS records would be updated with new IPs Instance Status Check: INSTANCEID | INSTANCESTATUS | SYSTEMSTATUS i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 | ok | ok i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | ok | ok
# Production startup with DNS and load balancer update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.core.cache import cache from django.db import connections import json # Production instances being restarted production_startup = { 'instances': [ {'id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'old_ip': '54.234.56.78', 'new_ip': '54.234.56.78'}, {'id': 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'old_ip': '54.145.67.89', 'new_ip': '54.145.67.89'} ], 'domain': 'jobpost-api.example.com', 'load_balancer': 'arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:xxx:loadbalancer/app/jobpost-lb/xxx' } print(f"Production startup: {len(production_startup['instances'])} instances") # Output: Production startup: 2 instances # Verify database connections on all instances for instance in production_startup['instances']: print(f"\nInstance {instance['id']} database check:") # Test connection try: total_records = (Skills.objects.count() + User.objects.count() + Author.objects.count() + Location.objects.count() + JobPost.objects.count()) print(f" Database OK - {total_records} total records") except Exception as e: print(f" Database error: {e}") # Clear cache after restart cache.clear() print("\nCache cleared after restart") # Verify jobpost app health health_metrics = { 'skills_available': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors_count': Author.objects.count(), 'job_posts': JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count(), 'unique_locations': Location.objects.values('id').distinct().count() } print(f"\nJobpost app health check:") for metric, value in health_metrics.items(): print(f" {metric}: {value}") # Output: (all values 0 initially)
Example 4: Start Instance with Dependency Check (Database and Cache)

Resume an application instance and verify all dependent services (database, cache, API endpoints) are ready before routing traffic.

#!/bin/bash
# Start instance with health verification
INSTANCE_ID="i-0987654321fedcba0"
MAX_RETRIES=30
RETRY_INTERVAL=5

# Start the instance
echo "Starting instance $INSTANCE_ID..."
aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for instance to be running
aws ec2 wait instance-running \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Get IP address
PUBLIC_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].PublicIpAddress' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "Instance $INSTANCE_ID is running at $PUBLIC_IP"

# Check Django app availability
echo "Checking Django app health..."
for ((i=1; i<=MAX_RETRIES; i++)); do
  HTTP_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://$PUBLIC_IP:8000/health || echo "000")
  
  if [ "$HTTP_CODE" = "200" ]; then
    echo "✓ Django app is healthy (HTTP $HTTP_CODE)"
    break
  else
    echo "  Attempt $i/$MAX_RETRIES - HTTP $HTTP_CODE (waiting...)"
    sleep $RETRY_INTERVAL
  fi
done

echo "Instance startup complete and verified"
Starting instance i-0987654321fedcba0... { "StartingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "CurrentState": { "Code": 0, "Name": "pending" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } }] } Instance i-0987654321fedcba0 is running at 54.123.45.67 Checking Django app health... Attempt 1/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 2/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 3/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 4/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 5/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 6/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 7/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 8/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 9/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) Attempt 10/30 - HTTP 000 (waiting...) ✓ Django app is healthy (HTTP 200) Instance startup complete and verified
# Instance startup with service dependency verification from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db import connection from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser import socket import time # Instance startup context startup_context = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'public_ip': '54.123.45.67', 'startup_time': 45, 'dependencies': ['PostgreSQL', 'Redis', 'Django app'] } print(f"Instance {startup_context['instance_id']} dependencies check:") # 1. Check database connection try: with connection.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("SELECT 1") print(f"✓ PostgreSQL database connected") db_ok = True except Exception as e: print(f"✗ PostgreSQL error: {e}") db_ok = False # 2. Verify jobpost models are accessible if db_ok: try: # Test all models skills = Skills.objects.count() authors = Author.objects.count() jobs = JobPost.objects.count() locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"✓ Jobpost models accessible") print(f" - Skills: {skills}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" - Authors: {authors}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" - Jobs: {jobs}") # Output: Jobs: 0 print(f" - Locations: {locations}") # Output: Locations: 0 except Exception as e: print(f"✗ Model access error: {e}") # 3. Test Django HTTP endpoint try: sock = socket.create_connection(('54.123.45.67', 8000), timeout=2) sock.close() print(f"✓ Django development server responding on port 8000") except Exception as e: print(f"✗ Django server error: {e}") # Final status print(f"\nInstance startup verification complete") print(f"Ready to accept job post creation requests")
Example 5: Start Instances with Monitoring and CloudWatch Alarms

Resume instances and enable detailed monitoring with CloudWatch alarms to track CPU, memory, and disk usage.

#!/bin/bash
# Start instances with monitoring enabled
INSTANCE_IDS="i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi"

# Start instances
aws ec2 start-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_IDS \
  --region us-east-1

# Enable detailed monitoring (1-minute intervals instead of 5-minute)
for instance_id in $INSTANCE_IDS; do
  aws ec2 monitor-instances \
    --instance-ids $instance_id \
    --region us-east-1
done

echo "✓ Detailed monitoring enabled"

# Create CloudWatch alarms for CPU
for instance_id in $INSTANCE_IDS; do
  aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
    --alarm-name "jobpost-high-cpu-$instance_id" \
    --alarm-description "Alert when CPU exceeds 80%" \
    --metric-name CPUUtilization \
    --namespace AWS/EC2 \
    --statistic Average \
    --period 300 \
    --threshold 80 \
    --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
    --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=$instance_id \
    --evaluation-periods 2 \
    --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:jobpost-alerts \
    --region us-east-1
done

echo "✓ CloudWatch alarms created"

# Wait for instances to be fully running
for instance_id in $INSTANCE_IDS; do
  aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids $instance_id --region us-east-1
done

# Display monitoring status
aws cloudwatch list-metrics \
  --namespace AWS/EC2 \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --query 'Metrics[?Dimensions[?Name==`InstanceId`]]' \
  --output json \
  --region us-east-1
✓ Detailed monitoring enabled { "MonitoringState": "enabled" } ✓ CloudWatch alarms created Alarm created: jobpost-high-cpu-i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 Alarm created: jobpost-high-cpu-i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi Monitoring Status Output: { "Metrics": [ { "Namespace": "AWS/EC2", "MetricName": "CPUUtilization", "Dimensions": [ { "Name": "InstanceId", "Value": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4" } ], "Statistics": ["Average", "Sum", "Minimum", "Maximum"], "Unit": "Percent" }, { "Namespace": "AWS/EC2", "MetricName": "CPUUtilization", "Dimensions": [ { "Name": "InstanceId", "Value": "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi" } ], "Statistics": ["Average", "Sum", "Minimum", "Maximum"], "Unit": "Percent" } ] }
# Monitoring initialization with jobpost metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q from django.utils import timezone import json # Monitoring configuration for restarted instances monitoring_config = { 'instances': ['i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi'], 'detailed_monitoring_enabled': True, 'metrics_interval': '1-minute', 'alarms_configured': { 'cpu_threshold': 80, 'memory_threshold': 85, 'disk_threshold': 90 }, 'notification_channel': 'SNS Topic: jobpost-alerts' } print(f"Monitoring enabled for {len(monitoring_config['instances'])} instances") print(f"Detailed metrics: {monitoring_config['detailed_monitoring_enabled']}") print(f"Interval: {monitoring_config['metrics_interval']}") # Application metrics to track alongside CloudWatch metrics app_metrics = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'total_users': User.objects.count(), 'active_authors': Author.objects.filter(user__is_active=True).count(), 'job_posts': { 'total': JobPost.objects.count(), 'published': JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count(), 'drafts': JobPost.objects.filter(status='draft').count() }, 'locations_coverage': Location.objects.values('country').distinct().count(), 'skills_library': Skills.objects.count() } print(f"\nApplication metrics snapshot:") print(f" Total users: {app_metrics['total_users']}") # Output: Total users: 0 print(f" Active authors: {app_metrics['active_authors']}") # Output: Active authors: 0 print(f" Published jobs: {app_metrics['job_posts']['published']}") # Output: Published jobs: 0 print(f" Draft jobs: {app_metrics['job_posts']['drafts']}") # Output: Draft jobs: 0 print(f" Countries: {app_metrics['locations_coverage']}") # Output: Countries: 0 print(f" Skills available: {app_metrics['skills_library']}") # Output: Skills available: 0 # Combined dashboard data dashboard_data = { 'cloudwatch_monitoring': monitoring_config, 'application_metrics': app_metrics, 'health_status': 'monitoring_enabled', 'next_check': 'Every 1 minute via CloudWatch' } print(f"\nMonitoring dashboard initialized: {json.dumps(dashboard_data, indent=2)}")

stop-instances

Stops one or more running EC2 instances. Preserves the instance for later restart while stopping charges for compute time (only storage charges apply). Data and configuration remain intact on the instance.

Syntax
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids {instance-id} \
  --force \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list, required): One or more instance IDs to stop. Multiple IDs can be provided separated by spaces
  • --force (boolean): Forces shutdown without graceful stop. Use only when graceful stop is not responding. Can cause data loss - use with caution
  • --region (string): AWS region where instances are located
  • --hibernate (boolean): Hibernates the instance instead of shutting down (requires EBS-backed instance with hibernation enabled)
Return Value
  • StoppingInstances (Array): List of instances being stopped
  • InstanceId (string): ID of the instance
  • CurrentState (Object): State transitioning to stopped
  • PreviousState (Object): State before stop (was running)
5 Detailed EC2 stop-instances Examples
Example 1: Stop Development Instance for Maintenance

Gracefully stop a development instance to perform system updates and maintenance on the jobpost application server.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop development instance gracefully
INSTANCE_ID="i-0987654321fedcba0"

echo "Stopping development instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Stop the instance (graceful shutdown)
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for instance to be stopped
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify it's stopped
FINAL_STATE=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].State.Name' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "Instance $INSTANCE_ID is now: $FINAL_STATE"
echo "Instance charges paused - only storage costs $0.10/month"
{ "StoppingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } } ] } Instance i-0987654321fedcba0 is now: stopped Instance charges paused - only storage costs $0.10/month
# Development instance graceful shutdown from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser from django.core.management import call_command import logging # Configure logging for shutdown logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) # Instance stop event stop_event = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'reason': 'Maintenance window', 'previous_state': 'running', 'target_state': 'stopped', 'estimated_cost_savings': 23.50 # Monthly t2.micro charges } print(f"Stopping {stop_event['instance_id']} for {stop_event['reason']}") # Pre-shutdown jobpost database state print("\nJobpost database state before shutdown:") print(f" Skills: {Skills.objects.count()}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" Authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" Job Posts: {JobPost.objects.count()}") # Output: Job Posts: 0 print(f" Locations: {Location.objects.count()}") # Output: Locations: 0 # Graceful application shutdown print("\nPerforming graceful shutdown...") print(" - Closing active connections") print(" - Saving session data") print(" - Flushing caches") print(" - Logging final metrics") # Log current metrics logger.info(f"Instance stopping: {stop_event}") # Final state after shutdown shutdown_summary = { 'instance_id': stop_event['instance_id'], 'final_state': 'stopped', 'data_preserved': True, 'estimated_monthly_savings': stop_event['estimated_cost_savings'], 'maintenance_tasks': [ 'System package updates', 'Security patches', 'Django dependency updates', 'Database optimization' ] } print(f"\nInstance stopped successfully") print(f" Data: Preserved on EBS volume") print(f" Cost: $0 compute (only $0.10/month storage)") print(f" Ready for: System updates and maintenance")
Example 2: Stop Multiple Backup Instances to Reduce Costs

Stop multiple backup instances during off-peak hours or when not needed to reduce AWS costs while keeping them available for quick restart.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop multiple backup instances for cost optimization
BACKUP_INSTANCES="i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4"

echo "Stopping backup instances: $BACKUP_INSTANCES"

# Stop all instances
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids $BACKUP_INSTANCES \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for all to be stopped
for instance_id in $BACKUP_INSTANCES; do
  echo "Waiting for $instance_id..."
  aws ec2 wait instance-stopped \
    --instance-ids $instance_id \
    --region us-east-1
  echo "✓ $instance_id stopped"
done

# Calculate cost savings
INSTANCE_COUNT=$(echo $BACKUP_INSTANCES | wc -w)
HOURLY_RATE=0.0376  # t3.small hourly cost
HOURS_PER_MONTH=730

SAVINGS=$(echo "scale=2; $INSTANCE_COUNT * $HOURLY_RATE * $HOURS_PER_MONTH" | bc)

echo ""
echo "Cost Analysis:"
echo "  Stopped instances: $INSTANCE_COUNT"
echo "  Hourly rate per instance: \$$HOURLY_RATE"
echo "  Monthly savings: \$$SAVINGS (compute only)"
echo "  Storage cost remaining: \$0.10/instance/month"
{ "StoppingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } } ] } Waiting for i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4... ✓ i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 stopped Waiting for i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4... ✓ i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 stopped Cost Analysis: Stopped instances: 2 Hourly rate per instance: $0.0376 Monthly savings: $54.95 (compute only) Storage cost remaining: $0.10/instance/month
# Backup instance shutdown for cost optimization from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db import connections from django.utils import timezone import json # Backup cluster shutdown backup_shutdown = { 'instances_stopping': 2, 'instance_ids': ['i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4', 'i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4'], 'reason': 'Off-peak hours cost optimization', 'estimated_monthly_savings': 54.95 } print(f"Shutting down {backup_shutdown['instances_stopping']} backup instances") print(f"Monthly savings: ${backup_shutdown['estimated_monthly_savings']}") # Backup cluster data snapshot before shutdown backup_snapshot = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'skills_count': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors_count': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs_count': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations_count': Location.objects.count(), 'total_records': (Skills.objects.count() + Author.objects.count() + JobPost.objects.count() + Location.objects.count()) } print(f"\nData snapshot before shutdown:") print(f" Skills: {backup_snapshot['skills_count']}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" Authors: {backup_snapshot['authors_count']}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" Jobs: {backup_snapshot['jobs_count']}") # Output: Jobs: 0 print(f" Locations: {backup_snapshot['locations_count']}") # Output: Locations: 0 print(f" Total records backed up: {backup_snapshot['total_records']}") # Output: Total records backed up: 0 # Cluster status cluster_status = { 'primary_instances': 'still running', 'backup_instances': 'being stopped', 'data_consistency': 'preserved on EBS', 'failover_capable': False, # Failover not available while backups are stopped 'restart_time_seconds': 45 } print(f"\nCluster status:") print(f" Primary: {cluster_status['primary_instances']}") print(f" Backup: {cluster_status['backup_instances']}") print(f" Restart time: {cluster_status['restart_time_seconds']}s when needed") # Cost breakdown cost_analysis = { 'monthly_compute_savings': backup_shutdown['estimated_monthly_savings'], 'monthly_storage_cost': 0.20, # 2 instances × $0.10 'net_monthly_savings': backup_shutdown['estimated_monthly_savings'] - 0.20, 'annual_savings': (backup_shutdown['estimated_monthly_savings'] - 0.20) * 12 } print(f"\nCost analysis:") print(f" Monthly compute savings: ${cost_analysis['monthly_compute_savings']}") print(f" Monthly storage cost: ${cost_analysis['monthly_storage_cost']}") print(f" Net monthly savings: ${cost_analysis['net_monthly_savings']:.2f}") print(f" Annual savings: ${cost_analysis['annual_savings']:.2f}")
Example 3: Stop Instance with Pre-Stop Data Export

Before stopping a production instance, export jobpost database snapshot and create backup for disaster recovery.

#!/bin/bash
# Export data before stopping instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
BACKUP_BUCKET="s3://anihpj-backups"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)

echo "Pre-stop backup: exporting jobpost data..."

# Create database dump (SSH into instance and run)
# aws ssm send-command \
#   --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
#   --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
#   --parameters 'commands=[
#     "pg_dump -U postgres jobpost_db > /tmp/jobpost-backup-$TIMESTAMP.sql",
#     "tar -czf /tmp/jobpost-backup-$TIMESTAMP.tar.gz -C /var/www anihpj/",
#     "aws s3 cp /tmp/jobpost-backup-$TIMESTAMP.sql $BACKUP_BUCKET/",
#     "aws s3 cp /tmp/jobpost-backup-$TIMESTAMP.tar.gz $BACKUP_BUCKET/"
#   ]' \
#   --region us-east-1

# For demonstration, show what would be backed up
echo "Backup items for instance $INSTANCE_ID:"
echo "  - PostgreSQL database: jobpost_db"
echo "  - Django application: /var/www/anihpj/"
echo "  - Static files: /var/www/anihpj/staticfiles/"
echo "  - Media files: /var/www/anihpj/media/"

# Create EBS snapshot of instance volume
aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id vol-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --description "Pre-stop backup of jobpost instance $INSTANCE_ID - $TIMESTAMP" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=jobpost-backup-$TIMESTAMP},{Key=Instance,Value=$INSTANCE_ID}]" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ EBS snapshot created"

# Now stop the instance
echo ""
echo "Stopping instance $INSTANCE_ID..."
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

aws ec2 wait instance-stopped \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance stopped - backups secured"
Backup items for instance i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8: - PostgreSQL database: jobpost_db - Django application: /var/www/anihpj/ - Static files: /var/www/anihpj/staticfiles/ - Media files: /var/www/anihpj/media/ { "Snapshot": { "SnapshotId": "snap-0123456789abcdef0", "VolumeSize": 100, "State": "pending", "StartTime": "2025-01-11T15:30:00.000Z", "Description": "Pre-stop backup of jobpost instance i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 - 20250111-153000" } } ✓ EBS snapshot created { "StoppingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }] } ✓ Instance stopped - backups secured
# Data export and backup before instance shutdown from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.core.management import call_command from django.core import serializers import json import os # Instance backup context backup_context = { 'instance_id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'backup_timestamp': '20250111-153000', 'backup_location': 's3://anihpj-backups', 'ebs_snapshot_id': 'snap-0123456789abcdef0' } print(f"Pre-stop backup for instance {backup_context['instance_id']}") # Export jobpost models to JSON for backup print("\nExporting jobpost models...") # Export Skills skills_data = serializers.serialize('json', Skills.objects.all()) print(f" Skills exported: {len(Skills.objects.all())} records") # Export Authors authors_data = serializers.serialize('json', Author.objects.all()) print(f" Authors exported: {len(Author.objects.all())} records") # Export Job Posts jobs_data = serializers.serialize('json', JobPost.objects.all()) print(f" JobPosts exported: {len(JobPost.objects.all())} records") # Export Locations locations_data = serializers.serialize('json', Location.objects.all()) print(f" Locations exported: {len(Location.objects.all())} records") # Database backup summary backup_summary = { 'backup_id': backup_context['backup_timestamp'], 'instance_id': backup_context['instance_id'], 'models_backed_up': { 'Skills': len(Skills.objects.all()), 'Authors': len(Author.objects.all()), 'JobPosts': len(JobPost.objects.all()), 'Locations': len(Location.objects.all()) }, 'ebs_snapshot': backup_context['ebs_snapshot_id'], 's3_location': f"{backup_context['backup_location']}/jobpost-backup-{backup_context['backup_timestamp']}" } print(f"\nBackup Summary:") print(f" Timestamp: {backup_summary['backup_id']}") print(f" EBS Snapshot: {backup_summary['ebs_snapshot']}") print(f" S3 Location: {backup_summary['s3_location']}") print(f" Total records: {sum(backup_summary['models_backed_up'].values())}") # Ready for shutdown print(f"\n✓ All backups completed") print(f"✓ Instance {backup_context['instance_id']} safe to stop")
Example 4: Stop Production Instance Gracefully with Load Balancer Deregistration

Remove a production instance from load balancer before stopping to ensure no traffic loss during maintenance window.

#!/bin/bash
# Graceful production instance stop with LB deregistration
INSTANCE_ID="i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4"
TARGET_GROUP_ARN="arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/jobpost-api/abc123def456"
DRAINING_TIMEOUT=300  # 5 minutes

echo "Gracefully stopping production instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Step 1: Deregister from load balancer..."

# Get current instance details
INSTANCE_PORT=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[0].GroupId' \
  --output text)

# Deregister from target group
aws elbv2 deregister-targets \
  --target-group-arn $TARGET_GROUP_ARN \
  --targets Id=$INSTANCE_ID,Port=8000 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "  ✓ Instance deregistered from load balancer"
echo "Step 2: Waiting for connection draining ($DRAINING_TIMEOUT seconds)..."

# Wait for existing connections to close
sleep $DRAINING_TIMEOUT

echo "  ✓ Connection draining complete"
echo "Step 3: Stopping instance..."

# Stop the instance
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify it's stopping
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "  ✓ Instance stopped successfully"
echo ""
echo "Maintenance complete. Instance ready for updates."
echo "To resume: aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID"
Gracefully stopping production instance: i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 Step 1: Deregister from load balancer... ✓ Instance deregistered from load balancer Step 2: Waiting for connection draining (300 seconds)... ✓ Connection draining complete Step 3: Stopping instance... { "StoppingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }] } ✓ Instance stopped successfully Maintenance complete. Instance ready for updates. To resume: aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4
# Production instance graceful shutdown with LB deregistration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q from django.utils import timezone import logging # Configure logging logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) # Production shutdown context production_stop = { 'instance_id': 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'load_balancer_target_group': 'jobpost-api', 'draining_timeout_seconds': 300, 'maintenance_reason': 'Security patches and updates' } print(f"Production shutdown sequence initiated") print(f"Instance: {production_stop['instance_id']}") # Step 1: Log current active sessions active_sessions = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'instance_id': production_stop['instance_id'], 'active_jobs': JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count(), 'active_authors': Author.objects.filter(user__is_active=True).count() } print(f"\nActive traffic snapshot:") print(f" Published jobs: {active_sessions['active_jobs']}") # Output: Published jobs: 0 print(f" Active authors: {active_sessions['active_authors']}") # Output: Active authors: 0 # Step 2: Log deregistration from load balancer lb_deregistration = { 'target_group': production_stop['load_balancer_target_group'], 'instance_id': production_stop['instance_id'], 'port': 8000, 'drain_timeout': production_stop['draining_timeout_seconds'], 'status': 'deregistered' } print(f"\nLoad Balancer deregistration:") print(f" Target Group: {lb_deregistration['target_group']}") print(f" Instance: {lb_deregistration['instance_id']}") print(f" Draining for: {lb_deregistration['drain_timeout']}s") # Step 3: Prepare for maintenance maintenance_tasks = [ 'Apply security patches', 'Update Django dependencies', 'Optimize database indexes', 'Review and clear logs', 'Test jobpost app endpoints' ] print(f"\nScheduled maintenance:") for i, task in enumerate(maintenance_tasks, 1): print(f" {i}. {task}") # Final state shutdown_complete = { 'instance_id': production_stop['instance_id'], 'state': 'stopped', 'data_status': 'preserved', 'ready_for_restart': True, 'estimated_maintenance_time': '30 minutes' } print(f"\n✓ Instance safe to stop") print(f"✓ No active connections") print(f"✓ Data preserved on EBS") print(f"✓ Ready for: {shutdown_complete['estimated_maintenance_time']} maintenance")
Example 5: Stop Instance with Scheduled Restart

Stop instance and schedule automatic restart using AWS Systems Manager for predictable downtime and patching windows.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop instance and schedule automatic restart
INSTANCE_ID="i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi"
RESTART_TIME="2025-01-12T02:00:00Z"  # 2 AM UTC tomorrow

echo "Scheduling maintenance window for instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Stop the instance
aws ec2 stop-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for it to stop
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance stopped"

# Schedule restart using SSM Automation (pseudo-code)
# In practice, you'd use EventBridge or Lambda for this
cat > restart-schedule.json << EOF
{
  "instance_id": "$INSTANCE_ID",
  "scheduled_restart_time": "$RESTART_TIME",
  "automation_document": "AWS-StartEC2Instance",
  "tags": {
    "Maintenance": "scheduled",
    "App": "jobpost"
  }
}
EOF

echo "✓ Maintenance window scheduled"
echo "  Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "  Restart time: $RESTART_TIME"
echo "  Status: Stopped and waiting for automatic restart"

# Display status
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].[InstanceId,State.Name,LaunchTime]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
Scheduling maintenance window for instance: i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi { "StoppingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi", "CurrentState": { "Code": 64, "Name": "stopping" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }] } ✓ Instance stopped ✓ Maintenance window scheduled Instance: i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi Restart time: 2025-01-12T02:00:00Z Status: Stopped and waiting for automatic restart INSTANCEID | STATE | LAUNCHTIME i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi | stopped | 2025-01-11T14:35:45Z
# Scheduled maintenance with automatic restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import json # Scheduled maintenance context scheduled_maintenance = { 'instance_id': 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi', 'current_state': 'stopped', 'scheduled_restart': '2025-01-12T02:00:00Z', 'maintenance_duration_hours': 4, 'automation_document': 'AWS-StartEC2Instance' } print(f"Scheduled Maintenance Plan") print(f"Instance: {scheduled_maintenance['instance_id']}") print(f"Restart Time: {scheduled_maintenance['scheduled_restart']}") # Database state before maintenance current_state = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'skills_count': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors_count': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs_count': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations_count': Location.objects.count() } print(f"\nDatabase state before maintenance:") print(f" Skills: {current_state['skills_count']}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" Authors: {current_state['authors_count']}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" Jobs: {current_state['jobs_count']}") # Output: Jobs: 0 print(f" Locations: {current_state['locations_count']}") # Output: Locations: 0 # Scheduled tasks during maintenance window maintenance_plan = { 'maintenance_window': f"{scheduled_maintenance['maintenance_duration_hours']} hours", 'scheduled_tasks': [ 'System package updates', 'Django security patches', 'Database optimization', 'Log rotation and cleanup', 'Disk space verification' ], 'post_maintenance_checks': [ 'Verify instance health', 'Check jobpost app startup', 'Validate database connectivity', 'Test all models accessible', 'Monitor CloudWatch metrics' ] } print(f"\nMaintenance Tasks ({maintenance_plan['maintenance_window']}):") for task in maintenance_plan['scheduled_tasks']: print(f" □ {task}") print(f"\nPost-Maintenance Checks:") for check in maintenance_plan['post_maintenance_checks']: print(f" ☐ {check}") # Automatic restart verification print(f"\n✓ Instance stopped and ready") print(f"✓ Automatic restart scheduled") print(f"✓ No manual intervention required") print(f"✓ JobPost app will resume automatically")

terminate-instances

Terminates (permanently deletes) one or more EC2 instances. This is irreversible - the instance and its data will be destroyed. Use with extreme caution, especially for production instances. Attached EBS volumes marked for deletion will be removed.

Syntax
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids {instance-id} \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list, required): One or more instance IDs to terminate. Cannot be undone. Multiple IDs separated by spaces
  • --region (string): AWS region where instances are located
  • --dry-run (boolean): Checks whether you have the required permissions without actually terminating
Return Value
  • TerminatingInstances (Array): List of instances being terminated
  • InstanceId (string): ID of the instance
  • CurrentState (Object): State transitioning to terminated
  • PreviousState (Object): State before termination
5 Detailed EC2 terminate-instances Examples
Example 1: Terminate Development Instance After Testing Complete

Remove a temporary development instance after testing cycle is complete to reduce unnecessary costs. Always backup data first!

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate development instance after testing
INSTANCE_ID="i-0987654321fedcba0"

echo "⚠️  WARNING: Terminating instance will PERMANENTLY DELETE all data!"
echo "Instance ID: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Safety check - require confirmation
read -p "Are you sure you want to terminate? Type 'yes' to confirm: " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" != "yes" ]; then
  echo "Termination cancelled."
  exit 0
fi

echo "Creating final snapshot before termination..."

# Get the volume ID
VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].BlockDeviceMappings[0].Ebs.VolumeId' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

# Create snapshot as final backup
aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
