  --description "Final backup before termination of $INSTANCE_ID" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Final snapshot created: $VOLUME_ID"

# Terminate the instance
echo ""
echo "Terminating instance $INSTANCE_ID..."
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance termination initiated"
echo "  State: shutting-down → terminated"
echo "  Completion time: ~30 seconds"
⚠️ WARNING: Terminating instance will PERMANENTLY DELETE all data! Instance ID: i-0987654321fedcba0 Are you sure you want to terminate? Type 'yes' to confirm: yes Creating final snapshot before termination... { "Snapshot": { "SnapshotId": "snap-final0987654321", "VolumeId": "vol-0123456789abcdef0", "State": "pending", "StartTime": "2025-01-11T16:00:00.000Z", "Description": "Final backup before termination of i-0987654321fedcba0" } } ✓ Final snapshot created: vol-0123456789abcdef0 Terminating instance i-0987654321fedcba0... { "TerminatingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0987654321fedcba0", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } }] } ✓ Instance termination initiated State: shutting-down → terminated Completion time: ~30 seconds
# Development instance final shutdown and cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser import json # Final state before termination final_state = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'termination_reason': 'Testing cycle complete', 'snapshot_created': 'snap-final0987654321', 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat() } print(f"Final database state for {final_state['instance_id']}:") # Last backup of all data final_data = { 'skills': list(Skills.objects.all().values()), 'authors': list(Author.objects.all().values()), 'jobs': list(JobPost.objects.all().values()), 'locations': list(Location.objects.all().values()), 'users': list(User.objects.all().values()) } print(f" Skills: {len(final_data['skills'])} records") # Output: Skills: 0 records print(f" Authors: {len(final_data['authors'])} records") # Output: Authors: 0 records print(f" Jobs: {len(final_data['jobs'])} records") # Output: Jobs: 0 records print(f" Locations: {len(final_data['locations'])} records") # Output: Locations: 0 records print(f" Total records archived: {sum(len(v) for v in final_data.values())}") print(f"\n✓ Instance {final_state['instance_id']} permanently terminated") print(f"✓ Data preserved in snapshot: {final_state['snapshot_created']}") print(f"✓ Compute charges stopped immediately")
Example 2: Terminate Multiple Failed Instances After Retry Attempts

Remove multiple instances that failed health checks and cleanup procedures to avoid unnecessary charges and resource waste.

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate failed instances
FAILED_INSTANCES="i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4"
FAILURE_REASON="Health check failures - beyond recovery"

echo "Cleaning up failed instances..."
echo "Reason: $FAILURE_REASON"
echo ""

# Check each instance before termination
for instance_id in $FAILED_INSTANCES; do
  echo "Instance $instance_id details:"
  
  # Get status
  STATUS=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
    --instance-ids $instance_id \
    --query 'InstanceStatuses[0].InstanceStatus.Status' \
    --output text \
    --region us-east-1 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
  
  echo "  Status: $STATUS"
  echo "  Action: Will be terminated"
done

echo ""
echo "Proceeding with termination of $FAILED_INSTANCES..."

# Terminate all failed instances
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids $FAILED_INSTANCES \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Termination initiated for failed instances"

# Calculate cost savings
INSTANCE_COUNT=$(echo $FAILED_INSTANCES | wc -w)
HOURLY_RATE=0.0376  # t3.small
DAILY_SAVINGS=$(echo "scale=2; $INSTANCE_COUNT * $HOURLY_RATE * 24" | bc)

echo ""
echo "Cost savings from termination:"
echo "  Failed instances removed: $INSTANCE_COUNT"
echo "  Daily savings: \$$DAILY_SAVINGS"
echo "  Estimated monthly savings: \$$(echo "scale=2; $DAILY_SAVINGS * 30" | bc)"
Cleaning up failed instances... Reason: Health check failures - beyond recovery Instance i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 details: Status: impaired Action: Will be terminated Instance i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4 details: Status: impaired Action: Will be terminated Proceeding with termination of i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4 i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4... { "TerminatingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 80, "Name": "stopped" } } ] } ✓ Termination initiated for failed instances Cost savings from termination: Failed instances removed: 2 Daily savings: $1.80 Estimated monthly savings: $54.00
# Failed instances cleanup and recovery from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db import connections, connection from django.core.management import call_command # Failed instances being terminated failed_instances = [ {'id': 'i-0def1abc2ghi3jk4', 'status': 'impaired'}, {'id': 'i-0ghi1def2abc3jk4', 'status': 'impaired'} ] print(f"Terminating {len(failed_instances)} failed instances") # Verify all data is on healthy primary instance print(f"\nVerifying data on primary instance...") try: primary_data_count = (Skills.objects.count() + Author.objects.count() + JobPost.objects.count() + Location.objects.count()) print(f"✓ Primary has {primary_data_count} records") # Output: Primary has 0 records except Exception as e: print(f"✗ Primary access error: {e}") # Cleanup impact analysis cleanup_summary = { 'instances_removed': len(failed_instances), 'data_loss': 'None - replicated on primary', 'monthly_savings': 54.00, 'failover_impact': 'Backup capacity removed' } print(f"\nCleanup Summary:") print(f" Instances removed: {cleanup_summary['instances_removed']}") print(f" Data safety: {cleanup_summary['data_loss']}") print(f" Monthly savings: ${cleanup_summary['monthly_savings']}") print(f"\n✓ Failed instances terminated") print(f"✓ Primary instance still healthy")
Example 3: Terminate Auto-Scaling Group Instances After Demand Decrease

Remove instances created by auto-scaling when demand returns to normal levels, cleaning up temporary capacity.

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate temporary ASG instances
# This is typically done by terminating the ASG, but example shows manual cleanup
TEMPORARY_INSTANCES="i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw"
SCALING_REASON="Peak demand passed - scaling down to baseline"

echo "ASG Scaling-Down: Removing temporary instances"
echo "Reason: $SCALING_REASON"
echo ""

# Get metrics before termination
echo "Current cluster capacity:"
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $TEMPORARY_INSTANCES \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType,State.Name]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1

# Terminate the temporary instances
echo ""
echo "Terminating temporary capacity..."
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids $TEMPORARY_INSTANCES \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Temporary instances queued for termination"

# Show remaining cluster size
echo ""
echo "Remaining production instances:"
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=production" \
            "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].[InstanceId,InstanceType]' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
ASG Scaling-Down: Removing temporary instances Reason: Peak demand passed - scaling down to baseline Current cluster capacity: INSTANCEID | INSTANCETYPE | STATE i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | t3.small | running i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi | t3.small | running i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw | t3.small | running Terminating temporary capacity... { "TerminatingInstances": [ { "InstanceId": "i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }, { "InstanceId": "i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } } ] } ✓ Temporary instances queued for termination Remaining production instances: INSTANCEID | INSTANCETYPE i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 | t3.medium (primary) i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 | t3.small (still running - may include new instance)
# Auto-scaling down and instance termination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count # ASG scaling down context scaling_context = { 'temporary_instances': 3, 'reason': 'Peak demand passed', 'remaining_capacity': 2, # 1 primary + 1 HA backup 'expected_cost_savings': 82.50 # 3 instances × $0.0376/hour × 730 hours } print(f"Auto-scaling down: Removing {scaling_context['temporary_instances']} temporary instances") print(f"Reason: {scaling_context['reason']}") # Before scaling down - total request capacity requests_per_instance = { 't3.medium': 1000, # Primary can handle this 't3.small': 500 # Each small can handle this } before_scaling = { 'total_capacity': 1000 + (500 * 3), # 1 medium + 3 small = 2500 req/sec 'instances': 4 } after_scaling = { 'total_capacity': 1000 + 500, # 1 medium + 1 small = 1500 req/sec 'instances': 2 } print(f"\nCapacity before scaling: {before_scaling['total_capacity']} req/sec ({before_scaling['instances']} instances)") print(f"Capacity after scaling: {after_scaling['total_capacity']} req/sec ({after_scaling['instances']} instances)") # Job post distribution impact jobs_by_location = JobPost.objects.values('location__city').annotate(count=Count('id')) print(f"\nJobpost distribution check:") print(f" Unique locations: {Location.objects.count()}") # Output: Unique locations: 0 print(f" Total jobs: {JobPost.objects.count()}") # Output: Total jobs: 0 print(f" Jobs in cache: Rebalancing across remaining instances") # Cost calculation monthly_savings = scaling_context['expected_cost_savings'] print(f"\nCost optimization:") print(f" Instances removed: {scaling_context['temporary_instances']}") print(f" Monthly savings: ${monthly_savings:.2f}") print(f" Remaining capacity: Sufficient for baseline load")
Example 4: Terminate Instance with Pre-Termination Health Report

Generate comprehensive health and performance report before terminating instance for post-mortem analysis.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate health report before termination
INSTANCE_ID="i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REPORT_BUCKET="s3://anihpj-reports"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)

echo "Generating pre-termination health report for $INSTANCE_ID..."

# Get comprehensive instance details
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0]' \
  --output json > report-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json

# Get CloudWatch metrics summary (last 7 days)
echo "Collecting CloudWatch metrics..."
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/EC2 \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=$INSTANCE_ID \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --period 3600 \
  --statistics Average Maximum \
  --output json > metrics-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json

# Upload reports
aws s3 cp report-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json $REPORT_BUCKET/pre-termination-reports/
aws s3 cp metrics-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json $REPORT_BUCKET/pre-termination-reports/

echo "✓ Health report generated: report-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json"
echo "✓ Metrics saved: metrics-$INSTANCE_ID-$TIMESTAMP.json"
echo "✓ Reports uploaded to S3"

echo ""
echo "Final metrics summary:"
echo "  CPU Utilization (last 7 days): Check CloudWatch"
echo "  Network In/Out: Check CloudWatch"
echo "  Disk I/O: Check CloudWatch"

# Now safe to terminate
echo ""
echo "Proceeding with termination..."
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance $INSTANCE_ID terminated"
echo "✓ Health reports available in S3 for post-mortem analysis"
Generating pre-termination health report for i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8... ✓ Health report generated: report-i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8-20250111-160000.json ✓ Metrics saved: metrics-i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8-20250111-160000.json ✓ Reports uploaded to S3 Final metrics summary: CPU Utilization (last 7 days): 35% average, 87% peak Network In/Out: 2.5GB in, 1.8GB out Disk I/O: Normal Proceeding with termination... { "TerminatingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }] } ✓ Instance i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 terminated ✓ Health reports available in S3 for post-mortem analysis
# Pre-termination health analysis and reporting from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q, Avg from django.utils import timezone import json import statistics # Instance health report context health_report = { 'instance_id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'uptime_days': 45, 'termination_reason': 'Planned instance retirement', 'report_timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat() } print(f"Pre-Termination Health Report") print(f"Instance: {health_report['instance_id']}") print(f"Uptime: {health_report['uptime_days']} days") # Application performance metrics app_metrics = { 'total_jobs_processed': JobPost.objects.count(), 'unique_authors': Author.objects.values('id').distinct().count(), 'database_records': (Skills.objects.count() + Author.objects.count() + JobPost.objects.count() + Location.objects.count()), 'active_users': User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() } print(f"\nApplication Metrics:") print(f" Jobs processed: {app_metrics['total_jobs_processed']}") # Output: Jobs processed: 0 print(f" Unique authors: {app_metrics['unique_authors']}") # Output: Unique authors: 0 print(f" Database records: {app_metrics['database_records']}") # Output: Database records: 0 print(f" Active users: {app_metrics['active_users']}") # Output: Active users: 0 # System metrics summary system_metrics = { 'cpu_average': 35, 'cpu_peak': 87, 'memory_average': 42, 'disk_utilization': 58, 'network_in_gb': 2.5, 'network_out_gb': 1.8 } print(f"\nSystem Performance:") for metric, value in system_metrics.items(): if 'gb' in metric.lower(): print(f" {metric}: {value} GB") else: print(f" {metric}: {value}%") # Reliability metrics reliability = { 'health_check_failures': 0, 'restarts': 0, 'service_uptime': 99.98, 'last_deployment': '2024-12-01T00:00:00Z' } print(f"\nReliability:") print(f" Health check failures: {reliability['health_check_failures']}") print(f" Unplanned restarts: {reliability['restarts']}") print(f" Service uptime: {reliability['service_uptime']}%") # Final summary print(f"\n✓ Instance ready for termination") print(f"✓ All data backed up") print(f"✓ Health metrics archived for analysis")
Example 5: Terminate Instance with Rollback Safety - Snapshot Retention

Safely terminate instance while preserving EBS snapshots for potential rollback if issues are discovered post-termination.

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate with snapshot retention for safety
INSTANCE_ID="i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw"
RETENTION_DAYS=30

echo "Safe termination with snapshot retention"
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Snapshot retention: $RETENTION_DAYS days"

# Get all volumes attached to this instance
VOLUME_IDS=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].BlockDeviceMappings[*].Ebs.VolumeId' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo ""
echo "Creating final snapshots for rollback safety..."

# Create snapshots of all volumes
for volume_id in $VOLUME_IDS; do
  SNAPSHOT=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
    --volume-id $volume_id \
    --description "Rollback snapshot for terminated instance $INSTANCE_ID - Retain $RETENTION_DAYS days" \
    --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Retention,Value=$RETENTION_DAYS-days},{Key=OriginalInstance,Value=$INSTANCE_ID},{Key=RollbackSafe,Value=true}]" \
    --region us-east-1 \
    --query 'SnapshotId' \
    --output text)
  
  echo "  ✓ Snapshot created: $SNAPSHOT (Volume: $volume_id)"
done

echo ""
echo "Terminating instance $INSTANCE_ID..."

# Terminate the instance but keep snapshots
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance terminated"
echo ""
echo "Safety measures in place:"
echo "  - EBS snapshots retained for $RETENTION_DAYS days"
echo "  - Snapshots can be used to recover data or create new instances"
echo "  - Snapshots will auto-expire after $RETENTION_DAYS days (cost-saving)"
echo "  - Tags applied: RollbackSafe=true for easy identification"
Safe termination with snapshot retention Instance: i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw Snapshot retention: 30 days Creating final snapshots for rollback safety... ✓ Snapshot created: snap-rollback123456 (Volume: vol-0123456789abcdef0) ✓ Snapshot created: snap-rollback789012 (Volume: vol-0987654321fedcba0) Terminating instance i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw... { "TerminatingInstances": [{ "InstanceId": "i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw", "CurrentState": { "Code": 32, "Name": "shutting-down" }, "PreviousState": { "Code": 16, "Name": "running" } }] } ✓ Instance terminated Safety measures in place: - EBS snapshots retained for 30 days - Snapshots can be used to recover data or create new instances - Snapshots will auto-expire after 30 days (cost-saving) - Tags applied: RollbackSafe=true for easy identification
# Safe termination with rollback capability from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import json # Safe termination context safe_termination = { 'instance_id': 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw', 'snapshots_created': 2, 'retention_days': 30, 'rollback_possible': True } print(f"Safe Termination Process") print(f"Instance: {safe_termination['instance_id']}") print(f"Snapshots for rollback: {safe_termination['snapshots_created']}") # Final data export before termination final_export = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'skills': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations': Location.objects.count() } print(f"\nFinal data state:") print(f" Skills: {final_export['skills']}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" Authors: {final_export['authors']}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" Jobs: {final_export['jobs']}") # Output: Jobs: 0 print(f" Locations: {final_export['locations']}") # Output: Locations: 0 # Rollback information rollback_info = { 'rollback_possible_until': (timezone.now() + timedelta(days=safe_termination['retention_days'])).isoformat(), 'snapshots': [ 'snap-rollback123456', 'snap-rollback789012' ], 'recovery_time_hours': 0.5 # 30 minutes to restore from snapshot } print(f"\nRollback Safety:") print(f" Rollback possible until: {rollback_info['rollback_possible_until']}") print(f" Snapshots retained: {len(rollback_info['snapshots'])}") print(f" Recovery time if needed: {rollback_info['recovery_time_hours']} hours") # Cost impact cost_impact = { 'compute_savings_monthly': 27.40, # t3.small 'snapshot_cost_monthly': 0.05, # ~$0.05/GB stored 'net_savings': 27.35 } print(f"\nCost Impact:") print(f" Monthly compute savings: ${cost_impact['compute_savings_monthly']:.2f}") print(f" Snapshot storage cost: ${cost_impact['snapshot_cost_monthly']:.2f}/month") print(f" Net monthly savings: ${cost_impact['net_savings']:.2f}") print(f"\n✓ Instance {safe_termination['instance_id']} terminated safely") print(f"✓ Rollback possible for {safe_termination['retention_days']} days")

reboot-instances

Reboots one or more EC2 instances. The instance reboots without stopping - no data loss but brief downtime (1-2 minutes). Useful for applying system patches, clearing kernel issues, or refreshing the OS without terminating the instance.

Syntax
aws ec2 reboot-instances \
  --instance-ids {instance-id} \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list, required): One or more instance IDs to reboot. Instances must be in running state
  • --region (string): AWS region where instances are located
  • --dry-run (boolean): Checks permissions without actually rebooting
Return Value
  • Return (boolean): True if reboot request accepted
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP response metadata and request ID
5 Detailed EC2 reboot-instances Examples
Example 1: Reboot Development Instance for Testing Fresh OS State

Reboot development instance to verify application behavior on clean OS boot and test initialization scripts.

#!/bin/bash
# Reboot dev instance for fresh OS state testing
INSTANCE_ID="i-0987654321fedcba0"
APP_USER="ubuntu"

echo "Rebooting dev instance for fresh state testing..."
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Verify instance is running
STATUS=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].State.Name' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

if [ "$STATUS" != "running" ]; then
  echo "✗ Instance is not running (status: $STATUS)"
  exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Instance is running - proceeding with reboot"
echo ""

# Initiate reboot
aws ec2 reboot-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reboot initiated for $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "  Reboot time: 2-3 minutes"
echo "  Data preserved: YES"

# Wait for instance to come back online
echo ""
echo "Waiting for instance to restart and reach running status..."

aws ec2 wait instance-running \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance is running again after reboot"

# Verify system is responsive
sleep 30  # Wait for services to start
IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].PrivateIpAddress' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo ""
echo "Post-reboot verification:"
echo "  Instance IP: $IP"
echo "  Boot logs available via EC2 Instance Connect or SSM"
echo "  Application initialization: Check systemd journal"
Rebooting dev instance for fresh OS state testing... Instance: i-0987654321fedcba0 ✓ Instance is running - proceeding with reboot ✓ Reboot initiated for i-0987654321fedcba0 Reboot time: 2-3 minutes Data preserved: YES Waiting for instance to restart and reach running status... (System automatically waits for running state) ✓ Instance is running again after reboot Post-reboot verification: Instance IP: 10.0.1.150 Boot logs available via EC2 Instance Connect or SSM Application initialization: Check systemd journal
# Development instance reboot for fresh state verification from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.db import connections import time reboot_context = { 'instance_id': 'i-0987654321fedcba0', 'purpose': 'Fresh OS state verification', 'downtime_minutes': 3, 'reason': 'Test initialization scripts and systemd services' } print(f"Instance Reboot - Fresh State Testing") print(f"Instance: {reboot_context['instance_id']}") print(f"Purpose: {reboot_context['purpose']}") # Record pre-reboot application state pre_reboot_state = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'skills_count': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors_count': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs_count': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations_count': Location.objects.count() } print(f"\nPre-reboot application state:") print(f" Skills: {pre_reboot_state['skills_count']}") # Output: Skills: 0 print(f" Authors: {pre_reboot_state['authors_count']}") # Output: Authors: 0 print(f" Jobs: {pre_reboot_state['jobs_count']}") # Output: Jobs: 0 print(f" Locations: {pre_reboot_state['locations_count']}") # Output: Locations: 0 # Simulate reboot process print(f"\nReboot in progress...") print(f" Downtime: ~{reboot_context['downtime_minutes']} minutes") print(f" Services stopping...") print(f" System rebooting...") print(f" Services starting...") # Post-reboot state (same data, fresh OS) post_reboot_state = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'skills_count': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors_count': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs_count': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations_count': Location.objects.count(), 'boot_completed': True } print(f"\nPost-reboot application state:") print(f" Skills: {post_reboot_state['skills_count']}") # Output: Skills: 0 (same as before) print(f" Authors: {post_reboot_state['authors_count']}") # Output: Authors: 0 (same as before) print(f" Jobs: {post_reboot_state['jobs_count']}") # Output: Jobs: 0 (same as before) print(f" Locations: {post_reboot_state['locations_count']}") # Output: Locations: 0 (same as before) print(f"\n✓ Fresh OS state achieved") print(f"✓ Data integrity verified - no changes")
Example 2: Reboot Production Instance During Maintenance Window

Safely reboot production instance within planned maintenance window with health checks and load balancer management.

#!/bin/bash
# Reboot production instance during maintenance window
INSTANCE_ID="i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
ALB_TARGET_GROUP="arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/django-app/abc123"
MAINTENANCE_WINDOW="2025-01-15T02:00-06:00"  # Off-peak hours

echo "Production Instance Reboot - Maintenance Window"
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Maintenance window: $MAINTENANCE_WINDOW"
echo "Expected downtime: 2-3 minutes"

# Pre-reboot health check
echo ""
echo "Pre-reboot checks:"
aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[0].[SystemStatus.Status, InstanceStatus.Status]' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1

# Deregister from load balancer before reboot
echo ""
echo "Deregistering from ALB target group..."
aws elbv2 deregister-targets \
  --target-group-arn $ALB_TARGET_GROUP \
  --targets Id=$INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Deregistered from load balancer"

# Wait for connection draining
echo "Waiting for connection draining (30 seconds)..."
sleep 30

# Perform the reboot
echo ""
echo "Initiating reboot..."
aws ec2 reboot-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reboot initiated"
echo "  System rebooting... (2-3 minutes)"

# Wait for instance to fully restart
aws ec2 wait instance-status-ok \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Instance fully started and healthy"

# Re-register with load balancer
echo ""
echo "Re-registering with ALB target group..."
aws elbv2 register-targets \
  --target-group-arn $ALB_TARGET_GROUP \
  --targets Id=$INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Re-registered with load balancer"
echo ""
echo "Instance is now serving traffic again"
Production Instance Reboot - Maintenance Window Instance: i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 Maintenance window: 2025-01-15T02:00-06:00 Expected downtime: 2-3 minutes Pre-reboot checks: ok ok ✓ Deregistered from load balancer Waiting for connection draining (30 seconds)... Initiating reboot... ✓ Reboot initiated System rebooting... (2-3 minutes) ✓ Instance fully started and healthy Re-registering with ALB target group... ✓ Re-registered with load balancer Instance is now serving traffic again
# Production instance reboot with load balancer coordination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.contrib.auth.models import User as DjangoUser # Production reboot context prod_reboot = { 'instance_id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'environment': 'production', 'maintenance_window': '2025-01-15T02:00-06:00', 'expected_downtime_minutes': 3, 'alb_deregistered': True } print(f"Production Instance Reboot") print(f"Instance: {prod_reboot['instance_id']}") print(f"Maintenance window: {prod_reboot['maintenance_window']}") # Health status before reboot pre_reboot_health = { 'system_status': 'ok', 'instance_status': 'ok', 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat() } print(f"\nPre-reboot health:") print(f" System status: {pre_reboot_health['system_status']}") print(f" Instance status: {pre_reboot_health['instance_status']}") # Connection draining - wait for active requests to complete active_connections = { 'active_requests': 24, 'drain_timeout': 30, 'connections_completed': 22, 'new_requests_blocked': True } print(f"\nConnection draining:") print(f" Active requests being completed: {active_connections['active_requests']}") print(f" Drain timeout: {active_connections['drain_timeout']}s") print(f" Requests completed: {active_connections['connections_completed']}") # Reboot impact analysis reboot_impact = { 'jobs_not_processed': active_connections['active_requests'] - active_connections['connections_completed'], 'users_affected': DjangoUser.objects.filter(is_active=True).count(), 'authors_offline': Author.objects.count(), 'impact_duration_minutes': prod_reboot['expected_downtime_minutes'] } print(f"\nReboot impact:") print(f" Users potentially affected: {reboot_impact['users_affected']}") # Output: Users potentially affected: 0 print(f" Authors unable to post jobs: {reboot_impact['authors_offline']}") # Output: Authors unable to post jobs: 0 print(f" Expected recovery time: {reboot_impact['impact_duration_minutes']} minutes") # Post-reboot verification post_reboot_status = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'system_healthy': True, 'alb_registered': True, 'accepting_requests': True, 'jobs_count': JobPost.objects.count() } print(f"\nPost-reboot status:") print(f" System healthy: {post_reboot_status['system_healthy']}") print(f" ALB registered: {post_reboot_status['alb_registered']}") print(f" Accepting requests: {post_reboot_status['accepting_requests']}") print(f" Jobs in database: {post_reboot_status['jobs_count']}") print(f"\n✓ Production instance rebooted successfully") print(f"✓ Zero-downtime maintenance achieved (LB took traffic)")
Example 3: Reboot Multiple Instances in Sequence for Patching

Apply OS patches to multiple instances by rebooting them sequentially to maintain service availability (rolling update).

#!/bin/bash
# Sequential reboot for OS patching
INSTANCES=("i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4" "i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi" "i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw")
TARGET_GROUP="arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/django-app/abc123"

echo "Rolling update: Patch and reboot instances sequentially"
echo "Total instances: ${#INSTANCES[@]}"
echo ""

for ((i=0; i<${#INSTANCES[@]}; i++)); do
  INSTANCE_ID=${INSTANCES[$i]}
  INSTANCE_NUM=$((i + 1))
  
  echo "========================================="
  echo "Instance $INSTANCE_NUM/${#INSTANCES[@]}: $INSTANCE_ID"
  echo "========================================="
  
  # Deregister from load balancer
  echo "Deregistering from ALB..."
  aws elbv2 deregister-targets \
    --target-group-arn $TARGET_GROUP \
    --targets Id=$INSTANCE_ID \
    --region us-east-1
  
  sleep 10  # Connection draining
  
  # Reboot
  echo "Rebooting instance..."
  aws ec2 reboot-instances \
    --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
    --region us-east-1
  
  # Wait for healthy status
  echo "Waiting for instance to be healthy..."
  aws ec2 wait instance-status-ok \
    --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
    --region us-east-1
  
  # Re-register with load balancer
  echo "Re-registering with ALB..."
  aws elbv2 register-targets \
    --target-group-arn $TARGET_GROUP \
    --targets Id=$INSTANCE_ID \
    --region us-east-1
  
  sleep 10  # Wait for ALB to mark healthy
  
  echo "✓ Instance $INSTANCE_NUM ready"
  echo ""
done

echo "========================================="
echo "✓ All instances patched and rebooted"
echo "✓ Service maintained throughout update"
echo "========================================="
Rolling update: Patch and reboot instances sequentially Total instances: 3 ========================================= Instance 1/3: i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4 ========================================= Deregistering from ALB... {} Rebooting instance... {} Waiting for instance to be healthy... (auto-waiter polls status) ✓ Instance 1 ready ========================================= Instance 2/3: i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi ========================================= Deregistering from ALB... {} Rebooting instance... {} Waiting for instance to be healthy... ✓ Instance 2 ready ========================================= Instance 3/3: i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw ========================================= Deregistering from ALB... {} Rebooting instance... {} Waiting for instance to be healthy... ✓ Instance 3 ready ========================================= ✓ All instances patched and rebooted ✓ Service maintained throughout update =========================================
# Rolling update with sequential reboots for patching from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import time # Rolling update context rolling_update = { 'total_instances': 3, 'instances': [ 'i-0abc1def2ghi3jk4', 'i-0xyz9abc8def7ghi', 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw' ], 'patch_type': 'OS security updates', 'strategy': 'Rolling update - one at a time' } print(f"Rolling Update - OS Patching") print(f"Strategy: {rolling_update['strategy']}") print(f"Instances to patch: {rolling_update['total_instances']}") # Track reboot progress reboot_log = [] for idx, instance_id in enumerate(rolling_update['instances'], 1): log_entry = { 'instance_number': idx, 'instance_id': instance_id, 'status': 'deregistered_from_alb', 'reboot_initiated': True, 'recovery_status': 'healthy', 'reregistered_alb': True, 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat() } reboot_log.append(log_entry) print(f"\nInstance {idx}/3: {instance_id}") print(f" Deregistered from ALB: ✓") print(f" Reboot initiated: ✓") print(f" Recovery status: {log_entry['recovery_status']}") print(f" Re-registered with ALB: ✓") # Application availability during update availability = { 'instances_healthy': rolling_update['total_instances'], 'jobs_accessible': JobPost.objects.count(), 'users_served': User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count(), 'service_continuity': 'Maintained (ALB routed to healthy instances)' } print(f"\nApplication availability during update:") print(f" Healthy instances serving traffic: {availability['instances_healthy']}") print(f" Jobs accessible: {availability['jobs_accessible']}") print(f" Active users served: {availability['users_served']}") print(f" Service continuity: {availability['service_continuity']}") # Update summary summary = { 'total_reboot_time_minutes': 15, 'downtime_per_user': 0, 'patches_applied': 12, 'security_updated': True, 'all_healthy': True } print(f"\nUpdate summary:") print(f" Total update time: {summary['total_reboot_time_minutes']} minutes") print(f" User-facing downtime: {summary['downtime_per_user']} minutes") print(f" Security patches applied: {summary['patches_applied']}") print(f"\n✓ All instances patched and healthy") print(f"✓ Zero-downtime achieved")
Example 4: Reboot with Health Check and Automatic Rollback

Reboot instance with immediate health verification, and automatic recovery if instance fails to reach healthy status.

#!/bin/bash
# Reboot with health check and rollback capability
INSTANCE_ID="i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
SNAPSHOT_ID="snap-preboot123456"  # Pre-reboot snapshot
RECOVERY_TIMEOUT=300  # 5 minutes

echo "Reboot with automatic health verification"
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Recovery timeout: ${RECOVERY_TIMEOUT}s"

# Create pre-reboot snapshot for rollback
echo ""
echo "Creating pre-reboot snapshot for rollback capability..."
VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].BlockDeviceMappings[0].Ebs.VolumeId' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

PRE_BOOT_SNAP=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --description "Pre-reboot snapshot for $INSTANCE_ID" \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'SnapshotId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Pre-reboot snapshot: $PRE_BOOT_SNAP"

# Reboot the instance
echo ""
echo "Initiating reboot..."
aws ec2 reboot-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reboot initiated"

# Monitor for successful health check
echo ""
echo "Monitoring instance health status (timeout: ${RECOVERY_TIMEOUT}s)..."

ELAPSED=0
INTERVAL=10

while [ $ELAPSED -lt $RECOVERY_TIMEOUT ]; do
  INSTANCE_STATUS=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
    --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
    --query 'InstanceStatuses[0].InstanceStatus.Status' \
    --output text \
    --region us-east-1 2>/dev/null)
  
  if [ "$INSTANCE_STATUS" == "ok" ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "✓ Instance health check PASSED"
    exit 0
  fi
  
  echo "  Status: $INSTANCE_STATUS (${ELAPSED}s elapsed)"
  sleep $INTERVAL
  ELAPSED=$((ELAPSED + INTERVAL))
done

# If we reach here, reboot failed
echo ""
echo "✗ Instance health check FAILED after ${RECOVERY_TIMEOUT}s"
echo "Attempting automatic recovery..."
echo "  - Snapshot available: $PRE_BOOT_SNAP"
echo "  - Manual recovery required"
Reboot with automatic health verification Instance: i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 Recovery timeout: 300s Creating pre-reboot snapshot for rollback capability... ✓ Pre-reboot snapshot: snap-preboot123456 Initiating reboot... ✓ Reboot initiated Monitoring instance health status (timeout: 300s)... Status: initializing (10s elapsed) Status: initializing (20s elapsed) Status: initializing (30s elapsed) Status: ok (45s elapsed) ✓ Instance health check PASSED
# Instance reboot with health verification from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.db import connection import time health_check = { 'instance_id': 'i-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'reboot_type': 'with health verification', 'recovery_timeout_seconds': 300, 'pre_reboot_snapshot': 'snap-preboot123456' } print(f"Instance Reboot - Health Verification") print(f"Instance: {health_check['instance_id']}") print(f"Recovery timeout: {health_check['recovery_timeout_seconds']}s") # Database connectivity check before reboot pre_reboot_db = { 'connection_status': 'connected', 'queries_executed': 4, 'response_time_ms': 12 } print(f"\nPre-reboot database status:") print(f" Connection: {pre_reboot_db['connection_status']}") print(f" Recent queries: {pre_reboot_db['queries_executed']}") # Application health check pre_reboot_app = { 'skills': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations': Location.objects.count(), 'total_records': Skills.objects.count() + Author.objects.count() + JobPost.objects.count() + Location.objects.count() } print(f"\nPre-reboot application state:") print(f" Total records: {pre_reboot_app['total_records']}") # Health check sequence during reboot health_sequence = [ {'elapsed_seconds': 10, 'status': 'initializing', 'description': 'System starting'}, {'elapsed_seconds': 20, 'status': 'initializing', 'description': 'Services loading'}, {'elapsed_seconds': 30, 'status': 'initializing', 'description': 'Database connection'}, {'elapsed_seconds': 45, 'status': 'ok', 'description': 'Fully healthy'} ] print(f"\nHealth check progression:") for check in health_sequence: print(f" {check['elapsed_seconds']:3d}s: {check['status']:15s} - {check['description']}") # Post-reboot validation post_reboot = { 'timestamp': timezone.now().isoformat(), 'system_healthy': True, 'database_accessible': True, 'app_state_intact': pre_reboot_app['total_records'] > 0, 'recovery_time_seconds': 45 } print(f"\nPost-reboot validation:") print(f" System healthy: {post_reboot['system_healthy']}") print(f" Database accessible: {post_reboot['database_accessible']}") print(f" Recovery time: {post_reboot['recovery_time_seconds']}s") print(f"\n✓ Reboot completed successfully") print(f"✓ All health checks passed") print(f"✓ No manual intervention needed")
Example 5: Reboot with Performance Baseline Comparison

Reboot instance and compare performance metrics before and after to verify system efficiency improvements (e.g., memory leaks cleared, kernel optimizations).

#!/bin/bash
# Reboot with performance comparison
INSTANCE_ID="i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw"
NAMESPACE="AWS/EC2"

echo "Instance reboot with performance baseline comparison"
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Capture pre-reboot metrics (last hour)
echo ""
echo "Collecting pre-reboot metrics..."

PRE_REBOOT_CPU=$(aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace $NAMESPACE \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=$INSTANCE_ID \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '1 hour ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --period 300 \
  --statistics Average \
  --query 'Datapoints[*].Average' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "✓ Pre-reboot metrics captured"

# Perform reboot
echo ""
echo "Initiating reboot..."
aws ec2 reboot-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

# Wait for recovery
aws ec2 wait instance-status-ok \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Reboot completed"

# Allow time for post-reboot stabilization
echo "Waiting for post-reboot stabilization (2 minutes)..."
sleep 120

# Capture post-reboot metrics
echo ""
echo "Collecting post-reboot metrics..."

POST_REBOOT_CPU=$(aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace $NAMESPACE \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=$INSTANCE_ID \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '2 minutes ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --end-time $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S) \
  --period 300 \
  --statistics Average \
  --query 'Datapoints[*].Average' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

echo "✓ Post-reboot metrics captured"

echo ""
echo "Performance comparison:"
echo "  Pre-reboot average CPU: $(echo $PRE_REBOOT_CPU | awk '{print $NF}')%"
echo "  Post-reboot average CPU: $(echo $POST_REBOOT_CPU | awk '{print $NF}')%"
echo ""
echo "Metric analysis complete"
Instance reboot with performance baseline comparison Instance: i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw Collecting pre-reboot metrics... ✓ Pre-reboot metrics captured Initiating reboot... ✓ Reboot completed Waiting for post-reboot stabilization (2 minutes)... Collecting post-reboot metrics... ✓ Post-reboot metrics captured Performance comparison: Pre-reboot average CPU: 72% Post-reboot average CPU: 48% Metric analysis complete
# Instance reboot with performance baseline analysis from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.db.models import Count import json # Performance baseline context perf_baseline = { 'instance_id': 'i-0lmn5opq6rst7uvw', 'reboot_reason': 'Clear memory leaks and reset system', 'measurement_period': 'Pre vs Post reboot' } print(f"Instance Performance Baseline") print(f"Instance: {perf_baseline['instance_id']}") # Pre-reboot performance pre_reboot = { 'cpu_average': 72, 'cpu_peak': 92, 'memory_available_mb': 1024, 'network_in_mbps': 15.5, 'disk_io_ops': 450 } print(f"\nPre-reboot performance (last hour):") print(f" CPU utilization: {pre_reboot['cpu_average']}% avg, {pre_reboot['cpu_peak']}% peak") print(f" Available memory: {pre_reboot['memory_available_mb']} MB") print(f" Network throughput: {pre_reboot['network_in_mbps']} Mbps") print(f" Disk I/O: {pre_reboot['disk_io_ops']} ops/sec") # Application metrics during high load app_metrics_pre = { 'jobs_loaded': JobPost.objects.count(), 'query_time_ms': 45, 'concurrent_requests': 145 } print(f"\nApplication metrics (pre-reboot):") print(f" Jobs in database: {app_metrics_pre['jobs_loaded']}") print(f" Avg query time: {app_metrics_pre['query_time_ms']}ms") print(f" Concurrent requests: {app_metrics_pre['concurrent_requests']}") # Post-reboot performance post_reboot = { 'cpu_average': 48, 'cpu_peak': 68, 'memory_available_mb': 2048, 'network_in_mbps': 14.2, 'disk_io_ops': 320 } print(f"\nPost-reboot performance (after 2 min stabilization):") print(f" CPU utilization: {post_reboot['cpu_average']}% avg, {post_reboot['cpu_peak']}% peak") print(f" Available memory: {post_reboot['memory_available_mb']} MB") print(f" Network throughput: {post_reboot['network_in_mbps']} Mbps") print(f" Disk I/O: {post_reboot['disk_io_ops']} ops/sec") # Performance improvements improvements = { 'cpu_reduction': pre_reboot['cpu_average'] - post_reboot['cpu_average'], 'memory_gain': post_reboot['memory_available_mb'] - pre_reboot['memory_available_mb'], 'disk_io_reduction': pre_reboot['disk_io_ops'] - post_reboot['disk_io_ops'], 'cpu_improvement_percent': ((pre_reboot['cpu_average'] - post_reboot['cpu_average']) / pre_reboot['cpu_average'] * 100) } print(f"\nPerformance improvements:") print(f" CPU reduction: {improvements['cpu_reduction']}% ({improvements['cpu_improvement_percent']:.1f}%)") print(f" Memory recovery: +{improvements['memory_gain']} MB") print(f" Disk I/O reduction: {improvements['disk_io_reduction']} ops/sec") # Application performance after reboot app_metrics_post = { 'query_time_ms': 28, 'concurrent_capacity': 220 } print(f"\nApplication efficiency gains:") print(f" Query time improved: {app_metrics_pre['query_time_ms']}ms → {app_metrics_post['query_time_ms']}ms") print(f" Concurrent capacity: {app_metrics_pre['concurrent_requests']} → {app_metrics_post['concurrent_capacity']} requests") print(f"\n✓ Reboot improved system efficiency") print(f"✓ Memory leaks cleared, performance optimized")

create-security-group

Creates a security group that acts as a firewall controlling inbound and outbound traffic to EC2 instances. Security groups define which protocols, ports, and IP addresses are allowed to communicate with your instances. Essential for Django application access control and network isolation.

Syntax
aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name {name} \
  --description '{description}' \
  --vpc-id {vpc-id} \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --group-name (string, required): Name of the security group. Cannot contain spaces or special characters
  • --description (string, required): Description of what this security group is for
  • --vpc-id (string): VPC ID where security group is created. If omitted, uses default VPC
  • --region (string): AWS region where security group will be created
Return Value
  • GroupId (string): ID of the newly created security group (sg-xxxxxxxxx)
  • GroupName (string): Name of the security group
  • VpcId (string): VPC where security group was created
  • Tags (Array): Any tags attached to the security group
5 Detailed EC2 create-security-group Examples
Example 1: Create Django Application Security Group for Web Traffic

Create a security group for Django web application allowing HTTP/HTTPS traffic from the internet.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Django web application security group
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Django Web Application Security Group..."

# Create the security group
SG_RESPONSE=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name django-web-app \
  --description "Security group for Django web application - allows HTTP/HTTPS from internet" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION)

SG_ID=$(echo $SG_RESPONSE | jq -r '.GroupId')

echo "✓ Security Group Created: $SG_ID"
echo "  Name: django-web-app"
echo "  VPC: $VPC_ID"

# Add tags for identification
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources $SG_ID \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=django-web-app Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Application,Value=jobpost \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Tags applied"
echo ""
echo "Security Group Details:"
echo "  Group ID: $SG_ID"
echo "  Group Name: django-web-app"
echo "  Description: Security group for Django web application - allows HTTP/HTTPS from internet"
echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "  1. Add inbound rules (authorize-security-group-ingress)"
echo "  2. Attach to EC2 instances"
echo "  3. Configure database and cache security groups"
Creating Django Web Application Security Group... ✓ Security Group Created: sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 { "GroupId": "sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "OwnerId": "123456789012" } ✓ Tags applied Security Group Details: Group ID: sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 Group Name: django-web-app Description: Security group for Django web application - allows HTTP/HTTPS from internet Next steps: 1. Add inbound rules (authorize-security-group-ingress) 2. Attach to EC2 instances 3. Configure database and cache security groups
# Create Django web application security group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import json sg_context = { 'group_id': 'sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'group_name': 'django-web-app', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'environment': 'production', 'purpose': 'Django web application traffic' } print(f"Security Group Created") print(f"Group ID: {sg_context['group_id']}") print(f"Name: {sg_context['group_name']}") # Security group rules to be added (not yet active) planned_rules = { 'inbound': [ {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 80, 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0', 'description': 'HTTP from internet'}, {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 443, 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0', 'description': 'HTTPS from internet'}, {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 22, 'cidr': '10.0.0.0/8', 'description': 'SSH from VPC'} ], 'outbound': [ {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 3306, 'cidr': 'sg-db-group', 'description': 'MySQL to database SG'}, {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 6379, 'cidr': 'sg-cache-group', 'description': 'Redis to cache SG'}, {'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 443, 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0', 'description': 'HTTPS to external APIs'} ] } print(f"\nPlanned inbound rules (to be configured):") for rule in planned_rules['inbound']: print(f" - {rule['protocol'].upper():3s} port {rule['port']:5d} from {rule['cidr']:18s} ({rule['description']})") print(f"\nPlanned outbound rules (to be configured):") for rule in planned_rules['outbound']: print(f" - {rule['protocol'].upper():3s} port {rule['port']:5d} to {rule['cidr']:18s} ({rule['description']})") # Current rules (empty - newly created) current_rules = { 'inbound': [], 'outbound': [ {'protocol': '-1', 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0', 'description': 'All outbound (default)'} ] } print(f"\nCurrent rules (active now):") print(f" Inbound: {len(current_rules['inbound'])} rules") print(f" Outbound: {len(current_rules['outbound'])} rules (all traffic allowed by default)") # Application data count app_data = { 'skills': Skills.objects.count(), 'authors': Author.objects.count(), 'jobs': JobPost.objects.count(), 'locations': Location.objects.count() } print(f"\nApplication readiness:") print(f" Skills configured: {app_data['skills']}") print(f" Authors in system: {app_data['authors']}") print(f" Job postings: {app_data['jobs']}") print(f" Locations: {app_data['locations']}") print(f"\n✓ Security group created and ready for rules") print(f"✓ Tags applied for organization")
Example 2: Create Database Security Group for MySQL Access

Create a dedicated security group for RDS MySQL database that only allows traffic from web application security group.

#!/bin/bash
# Create RDS database security group
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating RDS Database Security Group..."

# Create the security group
SG_RESPONSE=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name django-database \
  --description "Security group for RDS MySQL - allows access only from Django app security group" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION)

DB_SG_ID=$(echo $SG_RESPONSE | jq -r '.GroupId')

echo "✓ Database Security Group Created: $DB_SG_ID"

# Add tags
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources $DB_SG_ID \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=django-database Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Database,Value=mysql-rds \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Tags applied to database security group"
echo ""
echo "Database Security Group Details:"
echo "  Group ID: $DB_SG_ID"
echo "  Purpose: RDS MySQL database access"
echo ""
echo "Important:"
echo "  - This security group is empty (no inbound rules yet)"
echo "  - You must authorize inbound traffic from django-web-app security group"
echo "  - MySQL port 3306 will be accessible only from web app instances"
Creating RDS Database Security Group... ✓ Database Security Group Created: sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9 { "GroupId": "sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9", "OwnerId": "123456789012" } ✓ Tags applied to database security group Database Security Group Details: Group ID: sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9 Purpose: RDS MySQL database access Important: - This security group is empty (no inbound rules yet) - You must authorize inbound traffic from django-web-app security group - MySQL port 3306 will be accessible only from web app instances
# Create database security group for RDS MySQL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.db import connections db_sg_context = { 'group_id': 'sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9', 'group_name': 'django-database', 'database_type': 'RDS MySQL', 'database_port': 3306 } print(f"Database Security Group Created") print(f"Group ID: {db_sg_context['group_id']}") print(f"Name: {db_sg_context['group_name']}") print(f"Purpose: {db_sg_context['database_type']} access control") # Database connection details (not yet connected) db_config = { 'host': 'django-db.c1a2b3c4d5e6.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com', 'port': 3306, 'username': 'admin', 'database': 'anihpj_jobpost' } print(f"\nDatabase configuration (to be used):") print(f" Host: {db_config['host']}") print(f" Port: {db_config['port']}") print(f" Database: {db_config['database']}") # Security rules to be applied inbound_rules = [ { 'source': 'sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'source_name': 'django-web-app', 'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 3306, 'description': 'MySQL from Django web app' }, { 'source': 'sg-cache-group', 'source_name': 'django-cache', 'protocol': 'tcp', 'port': 3306, 'description': 'MySQL from cache/backup processes' } ] print(f"\nPlanned inbound rules for database:") for rule in inbound_rules: print(f" - {rule['source_name']:20s} ({rule['source']}) → TCP {rule['port']}") # Current database access try: # Try to get connection stats from django.db import connection db_connection = { 'status': 'connected', 'queries_executed': 0 } except Exception as e: db_connection = { 'status': 'not yet configured', 'reason': 'Security group rules not yet applied' } print(f"\nDatabase connection status: {db_connection['status']}") # Models that depend on database access models_dependent = { 'Skills': Skills, 'User': User, 'Author': Author, 'Location': Location, 'JobPost': JobPost } print(f"\nModels waiting for database access:") for model_name, model_class in models_dependent.items(): print(f" - {model_name}") print(f"\n✓ Database security group created") print(f"✓ Ready for inbound rules configuration")
Example 3: Create Cache/Redis Security Group

Create security group for Redis cache with restricted access from application instances only.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Redis cache security group
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Redis Cache Security Group..."

# Create the security group
SG_RESPONSE=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name django-cache \
  --description "Security group for Redis cache - allows access from Django application instances" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION)

CACHE_SG_ID=$(echo $SG_RESPONSE | jq -r '.GroupId')

echo "✓ Cache Security Group Created: $CACHE_SG_ID"

# Add tags
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources $CACHE_SG_ID \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=django-cache Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Service,Value=redis \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Tags applied"
echo ""
echo "Cache Security Group Details:"
echo "  Group ID: $CACHE_SG_ID"
echo "  Purpose: Redis cache for session storage and query caching"
echo "  Port: 6379 (Redis default)"
echo ""
echo "Security characteristics:"
echo "  - Restricted to application security group only"
echo "  - No internet access allowed"
echo "  - High performance for in-memory operations"
Creating Redis Cache Security Group... ✓ Cache Security Group Created: sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0 { "GroupId": "sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0", "OwnerId": "123456789012" } ✓ Tags applied Cache Security Group Details: Group ID: sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0 Purpose: Redis cache for session storage and query caching Port: 6379 (Redis default) Security characteristics: - Restricted to application security group only - No internet access allowed - High performance for in-memory operations
# Create Redis cache security group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.core.cache import cache from django.contrib.sessions.models import Session cache_sg_context = { 'group_id': 'sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0', 'group_name': 'django-cache', 'cache_type': 'Redis', 'cache_port': 6379 } print(f"Cache Security Group Created") print(f"Group ID: {cache_sg_context['group_id']}") print(f"Purpose: Redis cache access control") # Cache configuration (to be used with app) cache_config = { 'host': 'django-cache.c1a2b3c4d5e6.ng.0001.use1.cache.amazonaws.com', 'port': 6379, 'protocol': 'redis', 'max_connections': 100 } print(f"\nCache configuration:") print(f" Type: {cache_sg_context['cache_type']}") print(f" Port: {cache_config['port']}") print(f" Max connections: {cache_config['max_connections']}") # Cache usage patterns cache_usage = { 'session_storage': True, 'query_caching': True, 'task_queue': True, 'rate_limiting': True } print(f"\nCache usage in Django application:") print(f" Session storage: {cache_usage['session_storage']}") print(f" Query caching: {cache_usage['query_caching']}") print(f" Task queue: {cache_usage['task_queue']}") print(f" Rate limiting: {cache_usage['rate_limiting']}") # Expected cache operations cache_operations = { 'set_operations': 'Storing user sessions, query results, job listings', 'get_operations': 'Retrieving cached job searches, user preferences', 'ttl_operations': 'Session expiry (24 hours), query cache (1 hour)' } print(f"\nCache operations:") print(f" Write: {cache_operations['set_operations']}") print(f" Read: {cache_operations['get_operations']}") # Security profile security_profile = { 'source_restriction': 'django-web-app security group only', 'encryption': 'In-transit encryption recommended', 'auth': 'AUTH token required for connection', 'backup': 'Automatic backup every 5 minutes' } print(f"\nSecurity profile:") print(f" Allowed sources: {security_profile['source_restriction']}") print(f" Encryption: {security_profile['encryption']}") print(f"\n✓ Cache security group created") print(f"✓ Ready for configuration with web app security group")
Example 4: Create Load Balancer Security Group with ALB Access

Create security group for Application Load Balancer that accepts internet traffic and forwards to web application instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Application Load Balancer security group
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Application Load Balancer Security Group..."

# Create the security group
SG_RESPONSE=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name django-alb \
  --description "Security group for Application Load Balancer - accepts internet HTTP/HTTPS traffic" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION)

ALB_SG_ID=$(echo $SG_RESPONSE | jq -r '.GroupId')

echo "✓ ALB Security Group Created: $ALB_SG_ID"

# Add tags
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources $ALB_SG_ID \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=django-alb Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Service,Value=load-balancer \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Tags applied"
echo ""
echo "ALB Security Group Details:"
echo "  Group ID: $ALB_SG_ID"
echo "  Purpose: Public-facing load balancer for Django application"
echo "  Inbound: HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) from 0.0.0.0/0"
echo "  Outbound: All traffic to web app security group"
echo ""
echo "Traffic flow:"
echo "  Internet → ALB (port 80/443) → Django instances (port 8000)"
Creating Application Load Balancer Security Group... ✓ ALB Security Group Created: sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1 { "GroupId": "sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1", "OwnerId": "123456789012" } ✓ Tags applied ALB Security Group Details: Group ID: sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1 Purpose: Public-facing load balancer for Django application Inbound: HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) from 0.0.0.0/0 Outbound: All traffic to web app security group Traffic flow: Internet → ALB (port 80/443) → Django instances (port 8000)
# Create load balancer security group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import json alb_sg_context = { 'group_id': 'sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1', 'group_name': 'django-alb', 'purpose': 'Application Load Balancer' } print(f"ALB Security Group Created") print(f"Group ID: {alb_sg_context['group_id']}") print(f"Purpose: {alb_sg_context['purpose']}") # ALB configuration alb_config = { 'dns_name': 'django-alb-1234567890.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com', 'scheme': 'internet-facing', 'ip_address_type': 'ipv4', 'type': 'application' } print(f"\nALB configuration:") print(f" Type: {alb_config['type']}") print(f" Scheme: {alb_config['scheme']}") print(f" DNS: {alb_config['dns_name']}") # Listener rules to be configured listeners = [ { 'protocol': 'HTTP', 'port': 80, 'target_group': 'django-app-80', 'target_port': 8000, 'rule': 'Path /* → forward all' }, { 'protocol': 'HTTPS', 'port': 443, 'target_group': 'django-app-443', 'target_port': 8000, 'rule': 'Path /* → forward all' } ] print(f"\nPlanned listener rules:") for listener in listeners: print(f" - {listener['protocol']:6s} {listener['port']:4d} → {listener['target_group']:20s} (target port {listener['target_port']})") # Traffic distribution traffic_config = { 'health_check_interval': 30, 'health_check_timeout': 5, 'healthy_threshold': 2, 'unhealthy_threshold': 2, 'stickiness': 'Enabled (1 day)' } print(f"\nLoad balancing configuration:") print(f" Health check interval: {traffic_config['health_check_interval']}s") print(f" Healthy threshold: {traffic_config['healthy_threshold']} checks") print(f" Session stickiness: {traffic_config['stickiness']}") # Expected traffic patterns traffic_patterns = { 'avg_requests_per_minute': 1200, 'peak_requests_per_minute': 5000, 'concurrent_connections': 500 } print(f"\nExpected traffic:") print(f" Average: {traffic_patterns['avg_requests_per_minute']} req/min") print(f" Peak: {traffic_patterns['peak_requests_per_minute']} req/min") print(f" Concurrent: {traffic_patterns['concurrent_connections']} connections") print(f"\n✓ ALB security group created") print(f"✓ Ready to accept internet traffic")
Example 5: Create Development/Staging Environment Security Group with SSH Access

Create a security group for development/staging environment with SSH access and less restrictive rules for testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Create development security group with SSH and debugging access
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating Development Environment Security Group..."

# Create the security group
SG_RESPONSE=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name django-dev \
  --description "Security group for development environment - allows SSH and internal communication for testing" \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION)

DEV_SG_ID=$(echo $SG_RESPONSE | jq -r '.GroupId')

echo "✓ Development Security Group Created: $DEV_SG_ID"

# Add tags
aws ec2 create-tags \
  --resources $DEV_SG_ID \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=django-dev Key=Environment,Value=development Key=Team,Value=engineering \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Tags applied"
echo ""
echo "Development Security Group Details:"
echo "  Group ID: $DEV_SG_ID"
echo "  Environment: Development/Staging"
echo "  SSH Access: Enabled for team members"
echo "  Database Access: Direct access for debugging"
echo "  Cache Access: Full access for troubleshooting"
echo ""
echo "Rules to be configured:"
echo "  - SSH (22) from team VPN/office IPs"
echo "  - HTTP (80) from 0.0.0.0/0 for testing"
echo "  - Django debug port (8000-8100) for local testing"
echo "  - PostgreSQL (5432) from VPC for direct DB access"
Creating Development Environment Security Group... ✓ Development Security Group Created: sg-4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1k2 { "GroupId": "sg-4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1k2", "OwnerId": "123456789012" } ✓ Tags applied Development Security Group Details: Group ID: sg-4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1k2 Environment: Development/Staging SSH Access: Enabled for team members Database Access: Direct access for debugging Cache Access: Full access for troubleshooting Rules to be configured: - SSH (22) from team VPN/office IPs - HTTP (80) from 0.0.0.0/0 for testing - Django debug port (8000-8100) for local testing - PostgreSQL (5432) from VPC for direct DB access
# Create development environment security group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.conf import settings dev_sg_context = { 'group_id': 'sg-4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1k2', 'group_name': 'django-dev', 'environment': 'development', 'purpose': 'Team development and testing' } print(f"Development Security Group Created") print(f"Group ID: {dev_sg_context['group_id']}") print(f"Environment: {dev_sg_context['environment']}") # Development configuration dev_config = { 'team_vpn_cidr': '203.0.113.0/24', 'office_ip': '198.51.100.42/32', 'django_debug': True, 'logging_level': 'DEBUG' } print(f"\nDevelopment configuration:") print(f" VPN Access CIDR: {dev_config['team_vpn_cidr']}") print(f" Office IP: {dev_config['office_ip']}") print(f" Django Debug Mode: {dev_config['django_debug']}") # Planned access rules access_rules = [ {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 22, 'source': 'Team VPN', 'purpose': 'SSH management'}, {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 80, 'source': '0.0.0.0/0', 'purpose': 'HTTP testing'}, {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 8000, 'source': 'Team VPN', 'purpose': 'Django debug server'}, {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 5432, 'source': 'VPC CIDR', 'purpose': 'Direct DB access'}, {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 6379, 'source': 'VPC CIDR', 'purpose': 'Redis troubleshooting'}, {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 3000, 'source': 'Team VPN', 'purpose': 'Frontend dev server'} ] print(f"\nPlanned access rules:") for rule in access_rules: print(f" - {rule['protocol']:4s} port {rule['port']:5d} from {rule['source']:20s} ({rule['purpose']})") # Development tools and services dev_services = { 'django_server': 'Port 8000 (hot-reload enabled)', 'postgres_admin': 'pgAdmin on port 5050', 'redis_monitor': 'Redis CLI access for queue inspection', 'logs_viewer': 'CloudWatch Logs Insights' } print(f"\nDevelopment tools available:") for service, details in dev_services.items(): print(f" - {service}: {details}") # Debug information debug_info = { 'exception_details': 'Visible in responses', 'sql_queries': 'Logged and analyzed', 'api_responses': 'Full JSON with stack traces', 'request_logs': 'Verbose with headers' } print(f"\nDebug features:") for feature, enabled in debug_info.items(): print(f" - {feature}: {enabled}") print(f"\n✓ Development security group created") print(f"✓ Ready for team debugging and testing")

authorize-security-group-ingress

Adds inbound traffic rules to a security group, allowing specific protocols, ports, and source IPs/security groups to communicate with instances. This is how you open up access to Django applications (HTTP/HTTPS), databases (MySQL/PostgreSQL), and cache systems (Redis).

Syntax
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id {sg-id} \
  --protocol {tcp|udp|icmp|-1} \
  --port {port-number} \
  --cidr {cidr-block} \
  --region {region}
Parameters Description
  • --group-id (string, required): Security group ID to add rule to (sg-xxxxxxxxx)
  • --protocol (string, required): Protocol type - tcp, udp, icmp, or -1 for all
  • --port (string): Port number or port range (e.g., 80 or 8000-8100)
  • --cidr (string): CIDR block allowing access (e.g., 0.0.0.0/0 for internet, 10.0.0.0/16 for VPC)
  • --source-security-group-id (string): Allow traffic from another security group instead of CIDR
  • --description (string): Description of what this rule is for
Return Value
  • Return (boolean): True if rule was successfully added
  • SecurityGroupRules (Array): Details of the rule that was added
5 Detailed EC2 authorize-security-group-ingress Examples
Example 1: Allow HTTP and HTTPS from Internet to ALB

Add inbound rules to ALB security group allowing internet traffic on standard web ports.

#!/bin/bash
# Add HTTP/HTTPS inbound rules to ALB security group
ALB_SG="sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Adding HTTP and HTTPS rules to ALB security group..."

# Add HTTP rule (port 80)
echo "Adding HTTP (port 80) rule..."
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $ALB_SG \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 80 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --description "HTTP from internet" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ HTTP rule added"

# Add HTTPS rule (port 443)
echo "Adding HTTPS (port 443) rule..."
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $ALB_SG \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 443 \
  --cidr 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --description "HTTPS from internet" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ HTTPS rule added"
echo ""
echo "ALB security group rules configured:"
echo "  ✓ HTTP (port 80) from 0.0.0.0/0"
echo "  ✓ HTTPS (port 443) from 0.0.0.0/0"
echo ""
echo "Next: Configure target group and attach Django instances"
Adding HTTP and HTTPS rules to ALB security group... Adding HTTP (port 80) rule... ✓ HTTP rule added { "Return": true, "SecurityGroupRules": [{ "GroupId": "sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1", "GroupOwnerId": "123456789012", "SecurityGroupRuleId": "sgr-01234567890abcdef", "IpProtocol": "tcp", "FromPort": 80, "ToPort": 80, "CidrIpv4": "0.0.0.0/0", "IsEgress": false, "Tags": [] }] } ✓ HTTPS rule added Adding HTTP/HTTPS rules completed successfully
# Allow HTTP/HTTPS to ALB from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from django.conf import settings alb_rules_context = { 'alb_sg': 'sg-3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j1', 'rule1': {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 80, 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0'}, 'rule2': {'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 443, 'cidr': '0.0.0.0/0'} } print(f"ALB Security Group - Inbound Rules Added") print(f"Security Group: {alb_rules_context['alb_sg']}") # HTTP traffic http_rule = { 'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 80, 'source': 'Internet (0.0.0.0/0)', 'rule_id': 'sgr-01234567890abcdef' } print(f"\nHTTP Rule:") print(f" Protocol: {http_rule['protocol']}") print(f" Port: {http_rule['port']}") print(f" Source: {http_rule['source']}") print(f" Rule ID: {http_rule['rule_id']}") # HTTPS traffic https_rule = { 'protocol': 'TCP', 'port': 443, 'source': 'Internet (0.0.0.0/0)', 'rule_id': 'sgr-11234567890abcdef' } print(f"\nHTTPS Rule:") print(f" Protocol: {https_rule['protocol']}") print(f" Port: {https_rule['port']}") print(f" Source: {https_rule['source']}") # Traffic flow traffic_flow = { 'internet_users': 'Can now access ALB on port 80', 'alb_action': 'Listens and forwards to target group', 'target_group': 'Points to Django instances (port 8000)', 'django_app': 'Receives traffic on 0.0.0.0:8000' } print(f"\nTraffic flow enabled:") print(f" Users → Internet → ALB:80/443 → Target group → Django:8000") # Expected traffic patterns traffic_stats = { 'expected_requests_per_minute': 1200, 'peak_concurrent': 500, 'avg_response_time_ms': 150, 'error_rate_percent': 0.5 } print(f"\nExpected traffic patterns:") print(f" Average requests: {traffic_stats['expected_requests_per_minute']} req/min") print(f" Peak concurrent: {traffic_stats['peak_concurrent']} connections") print(f" Response time: {traffic_stats['avg_response_time_ms']}ms average") # Application data accessible now app_data = { 'job_listings': JobPost.objects.count(), 'authors': Author.objects.count(), 'users': User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() } print(f"\nApplication accessible now:") print(f" Job listings: {app_data['job_listings']}") print(f" Authors: {app_data['authors']}") print(f" Active users: {app_data['users']}") print(f"\n✓ ALB now accessible from internet") print(f"✓ Traffic flowing to Django application")
Example 2: Allow Django Instances to Access MySQL Database

Add inbound rule to database security group allowing the Django web app security group to access MySQL on port 3306.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow Django app security group to access MySQL database
DB_SG="sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9"
APP_SG="sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Adding database access rule..."
echo "Allowing Django app ($APP_SG) to access MySQL database ($DB_SG)"

# Add MySQL inbound rule from app security group
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $DB_SG \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 3306 \
  --source-group-id $APP_SG \
  --description "MySQL access from Django web app instances" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ MySQL rule added"
echo ""
echo "Database access configured:"
echo "  Source Security Group: $APP_SG (django-web-app)"
echo "  Target Security Group: $DB_SG (django-database)"
echo "  Protocol: TCP"
echo "  Port: 3306 (MySQL)"
echo ""
echo "Django instances can now:"
echo "  - Connect to RDS MySQL database"
echo "  - Execute queries"
echo "  - Access job listings, authors, skills data"
Adding database access rule... Allowing Django app (sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8) to access MySQL database (sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9) { "Return": true, "SecurityGroupRules": [{ "GroupId": "sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9", "GroupOwnerId": "123456789012", "SecurityGroupRuleId": "sgr-mysql123456789", "IpProtocol": "tcp", "FromPort": 3306, "ToPort": 3306, "ReferencedGroupInfo": { "GroupId": "sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8", "GroupOwnerId": "123456789012" }, "IsEgress": false, "Tags": [] }] } ✓ MySQL rule added Database access configured successfully
# Allow Django app to access MySQL database from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db import connections from django.utils import timezone db_access_context = { 'database_sg': 'sg-1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9', 'app_sg': 'sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'database_port': 3306, 'database_type': 'MySQL/RDS' } print(f"Database Access Rule - Django to MySQL") print(f"Database SG: {db_access_context['database_sg']}") print(f"App SG: {db_access_context['app_sg']}") # Connection details db_connection = { 'host': 'django-db.c1a2b3c4d5e6.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com', 'port': 3306, 'username': 'admin', 'database': 'anihpj_jobpost', 'status': 'accessible' } print(f"\nDatabase connection details:") print(f" Host: {db_connection['host']}") print(f" Port: {db_connection['port']}") print(f" Database: {db_connection['database']}") # Security group rule details rule_details = { 'protocol': 'TCP', 'port_from': 3306, 'port_to': 3306, 'source_type': 'Security Group (reference)', 'source_id': db_access_context['app_sg'], 'bidirectional': False } print(f"\nRule details:") print(f" Protocol: {rule_details['protocol']}") print(f" Port: {rule_details['port_from']}") print(f" Source: {rule_details['source_type']}") # Django models accessing database django_models = { 'Skills': Skills, 'User': User, 'Author': Author, 'Location': Location, 'JobPost': JobPost } print(f"\nDjango models now accessible:") for model_name in django_models.keys(): print(f" - {model_name}") # Database queries that now work example_queries = { 'skills_query': 'Retrieve all skill proficiency levels', 'author_query': 'Fetch job post authors with locations', 'job_query': 'List all published job posts with required skills', 'location_query': 'Get unique job posting locations' } print(f"\nQueries now working:") for query_name, description in example_queries.items(): print(f" - {description}") # Test connectivity try: from django.db import connection cursor = connection.cursor() cursor.execute("SELECT 1") connectivity = "✓ Connected" except Exception as e: connectivity = f"✗ Error: {str(e)}" print(f"\nDatabase connectivity: {connectivity}") # Expected traffic db_traffic = { 'queries_per_second': 50, 'avg_query_time_ms': 25, 'max_connections': 100, 'current_connections': 5 } print(f"\nDatabase traffic profile:") print(f" Expected queries: {db_traffic['queries_per_second']} req/sec") print(f" Max connections: {db_traffic['max_connections']}") print(f"\n✓ Django instances can access MySQL database") print(f"✓ Secure cross-SG access configured")
Example 3: Allow Django Instances to Access Redis Cache

Add inbound rule to cache security group allowing application instances to connect to Redis on port 6379.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow Django instances to access Redis cache
CACHE_SG="sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0"
APP_SG="sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Adding Redis cache access rule..."

# Add Redis inbound rule from app security group
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $CACHE_SG \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 6379 \
  --source-group-id $APP_SG \
  --description "Redis cache access from Django instances" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Redis rule added"
echo ""
echo "Redis cache access configured:"
echo "  Source: django-web-app ($APP_SG)"
echo "  Target: django-cache ($CACHE_SG)"
echo "  Port: 6379 (Redis)"
echo ""
echo "Django can now:"
echo "  - Store and retrieve user sessions"
echo "  - Cache query results (Jobs, Skills, Authors)"
echo "  - Queue background tasks"
echo "  - Implement rate limiting for API access"
Adding Redis cache access rule... { "Return": true, "SecurityGroupRules": [{ "GroupId": "sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0", "GroupOwnerId": "123456789012", "SecurityGroupRuleId": "sgr-redis123456789", "IpProtocol": "tcp", "FromPort": 6379, "ToPort": 6379, "ReferencedGroupInfo": { "GroupId": "sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8" }, "IsEgress": false }] } ✓ Redis rule added Redis cache access configured successfully
# Allow Django instances to access Redis cache from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.core.cache import cache from django.contrib.sessions.models import Session import json cache_access_context = { 'cache_sg': 'sg-2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0', 'app_sg': 'sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'cache_port': 6379, 'cache_type': 'Redis' } print(f"Cache Access Rule - Django to Redis") print(f"Cache SG: {cache_access_context['cache_sg']}") print(f"App SG: {cache_access_context['app_sg']}") # Cache connection details cache_config = { 'host': 'django-cache.c1a2b3c4d5e6.ng.0001.use1.cache.amazonaws.com', 'port': 6379, 'protocol': 'redis', 'status': 'accessible' } print(f"\nCache connection details:") print(f" Type: {cache_config['protocol'].upper()}") print(f" Host: {cache_config['host']}") print(f" Port: {cache_config['port']}") # Cache usage patterns cache_patterns = { 'session_storage': 'User session data (24 hour TTL)', 'query_cache': 'Job listings, author profiles (1 hour TTL)', 'api_cache': 'Third-party API responses (30 min TTL)', 'task_queue': 'Celery/background job queue', 'rate_limit': 'API rate limiting per user' } print(f"\nCache usage patterns now enabled:") for pattern, description in cache_patterns.items(): print(f" - {pattern}: {description}") # Expected cache operations cache_metrics = { 'set_operations_per_second': 200, 'get_operations_per_second': 500, 'avg_hit_rate': 85, 'avg_response_time_ms': 2 } print(f"\nCache performance metrics:") print(f" SET operations: {cache_metrics['set_operations_per_second']} req/sec") print(f" GET operations: {cache_metrics['get_operations_per_second']} req/sec") print(f" Hit rate: {cache_metrics['avg_hit_rate']}%") print(f" Response time: {cache_metrics['avg_response_time_ms']}ms") # Application data caching cached_data = { 'job_listings': 'All published JobPost objects', 'author_profiles': 'Author and Location data', 'skill_cache': 'Skills and proficiency levels', 'user_sessions': 'Active user session data' } print(f"\nData now cached:") for data_type, description in cached_data.items(): print(f" - {data_type}: {description}") # Example cache operations cache_ops = { 'session_set': 'cache.set(f"session:{user_id}", session_data, 86400)', 'job_get': 'cache.get(f"jobs:all:page:{page}")', 'skill_cache': 'cache.get("skills:all")' } print(f"\nExample cache operations:") for op_name, code in cache_ops.items(): print(f" {code}") print(f"\n✓ Redis cache now accessible") print(f"✓ Session and query caching enabled")
Example 4: Allow SSH Access from Team VPN for Administration
