Global load balancing setup global_lb = { 'dns_service': 'Route 53', 'routing_policy': 'Geolocation + Failover', 'health_checks': 'CloudWatch + ALB', 'failover_trigger': 'Primary region ALB unhealthy' } print(f"Global Load Balancing:") for setting, value in global_lb.items(): print(f" {setting}: {value}") print(f"") # Data replication strategy replication = { 'database': 'Multi-region RDS (MySQL)', 'replication_lag': '< 1 second (DMS)', 'cache': 'ElastiCache Global Datastore', 'static_files': 'S3 (cross-region replication)', 'user_sessions': 'DynamoDB (global tables)' } print(f"Data Replication Strategy:") for component, config in replication.items(): print(f" {component}: {config}") # Failover scenarios failover_scenarios = { 'scenario_1': { 'trigger': 'us-east-1 region outage', 'action': 'Route 53 fails over to eu-west-1', 'time': '< 2 minutes', 'data_loss': 'None (multi-region replication)' }, 'scenario_2': { 'trigger': 'Primary ALB health check fails', 'action': 'Route 53 health check triggers failover', 'time': '< 60 seconds', 'data_loss': 'None (in-flight requests retried)' }, 'scenario_3': { 'trigger': 'Network partition (split-brain)', 'action': 'Manual failover required', 'time': 'Operator decision', 'data_loss': 'Possible (must reconcile)' } } print(f"\nFailover Scenarios:") for scenario, details in failover_scenarios.items(): print(f" {scenario}:") print(f" Trigger: {details['trigger']}") print(f" Action: {details['action']}") print(f" Time: {details['time']}") print(f" Data loss: {details['data_loss']}") print(f"") # Cost implications multi_region_costs = { 'igw_hourly': '$0 × 3 regions', 'data_transfer_out': '$0.02/GB (each region)', 'inter_region_replication': '$0.02/GB (DMS)', 'route_53_hosted_zone': '$0.50 per zone', 'rds_multi_region': '3× database costs' } print(f"Multi-Region Cost Structure:") for component, cost in multi_region_costs.items(): print(f" {component}: {cost}") # Disaster recovery metrics dr_metrics = { 'rto_target': '< 5 minutes (automatic)', 'rpo_target': '< 1 minute (live replication)', 'availability_target': '99.99% (4 nines)', 'backup_retention': '30 days (automated)', 'test_frequency': 'Weekly DR drill' } print(f"\nDisaster Recovery Metrics (SLA):") for metric, target in dr_metrics.items(): print(f" {metric}: {target}") # Django application distribution app_distribution = { 'region_1_users': 'North America (us-east-1)', 'region_2_users': 'Europe/MENA (eu-west-1)', 'region_3_users': 'Asia-Pacific (ap-southeast-1)', 'user_base': '~100 concurrent per region', 'latency_target': '< 100ms p99' } print(f"\nDjango Application Distribution:") for setting, value in app_distribution.items(): print(f" {setting}: {value}") print(f"\n✓ Multi-region IGW pool created") print(f"✓ Disaster recovery enabled across 3 regions")
Example 5: Create and Validate IGW Configuration for Compliance

Create an internet gateway with compliance tags and validate configuration for security and regulatory requirements.

#!/bin/bash
# Create compliance-tagged IGW and validate
REGION="us-east-1"
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
COMPLIANCE_IGW_NAME="anihpj-compliance-igw"

echo "Creating compliance-configured Internet Gateway..."
echo ""

# Create IGW with comprehensive compliance tags
IGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-internet-gateway \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=internet-gateway,Tags=[
    {Key=Name,Value=$COMPLIANCE_IGW_NAME},
    {Key=Environment,Value=Production},
    {Key=Compliance,Value=SOC2-Type2},
    {Key=DataClassification,Value=Public},
    {Key=BackupRequired,Value=No},
    {Key=CostCenter,Value=Engineering},
    {Key=Owner,Value=CloudOps},
    {Key=Terraform,Value=True},
    {Key=CreatedDate,Value=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)},
    {Key=Version,Value=1.0}
  ]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InternetGateway.InternetGatewayId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ IGW created: $IGW_ID"
echo ""

# Attach to VPC
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
  --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ IGW attached to VPC: $VPC_ID"
echo ""

# Validate configuration
echo "=== Compliance Validation ==="
echo ""

# Check tags
echo "Tag Audit:"
aws ec2 describe-internet-gateways \
  --internet-gateway-ids $IGW_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InternetGateways[0].Tags' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n'

echo ""
echo "✓ All required compliance tags present"
echo "✓ Attachment verified: $VPC_ID"
echo ""

echo "Compliance Checklist:"
echo "  ✓ IGW created and attached"
echo "  ✓ SOC2 compliance tags applied"
echo "  ✓ Owner assigned (CloudOps)"
echo "  ✓ Cost center tracked (Engineering)"
echo "  ✓ Version controlled (v1.0)"
echo "  ✓ Created date logged"
Creating compliance-configured Internet Gateway... { "InternetGateway": { "InternetGatewayId": "igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h", "Attachments": [{ "State": "attaching", "VpcId": "vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8" }], "Tags": [ { "Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-compliance-igw" }, { "Key": "Compliance", "Value": "SOC2-Type2" }, { "Key": "Owner", "Value": "CloudOps" } ], "OwnerId": "123456789012" } } ✓ IGW created: igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h ✓ IGW attached to VPC: vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 === Compliance Validation === Tag Audit: Name anihpj-compliance-igw Compliance SOC2-Type2 DataClassification Public CostCenter Engineering ✓ All required compliance tags present ✓ Attachment verified
# Create compliance-tagged Internet Gateway from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import json compliance_igw = { 'igw_id': 'igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'compliance_framework': 'SOC2 Type 2', 'environment': 'Production' } print(f"Compliance-Tagged Internet Gateway") print(f"=" * 60) print(f"") # Compliance frameworks compliance_frameworks = { 'soc2': { 'name': 'SOC 2 Type 2', 'scope': 'Security, availability, processing integrity', 'audit_frequency': 'Annual (with quarterly reviews)', 'documentation': 'Architecture diagrams, controls, testing' }, 'gdpr': { 'name': 'GDPR (EU Data Protection)', 'scope': 'User data privacy, data residency', 'audit_frequency': 'Continuous (Data Protection Authority)', 'documentation': 'DPA, data processing agreements' }, 'hipaa': { 'name': 'HIPAA (Healthcare)', 'scope': 'Protected health information (PHI)', 'audit_frequency': 'Biennial', 'documentation': 'BAA, risk analysis, audit logs' } } print(f"Applicable Compliance Frameworks:") for framework, details in compliance_frameworks.items(): print(f" {details['name']}:") print(f" Scope: {details['scope']}") print(f" Audit: {details['audit_frequency']}") print(f"") # Required compliance tags compliance_tags = { 'environment': 'Production', 'owner': 'CloudOps', 'cost_center': 'Engineering', 'data_classification': 'Public (IGW itself)', 'compliance': 'SOC2-Type2', 'backup_required': 'No', 'terraform_managed': 'True', 'created_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'version': '1.0' } print(f"Compliance Tag Inventory:") for tag_name, tag_value in compliance_tags.items(): print(f" {tag_name}: {tag_value}") print(f"") # Audit trail requirements audit_trail = { 'creation_log': 'CloudTrail (who, when, from where)', 'attachment_log': 'Attachment event to VPC', 'modification_log': 'Any tag changes, route updates', 'access_log': 'Who accessed IGW configuration', 'retention_period': '365 days minimum' } print(f"Audit Trail Requirements:") for log_type, requirement in audit_trail.items(): print(f" {log_type}: {requirement}") # Security controls for IGW security_controls = { 'network_acl': 'VPC default (allow all, override with NACL)', 'security_group': 'Applied at instance level, not IGW', 'flow_logs': 'VPC Flow Logs capture IGW traffic', 'logging': 'Enable VPC Flow Logs to CloudWatch Logs/S3', 'monitoring': 'CloudWatch metrics for IGW health' } print(f"\nSecurity Controls:") for control, implementation in security_controls.items(): print(f" {control}: {implementation}") # Data flow documentation data_flow_doc = { 'inbound_traffic': 'Internet clients → ALB (public subnet)', 'outbound_traffic': 'App servers → NAT → IGW → Internet', 'internal_traffic': 'VPC-internal (no IGW involvement)', 'approval_status': 'Reviewed and approved by security team' } print(f"\nData Flow Documentation:") for flow, description in data_flow_doc.items(): print(f" {flow}: {description}") # Compliance sign-off sign_off = { 'reviewed_by': 'Security Team Lead', 'approved_by': 'Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)', 'review_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'next_review': (timezone.now() + timezone.timedelta(days=365)).strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'status': 'APPROVED for production' } print(f"\nCompliance Sign-Off:") for item, value in sign_off.items(): print(f" {item}: {value}") # Risk assessment risk_assessment = { 'igw_single_point': 'LOW (AWS manages redundancy)', 'exposure_risk': 'MEDIUM (must control with SGs)', 'data_exfiltration': 'MEDIUM (outbound NAT required)', 'ddos_exposure': 'MEDIUM (AWS Shield Standard included)', 'compliance_risk': 'LOW (when properly configured)' } print(f"\nRisk Assessment:") for risk, rating in risk_assessment.items(): print(f" {risk}: {rating}") # Remediation and incident response incident_response = { 'breach_notification': '< 1 hour', 'investigation_procedure': 'VPC Flow Logs + CloudTrail analysis', 'notification_stakeholders': 'Security team, CISO, affected users', 'remediation_action': 'Isolate, detach, investigate, restore' } print(f"\nIncident Response Procedures:") for procedure, detail in incident_response.items(): print(f" {procedure}: {detail}") print(f"\n✓ Compliance-tagged IGW created and validated") print(f"✓ Ready for SOC 2 Type 2 audit")

attach-internet-gateway

Attach an internet gateway to a VPC, enabling internet connectivity for resources in public subnets. The IGW must be created first, then attached to make it functional. Only one IGW can be attached to each VPC, and the attachment is instant once completed.

Syntax
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
  --internet-gateway-id  \
  --vpc-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --internet-gateway-id (string): The ID of the internet gateway to attach. Format: igw-xxxxxxxx
  • --vpc-id (string): The ID of the VPC to attach the internet gateway to. Format: vpc-xxxxxxxx
  • --region (string): AWS region where both the IGW and VPC exist (us-east-1, eu-west-1, etc.)
  • --dry-run (boolean): Check if the operation would succeed without actually attaching the IGW
Return Value
  • No return value: Operation returns empty response when successful
  • State change: IGW state changes from "available" to "attached"
  • VPC routing: Enables 0.0.0.0/0 routes in public route tables
  • Instance connectivity: Public subnets can now route traffic to/from internet
Example 1: Attach Production IGW to Enable ALB Internet Access

Attach the internet gateway to production VPC to enable Application Load Balancer to receive internet traffic.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach production IGW to VPC
IGW_ID="igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h"
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Attaching internet gateway to production VPC..."
echo "IGW ID: $IGW_ID"
echo "VPC ID: $VPC_ID"
echo ""

# Attach the internet gateway
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
  --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Internet gateway attached successfully"
echo ""

# Verify attachment
echo "Verifying IGW attachment..."
ATTACHMENT_STATE=$(aws ec2 describe-internet-gateways \
  --internet-gateway-ids $IGW_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InternetGateways[0].Attachments[0].State' \
  --output text)

echo "Attachment state: $ATTACHMENT_STATE"
echo ""

if [ "$ATTACHMENT_STATE" = "attached" ]; then
    echo "✓ IGW successfully attached to VPC"
    echo "✓ Public subnets now have internet access"
    echo "✓ ALB can receive external traffic"
    echo "✓ NAT Gateways can route private subnet traffic"
else
    echo "✗ IGW attachment failed"
    exit 1
fi

echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "  1. Configure public route table (0.0.0.0/0 → $IGW_ID)"
echo "  2. Deploy ALB in public subnets"
echo "  3. Deploy NAT Gateways for private subnet outbound"
Attaching internet gateway to production VPC... IGW ID: igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h VPC ID: vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8 ✓ Internet gateway attached successfully Verifying IGW attachment... Attachment state: attached ✓ IGW successfully attached to VPC ✓ Public subnets now have internet access ✓ ALB can receive external traffic ✓ NAT Gateways can route private subnet traffic Next steps: 1. Configure public route table (0.0.0.0/0 → igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h) 2. Deploy ALB in public subnets 3. Deploy NAT Gateways for private subnet outbound
# Attach production IGW to VPC from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone igw_attachment = { 'igw_id': 'igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'state': 'attached', 'environment': 'Production' } print(f"Production Internet Gateway Attachment") print(f"IGW ID: {igw_attachment['igw_id']}") print(f"VPC ID: {igw_attachment['vpc_id']}") print(f"State: {igw_attachment['state']}") print(f"") # Network topology after attachment network_topology = { 'vpc_cidr': '10.0.0.0/16', 'public_subnets': [ {'name': 'public-web-1a', 'cidr': '10.0.1.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1a'}, {'name': 'public-web-1b', 'cidr': '10.0.2.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1b'} ], 'private_subnets': [ {'name': 'private-app-1a', 'cidr': '10.0.10.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1a'}, {'name': 'private-app-1b', 'cidr': '10.0.11.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1b'}, {'name': 'private-db-1a', 'cidr': '10.0.20.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1a'}, {'name': 'private-db-1b', 'cidr': '10.0.21.0/24', 'az': 'us-east-1b'} ] } print(f"Network Topology (Post-Attachment):") print(f" VPC CIDR: {network_topology['vpc_cidr']}") print(f" Public Subnets: {len(network_topology['public_subnets'])} (internet access via IGW)") print(f" Private Subnets: {len(network_topology['private_subnets'])} (internet access via NAT)") print(f"") # Traffic flow after attachment traffic_flows = { 'inbound_internet': 'Internet → IGW → ALB (public subnet) → Django (private subnet)', 'outbound_private': 'Django (private) → NAT GW (public) → IGW → Internet', 'outbound_public': 'ALB (public) → IGW → Internet (health checks)', 'internal_vpc': 'Direct routing (no IGW involvement)' } print(f"Traffic Flows (IGW Enabled):") for flow_type, path in traffic_flows.items(): print(f" {flow_type}: {path}") print(f"") # Route table configuration required route_tables = { 'public_rt': { 'name': 'anihpj-public-rt', 'routes': [ {'dest': '10.0.0.0/16', 'target': 'local'}, {'dest': '0.0.0.0/0', 'target': igw_attachment['igw_id']} ] }, 'private_rt': { 'name': 'anihpj-private-rt', 'routes': [ {'dest': '10.0.0.0/16', 'target': 'local'}, {'dest': '0.0.0.0/0', 'target': 'nat-gateway-id'} ] } } print(f"Required Route Table Configuration:") for rt_name, config in route_tables.items(): print(f" {config['name']}:") for route in config['routes']: print(f" {route['dest']} → {route['target']}") print(f"") # ALB configuration ready alb_config = { 'name': 'anihpj-prod-alb', 'scheme': 'internet-facing', 'type': 'application', 'ip_address_type': 'ipv4', 'subnets': network_topology['public_subnets'], 'security_group': 'sg-alb-web', 'listeners': [ {'port': 80, 'protocol': 'HTTP', 'action': 'redirect to HTTPS'}, {'port': 443, 'protocol': 'HTTPS', 'action': 'forward to target group'} ] } print(f"ALB Configuration (IGW Required):") print(f" Name: {alb_config['name']}") print(f" Scheme: {alb_config['scheme']}") print(f" Subnets: {len(alb_config['subnets'])} public subnets") for listener in alb_config['listeners']: print(f" Listener {listener['port']}: {listener['action']}") print(f"") # NAT Gateway placement nat_gateway_setup = { 'nat_1a': { 'subnet': 'public-web-1a (10.0.1.0/24)', 'eip': 'Required (allocate-address)', 'serves': 'private-app-1a, private-db-1a' }, 'nat_1b': { 'subnet': 'public-web-1b (10.0.2.0/24)', 'eip': 'Required (allocate-address)', 'serves': 'private-app-1b, private-db-1b' } } print(f"NAT Gateway Setup (IGW Dependency):") for nat_name, config in nat_gateway_setup.items(): print(f" {nat_name}:") print(f" Subnet: {config['subnet']}") print(f" Serves: {config['serves']}") # Django deployment readiness django_readiness = { 'database_access': 'Private subnet → RDS (internal, no internet)', 'api_integrations': 'Private subnet → NAT → IGW → External APIs', 'user_traffic': 'Internet → IGW → ALB → Django', 'admin_access': 'VPN → Private subnet (no public IPs)', 'monitoring': 'CloudWatch via VPC endpoints (no internet)' } print(f"\nDjango Deployment Readiness:") for aspect, config in django_readiness.items(): print(f" {aspect}: {config}") # Security implications security_implications = { 'public_subnet_exposure': 'Only ALB and NAT Gateways (no Django instances)', 'private_subnet_protection': 'No direct internet (controlled via NAT)', 'inbound_filtering': 'Security groups + NACLs + ALB rules', 'outbound_monitoring': 'VPC Flow Logs + NAT Gateway logs', 'ddos_protection': 'AWS Shield Standard (included with ALB)' } print(f"\nSecurity Implications:") for aspect, implementation in security_implications.items(): print(f" {aspect}: {implementation}") print(f"\n✓ IGW attached to production VPC") print(f"✓ Ready for ALB and NAT Gateway deployment")
Example 2: Attach IGW to Staging VPC with Route Table Update

Attach internet gateway to staging environment and immediately update route table for end-to-end connectivity testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach IGW and configure routing for staging
IGW_ID="igw-1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i"
VPC_ID="vpc-1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Setting up staging VPC internet connectivity..."
echo ""

# Attach internet gateway
echo "Step 1: Attaching IGW to staging VPC"
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
  --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ IGW attached"
echo ""

# Find the main route table
echo "Step 2: Finding main route table"
ROUTE_TABLE_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-route-tables \
  --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=$VPC_ID" "Name=association.main,Values=true" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'RouteTables[0].RouteTableId' \
  --output text)

echo "Main route table: $ROUTE_TABLE_ID"
echo ""

# Add internet route
echo "Step 3: Adding internet route (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW)"
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id $ROUTE_TABLE_ID \
  --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --gateway-id $IGW_ID \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Internet route added"
echo ""

# Verify connectivity
echo "Step 4: Testing connectivity configuration"
echo "  ✓ IGW attached to VPC"
echo "  ✓ Route table configured for internet access"
echo "  ✓ All subnets now have internet routing"
echo ""

echo "Staging VPC ready for testing:"
echo "  - Deploy test instances"
echo "  - Run integration tests"
echo "  - Validate external API connections"
Setting up staging VPC internet connectivity... Step 1: Attaching IGW to staging VPC ✓ IGW attached Step 2: Finding main route table Main route table: rtb-0123456789abcdef0 Step 3: Adding internet route (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW) ✓ Internet route added Step 4: Testing connectivity configuration ✓ IGW attached to VPC ✓ Route table configured for internet access ✓ All subnets now have internet routing Staging VPC ready for testing: - Deploy test instances - Run integration tests - Validate external API connections
# Attach IGW to staging with routing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone staging_attachment = { 'igw_id': 'igw-1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9', 'route_table_id': 'rtb-0123456789abcdef0', 'environment': 'Staging' } print(f"Staging IGW Attachment with Routing") print(f"IGW ID: {staging_attachment['igw_id']}") print(f"VPC ID: {staging_attachment['vpc_id']}") print(f"Route Table: {staging_attachment['route_table_id']}") print(f"") # Staging testing capabilities enabled testing_capabilities = { 'external_api_testing': { 'payment_gateway': 'Stripe test mode connectivity', 'email_service': 'SendGrid sandbox API calls', 'cdn_service': 'CloudFront distribution testing', 'analytics': 'Google Analytics test property' }, 'load_testing': { 'tools': ['Artillery.js', 'JMeter', 'Locust'], 'targets': 'Public ALB endpoints', 'metrics': 'Response time, throughput, error rate' }, 'security_testing': { 'vulnerability_scans': 'OWASP ZAP, Nessus', 'penetration_testing': 'External security firm access', 'compliance_checks': 'Automated security scanning' } } print(f"Testing Capabilities Enabled:") for category, details in testing_capabilities.items(): print(f" {category.replace('_', ' ').title()}:") if isinstance(details, dict): for test_type, description in details.items(): print(f" {test_type}: {description}") elif isinstance(details, list): print(f" Tools: {', '.join(details)}") print(f"") # Route table configuration route_config = { 'route_1': { 'destination': '10.0.0.0/20', # Staging VPC CIDR 'target': 'local', 'purpose': 'Internal VPC communication' }, 'route_2': { 'destination': '0.0.0.0/0', # All internet traffic 'target': staging_attachment['igw_id'], 'purpose': 'Internet access for all subnets' } } print(f"Route Table Configuration:") for route_name, config in route_config.items(): print(f" {config['destination']} → {config['target']}") print(f" Purpose: {config['purpose']}") print(f"") # Django testing scenarios django_testing = { 'api_endpoints': { 'job_posts': '/api/v1/jobs/ (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)', 'user_auth': '/api/v1/auth/ (login, logout, refresh)', 'search': '/api/v1/search/ (skills, location, salary)', 'notifications': '/api/v1/notifications/ (real-time)' }, 'external_integrations': { 'stripe_payments': 'Job posting fees, premium subscriptions', 'sendgrid_emails': 'Welcome emails, job alerts, notifications', 'github_oauth': 'Developer authentication flow', 'linkedin_api': 'Profile import, job sharing' }, 'performance_testing': { 'concurrent_users': 100, 'test_duration': '30 minutes', 'target_response': '< 200ms p95', 'error_threshold': '< 1%' } } print(f"Django Testing Scenarios:") for category, tests in django_testing.items(): print(f" {category.replace('_', ' ').title()}:") for test, details in tests.items(): print(f" {test}: {details}") print(f"") # CI/CD integration cicd_integration = { 'trigger': 'Git push to staging branch', 'build_time': '~5 minutes (Docker image)', 'deploy_time': '~3 minutes (ECS rolling update)', 'test_execution': '~15 minutes (full test suite)', 'rollback_time': '~1 minute (previous image)', 'notification': 'Slack + email on completion' } print(f"CI/CD Integration:") for step, timing in cicd_integration.items(): print(f" {step}: {timing}") # Monitoring and logging monitoring = { 'alb_metrics': 'Request count, response time, error rate', 'ecs_metrics': 'CPU, memory, task health', 'rds_metrics': 'Connections, query time, storage', 'application_logs': 'CloudWatch Logs (Django logs)', 'vpc_flow_logs': 'Network traffic analysis' } print(f"\nMonitoring & Logging:") for metric_type, description in monitoring.items(): print(f" {metric_type}: {description}") # Cost optimization for staging cost_optimization = { 'instance_types': 't3.small (vs t3.large production)', 'auto_scaling': 'Disabled (fixed 2 instances)', 'database': 'Single AZ RDS (vs Multi-AZ)', 'backup_retention': '7 days (vs 30 days prod)', 'scheduled_shutdown': 'Off 8pm-8am weekdays, weekends' } print(f"\nCost Optimization:") for strategy, implementation in cost_optimization.items(): print(f" {strategy}: {implementation}") print(f"\n✓ Staging VPC internet connectivity configured") print(f"✓ Ready for comprehensive testing")
Example 3: Conditional IGW Attachment with Error Handling

Attach IGW with proper error handling and validation checks to prevent common attachment issues.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe IGW attachment with error handling
IGW_ID="igw-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j"
VPC_ID="vpc-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j0"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Safe IGW attachment with validation..."
echo ""

# Function to check if IGW exists
check_igw_exists() {
    aws ec2 describe-internet-gateways \
        --internet-gateway-ids $IGW_ID \
        --region $REGION \
        --query 'InternetGateways[0].InternetGatewayId' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null
}

# Function to check if VPC exists
check_vpc_exists() {
    aws ec2 describe-vpcs \
        --vpc-ids $VPC_ID \
        --region $REGION \
        --query 'Vpcs[0].VpcId' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null
}

# Function to check if IGW is already attached
check_attachment() {
    aws ec2 describe-internet-gateways \
        --internet-gateway-ids $IGW_ID \
        --region $REGION \
        --query 'InternetGateways[0].Attachments[0].VpcId' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null
}

# Pre-flight checks
echo "Pre-flight checks:"

if [ "$(check_igw_exists)" != "$IGW_ID" ]; then
    echo "✗ IGW $IGW_ID does not exist"
    exit 1
fi
echo "✓ IGW exists"

if [ "$(check_vpc_exists)" != "$VPC_ID" ]; then
    echo "✗ VPC $VPC_ID does not exist"
    exit 1
fi
echo "✓ VPC exists"

EXISTING_VPC=$(check_attachment)
if [ "$EXISTING_VPC" != "None" ] && [ "$EXISTING_VPC" != "" ]; then
    if [ "$EXISTING_VPC" = "$VPC_ID" ]; then
        echo "ℹ IGW already attached to this VPC"
        echo "✓ No action needed"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "✗ IGW already attached to different VPC: $EXISTING_VPC"
        echo "Detach first: aws ec2 detach-internet-gateway --igw-id $IGW_ID --vpc-id $EXISTING_VPC"
        exit 1
    fi
fi
echo "✓ IGW is available for attachment"

echo ""
echo "Attaching IGW to VPC..."
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
    --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID \
    --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
    --region $REGION

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ IGW successfully attached"
else
    echo "✗ IGW attachment failed"
    exit 1
fi

echo ""
echo "Post-attachment verification:"
ATTACHED_VPC=$(check_attachment)
if [ "$ATTACHED_VPC" = "$VPC_ID" ]; then
    echo "✓ Attachment verified"
    echo "✓ IGW $IGW_ID is now attached to VPC $VPC_ID"
else
    echo "✗ Attachment verification failed"
    exit 1
fi
Safe IGW attachment with validation... Pre-flight checks: ✓ IGW exists ✓ VPC exists ✓ IGW is available for attachment Attaching IGW to VPC... ✓ IGW successfully attached Post-attachment verification: ✓ Attachment verified ✓ IGW igw-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j is now attached to VPC vpc-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j0
# Safe IGW attachment with error handling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone safe_attachment = { 'igw_id': 'igw-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-2c3d4e5f6g7h8i9j0', 'validation_passed': True, 'attachment_successful': True } print(f"Safe IGW Attachment Process") print(f"IGW ID: {safe_attachment['igw_id']}") print(f"VPC ID: {safe_attachment['vpc_id']}") print(f"Validation: {'✓ Passed' if safe_attachment['validation_passed'] else '✗ Failed'}") print(f"Attachment: {'✓ Successful' if safe_attachment['attachment_successful'] else '✗ Failed'}") print(f"") # Pre-flight validation checks validation_checks = { 'igw_existence': 'Verify IGW exists in specified region', 'vpc_existence': 'Verify VPC exists in specified region', 'igw_availability': 'Check IGW is not attached to another VPC', 'vpc_availability': 'Check VPC does not have IGW already', 'permissions': 'Verify EC2 attach permissions', 'region_consistency': 'Ensure IGW and VPC in same region' } print(f"Pre-flight Validation Checks:") for check, description in validation_checks.items(): print(f" {check}: {description}") print(f"") # Common attachment errors and solutions common_errors = { 'InvalidInternetGatewayId.NotFound': { 'cause': 'IGW does not exist in specified region', 'solution': 'Verify IGW ID and region', 'prevention': 'Use describe-internet-gateways first' }, 'InvalidVpcId.NotFound': { 'cause': 'VPC does not exist in specified region', 'solution': 'Verify VPC ID and region', 'prevention': 'Use describe-vpcs first' }, 'Gateway.AlreadyAttached': { 'cause': 'IGW already attached to another VPC', 'solution': 'Detach from current VPC first', 'prevention': 'Check attachment state before attaching' }, 'VPC.AlreadyHasGateway': { 'cause': 'VPC already has an IGW attached', 'solution': 'One IGW per VPC limit', 'prevention': 'Check VPC IGW attachment status' }, 'UnauthorizedOperation': { 'cause': 'Insufficient IAM permissions', 'solution': 'Add ec2:AttachInternetGateway permission', 'prevention': 'Use proper IAM role with EC2 permissions' } } print(f"Common Attachment Errors & Solutions:") for error_code, details in common_errors.items(): print(f" {error_code}:") print(f" Cause: {details['cause']}") print(f" Solution: {details['solution']}") print(f"") # IAM permissions required iam_permissions = { 'ec2:AttachInternetGateway': 'Required for attachment operation', 'ec2:DescribeInternetGateways': 'Pre-flight validation', 'ec2:DescribeVpcs': 'VPC existence validation', 'ec2:DescribeRouteTables': 'Route configuration (optional)' } print(f"Required IAM Permissions:") for permission, purpose in iam_permissions.items(): print(f" {permission}: {purpose}") # Automation best practices automation_practices = { 'idempotency': 'Check existing state before making changes', 'error_handling': 'Graceful failure with meaningful error messages', 'validation': 'Pre-flight checks before resource operations', 'logging': 'Comprehensive operation logging for debugging', 'rollback': 'Automatic rollback on failure (if applicable)', 'testing': 'Unit and integration tests for automation scripts' } print(f"\nAutomation Best Practices:") for practice, description in automation_practices.items(): print(f" {practice}: {description}") # Post-attachment validation post_validation = { 'attachment_state': 'Verify state is "attached"', 'vpc_association': 'Confirm correct VPC association', 'route_table_ready': 'Check route tables can reference IGW', 'connectivity_test': 'Test basic internet connectivity', 'monitoring_setup': 'Enable CloudWatch metrics' } print(f"\nPost-Attachment Validation:") for validation, description in post_validation.items(): print(f" {validation}: {description}") # Django deployment readiness checklist deployment_checklist = { 'network_layer': '✓ IGW attached, routes configured', 'security_layer': 'Security groups and NACLs configured', 'compute_layer': 'EC2 instances or ECS tasks ready', 'database_layer': 'RDS in private subnets', 'load_balancer': 'ALB in public subnets with IGW access', 'monitoring': 'CloudWatch, logging configured' } print(f"\nDjango Deployment Readiness:") for layer, status in deployment_checklist.items(): print(f" {layer}: {status}") print(f"\n✓ Safe IGW attachment completed") print(f"✓ All validation checks passed")
Example 4: Bulk IGW Attachment for Multi-Environment Setup

Attach multiple internet gateways to their respective VPCs for a multi-environment deployment (dev, staging, prod).

#!/bin/bash
# Bulk IGW attachment for multi-environment
REGION="us-east-1"

declare -A environments=(
    ["dev"]="igw-dev123:vpc-dev456"
    ["staging"]="igw-stg123:vpc-stg456"  
    ["prod"]="igw-prd123:vpc-prd456"
)

echo "Bulk IGW attachment for multi-environment setup..."
echo ""

for env in "${!environments[@]}"; do
    IFS=':' read -r igw_id vpc_id <<< "${environments[$env]}"
    
    echo "Environment: $env"
    echo "  IGW: $igw_id"
    echo "  VPC: $vpc_id"
    
    # Attach IGW to VPC
    aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
        --internet-gateway-id $igw_id \
        --vpc-id $vpc_id \
        --region $REGION
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "  ✓ Attached successfully"
        
        # Tag the attachment for tracking
        aws ec2 create-tags \
            --resources $igw_id \
            --tags Key=AttachedTo,Value=$vpc_id Key=Environment,Value=$env \
            --region $REGION
        
        echo "  ✓ Tags updated"
    else
        echo "  ✗ Attachment failed"
        continue
    fi
    
    echo ""
done

echo "Multi-environment IGW attachment summary:"
echo "  ✓ Development VPC ready for feature testing"
echo "  ✓ Staging VPC ready for QA validation" 
echo "  ✓ Production VPC ready for live traffic"
echo ""

echo "Next steps per environment:"
echo "  Dev: Configure development route tables"
echo "  Staging: Set up load testing infrastructure"
echo "  Prod: Deploy production ALB and NAT Gateways"
Bulk IGW attachment for multi-environment setup... Environment: dev IGW: igw-dev123 VPC: vpc-dev456 ✓ Attached successfully ✓ Tags updated Environment: staging IGW: igw-stg123 VPC: vpc-stg456 ✓ Attached successfully ✓ Tags updated Environment: prod IGW: igw-prd123 VPC: vpc-prd456 ✓ Attached successfully ✓ Tags updated Multi-environment IGW attachment summary: ✓ Development VPC ready for feature testing ✓ Staging VPC ready for QA validation ✓ Production VPC ready for live traffic
# Bulk IGW attachment for multi-environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone multi_env_attachments = { 'development': { 'igw_id': 'igw-dev123', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-dev456', 'purpose': 'Feature development and testing' }, 'staging': { 'igw_id': 'igw-stg123', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-stg456', 'purpose': 'QA validation and load testing' }, 'production': { 'igw_id': 'igw-prd123', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-prd456', 'purpose': 'Live user traffic' } } print(f"Multi-Environment IGW Attachment") print(f"=" * 50) print(f"") for env_name, config in multi_env_attachments.items(): print(f"{env_name.upper()}:") print(f" IGW: {config['igw_id']}") print(f" VPC: {config['vpc_id']}") print(f" Purpose: {config['purpose']}") print(f"") # Environment-specific configurations env_configurations = { 'development': { 'instance_count': 2, 'instance_type': 't3.micro', 'database': 'Single AZ RDS t3.micro', 'load_balancer': 'None (direct access)', 'auto_scaling': False, 'cost_per_month': '$25' }, 'staging': { 'instance_count': 3, 'instance_type': 't3.small', 'database': 'Single AZ RDS t3.small', 'load_balancer': 'ALB (1 AZ)', 'auto_scaling': False, 'cost_per_month': '$85' }, 'production': { 'instance_count': 6, 'instance_type': 't3.large', 'database': 'Multi-AZ RDS r6i.xlarge', 'load_balancer': 'ALB (Multi-AZ)', 'auto_scaling': True, 'cost_per_month': '$450' } } print(f"Environment Configurations:") for env, config in env_configurations.items(): print(f" {env.upper()}:") for setting, value in config.items(): print(f" {setting}: {value}") print(f"") # Traffic patterns by environment traffic_patterns = { 'development': { 'concurrent_users': 5, 'requests_per_hour': 100, 'peak_hours': 'Business hours only', 'geographic_distribution': 'Local office only' }, 'staging': { 'concurrent_users': 20, 'requests_per_hour': 500, 'peak_hours': 'Testing schedules', 'geographic_distribution': 'QA team locations' }, 'production': { 'concurrent_users': 500, 'requests_per_hour': 10000, 'peak_hours': '9am-6pm EST weekdays', 'geographic_distribution': 'Global user base' } } print(f"Traffic Patterns:") for env, pattern in traffic_patterns.items(): print(f" {env.upper()}:") for metric, value in pattern.items(): print(f" {metric}: {value}") print(f"") # CI/CD pipeline integration pipeline_integration = { 'development': { 'deployment_trigger': 'Git push to feature branch', 'deployment_frequency': '10-20x per day', 'rollback_capability': 'Manual', 'testing_scope': 'Unit tests only' }, 'staging': { 'deployment_trigger': 'Merge to staging branch', 'deployment_frequency': '2-3x per day', 'rollback_capability': 'Automatic on failure', 'testing_scope': 'Integration + E2E tests' }, 'production': { 'deployment_trigger': 'Manual release approval', 'deployment_frequency': '1x per week', 'rollback_capability': 'Blue/green deployment', 'testing_scope': 'Full regression suite' } } print(f"CI/CD Pipeline Integration:") for env, config in pipeline_integration.items(): print(f" {env.upper()}:") for aspect, details in config.items(): print(f" {aspect}: {details}") print(f"") # Django settings per environment django_settings = { 'development': { 'debug': True, 'allowed_hosts': ['dev.anihpj.internal'], 'database_pool': 5, 'cache_backend': 'redis://dev-redis:6379/0', 'log_level': 'DEBUG' }, 'staging': { 'debug': False, 'allowed_hosts': ['staging.anihpj.com'], 'database_pool': 10, 'cache_backend': 'redis://staging-redis:6379/0', 'log_level': 'INFO' }, 'production': { 'debug': False, 'allowed_hosts': ['anihpj.com', 'www.anihpj.com'], 'database_pool': 25, 'cache_backend': 'redis://prod-cluster:6379/0', 'log_level': 'WARNING' } } print(f"Django Settings by Environment:") for env, settings in django_settings.items(): print(f" {env.upper()}:") for setting, value in settings.items(): print(f" {setting}: {value}") print(f"") # Security configurations security_configs = { 'development': { 'security_groups': 'Open for development team', 'ssl_certificate': 'Self-signed', 'waf': 'Disabled', 'backup_frequency': 'Weekly' }, 'staging': { 'security_groups': 'Restricted to QA team', 'ssl_certificate': 'Let\'s Encrypt', 'waf': 'Basic rules', 'backup_frequency': 'Daily' }, 'production': { 'security_groups': 'Least privilege access', 'ssl_certificate': 'AWS Certificate Manager', 'waf': 'Full protection + rate limiting', 'backup_frequency': 'Every 6 hours' } } print(f"Security Configurations:") for env, config in security_configs.items(): print(f" {env.upper()}:") for setting, value in config.items(): print(f" {setting}: {value}") print(f"\n✓ All environments configured with IGW access") print(f"✓ Ready for multi-environment Django deployment")
Example 5: IGW Attachment with Automatic Route Table Configuration

Attach internet gateway and automatically configure public route tables for immediate internet connectivity.

#!/bin/bash
# IGW attachment with automatic route configuration
IGW_ID="igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h"
VPC_ID="vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "IGW attachment with automatic route table configuration..."
echo ""

# Step 1: Attach Internet Gateway
echo "Step 1: Attaching Internet Gateway"
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway \
    --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID \
    --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
    --region $REGION

echo "✓ IGW attached to VPC"
echo ""

# Step 2: Create dedicated public route table
echo "Step 2: Creating public route table"
PUBLIC_RT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-route-table \
    --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
    --tag-specifications "ResourceType=route-table,Tags=[
        {Key=Name,Value=anihpj-public-rt},
        {Key=Type,Value=Public}
    ]" \
    --region $REGION \
    --query 'RouteTable.RouteTableId' \
    --output text)

echo "✓ Public route table created: $PUBLIC_RT_ID"
echo ""

# Step 3: Add internet route to public route table
echo "Step 3: Adding internet route (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW)"
aws ec2 create-route \
    --route-table-id $PUBLIC_RT_ID \
    --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 \
    --gateway-id $IGW_ID \
    --region $REGION

echo "✓ Internet route added"
echo ""

# Step 4: Associate public subnets with public route table
echo "Step 4: Associating public subnets with route table"
PUBLIC_SUBNETS=$(aws ec2 describe-subnets \
    --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=$VPC_ID" "Name=tag:Type,Values=Public" \
    --region $REGION \
    --query 'Subnets[].SubnetId' \
    --output text)

for subnet_id in $PUBLIC_SUBNETS; do
    aws ec2 associate-route-table \
        --route-table-id $PUBLIC_RT_ID \
        --subnet-id $subnet_id \
        --region $REGION
    echo "  ✓ Associated subnet: $subnet_id"
done

echo ""
echo "Configuration complete:"
echo "  ✓ IGW attached and functional"
echo "  ✓ Public route table configured"
echo "  ✓ Internet route active (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW)"
echo "  ✓ Public subnets have internet connectivity"
echo ""

# Step 5: Verify configuration
echo "Step 5: Verifying configuration"
aws ec2 describe-route-tables \
    --route-table-ids $PUBLIC_RT_ID \
    --region $REGION \
    --query 'RouteTables[0].Routes' \
    --output table

echo ""
echo "✓ Internet connectivity ready for:"
echo "  - Application Load Balancer deployment" 
echo "  - NAT Gateway creation"
echo "  - Public instance access"
IGW attachment with automatic route table configuration... Step 1: Attaching Internet Gateway ✓ IGW attached to VPC Step 2: Creating public route table ✓ Public route table created: rtb-0123456789abcdef Step 3: Adding internet route (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW) ✓ Internet route added Step 4: Associating public subnets with route table ✓ Associated subnet: subnet-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h ✓ Associated subnet: subnet-0b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i Configuration complete: ✓ IGW attached and functional ✓ Public route table configured ✓ Internet route active (0.0.0.0/0 → IGW) ✓ Public subnets have internet connectivity Step 5: Verifying configuration ---------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeRouteTables | +-----------------+----------------+--------------------+ |DestinationCidrBlock|GatewayId |State | +-----------------+----------------+--------------------+ |10.0.0.0/16 |local |active | |0.0.0.0/0 |igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h|active | +-----------------+----------------+--------------------+
# IGW attachment with automatic route configuration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone complete_setup = { 'igw_id': 'igw-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8', 'public_rt_id': 'rtb-0123456789abcdef', 'public_subnets': ['subnet-0a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h', 'subnet-0b2c3d4e5f6g7h8i'] } print(f"Complete IGW Setup with Route Configuration") print(f"=" * 60) print(f"") print(f"Network Configuration:") print(f" IGW: {complete_setup['igw_id']}") print(f" VPC: {complete_setup['vpc_id']}") print(f" Public Route Table: {complete_setup['public_rt_id']}") print(f" Public Subnets: {len(complete_setup['public_subnets'])}") print(f"") # Route table configuration details route_config = { 'local_route': { 'destination': '10.0.0.0/16', 'target': 'local', 'purpose': 'Internal VPC communication', 'status': 'Active' }, 'internet_route': { 'destination': '0.0.0.0/0', 'target': complete_setup['igw_id'], 'purpose': 'Internet access for public subnets', 'status': 'Active' } } print(f"Route Configuration:") for route_name, config in route_config.items(): print(f" {config['destination']} → {config['target']}") print(f" Purpose: {config['purpose']}") print(f" Status: {config['status']}") print(f"") # Public subnet capabilities public_subnet_capabilities = { 'alb_deployment': { 'enabled': True, 'description': 'Application Load Balancer can receive internet traffic', 'next_step': 'Create ALB with public subnets' }, 'nat_gateway': { 'enabled': True, 'description': 'NAT Gateway can route private subnet traffic to internet', 'next_step': 'Deploy NAT Gateway in public subnet' }, 'bastion_host': { 'enabled': True, 'description': 'Bastion host can provide SSH access to private instances', 'next_step': 'Launch EC2 instance with public IP' }, 'public_instances': { 'enabled': True, 'description': 'EC2 instances with public IPs have internet access', 'next_step': 'Configure security groups appropriately' } } print(f"Public Subnet Capabilities:") for capability, details in public_subnet_capabilities.items(): print(f" {capability.replace('_', ' ').title()}:") print(f" Status: {'✓ Enabled' if details['enabled'] else '✗ Disabled'}") print(f" Description: {details['description']}") print(f" Next step: {details['next_step']}") print(f"") # ALB deployment readiness alb_readiness = { 'network_requirements': { 'public_subnets': '✓ 2 subnets across 2 AZs', 'internet_gateway': '✓ IGW attached with routes', 'route_table': '✓ 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW configured', 'subnet_association': '✓ Public subnets associated' }, 'security_requirements': { 'security_group': 'Create ALB security group (80, 443 inbound)', 'target_group': 'Create target group for Django instances', 'ssl_certificate': 'Request/import SSL certificate', 'waf_configuration': 'Optional WAF for additional protection' } } print(f"ALB Deployment Readiness:") for category, requirements in alb_readiness.items(): print(f" {category.replace('_', ' ').title()}:") for requirement, status in requirements.items(): print(f" {requirement}: {status}") print(f"") # Django application impact django_impact = { 'user_traffic_flow': 'Internet → ALB (public) → Django (private) → Database (private)', 'static_files': 'Can serve from S3/CloudFront or ALB', 'api_endpoints': 'Accessible via ALB public endpoints', 'websockets': 'ALB supports WebSocket connections', 'health_checks': 'ALB can perform health checks on Django' } print(f"Django Application Impact:") for aspect, description in django_impact.items(): print(f" {aspect}: {description}") # Monitoring and observability monitoring_setup = { 'vpc_flow_logs': 'Enable VPC Flow Logs for network monitoring', 'alb_access_logs': 'Configure ALB access logs to S3', 'cloudwatch_metrics': 'IGW and ALB metrics in CloudWatch', 'route_table_monitoring': 'Monitor route table changes', 'security_monitoring': 'VPC security group and NACL monitoring' } print(f"\nMonitoring Setup:") for monitoring_type, description in monitoring_setup.items(): print(f" {monitoring_type}: {description}") # Cost implications cost_implications = { 'igw_cost': '$0.00 per hour (no charge)', 'route_table_cost': '$0.00 per hour (no charge)', 'data_transfer_out': '$0.02 per GB (after 1GB free tier)', 'nat_gateway_cost': '$0.045 per hour + $0.045 per GB processed', 'alb_cost': '$0.0225 per hour + $0.008 per LCU' } print(f"\nCost Implications:") for cost_item, amount in cost_implications.items(): print(f" {cost_item}: {amount}") # Security best practices security_practices = { 'public_subnet_principle': 'Only place load balancers and NAT gateways', 'no_direct_instance_access': 'Use bastion hosts or Systems Manager', 'security_group_restrictions': 'Least privilege access rules', 'nacl_configuration': 'Additional subnet-level filtering if needed', 'flow_logs': 'Enable for traffic analysis and security monitoring' } print(f"\nSecurity Best Practices:") for practice, description in security_practices.items(): print(f" {practice}: {description}") print(f"\n✓ Complete IGW setup with automatic routing") print(f"✓ Ready for ALB and Django application deployment")

create-key-pair

Creates an EC2 key pair for SSH access to instances. The private key is required to connect to your Django servers.

Full documentation coming in next step...

import-key-pair

Imports an existing public key to create a key pair in AWS. Use your own SSH keys for instance access.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-key-pairs

Lists all EC2 key pairs in your account. Shows key fingerprints and creation dates.

Full documentation coming in next step...

allocate-address

Allocates an Elastic IP address (static public IP) for your Django application instances.

Full documentation coming in next step...

associate-address

Associates an Elastic IP address with an EC2 instance, giving it a static public IP address.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-addresses

Retrieves information about Elastic IP addresses including which instances they're associated with.

Full documentation coming in next step...

release-address

Releases an Elastic IP address, making it available for reallocation and stopping charges if not in use.

Full documentation coming in next step...

create-volume

Creates an EBS volume (block storage) for persistent data storage with your Django application instances.

Full documentation coming in next step...

attach-volume

Attaches an EBS volume to an EC2 instance, making the storage available for use by your application.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-volumes

Retrieves information about EBS volumes including size, type, status, and attachment information.

Full documentation coming in next step...

create-snapshot

Creates a snapshot (backup) of an EBS volume. Snapshots can be used to create new volumes or restore data.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-snapshots

Retrieves information about EBS snapshots including size, creation time, and status.

Full documentation coming in next step...

modify-instance-attribute

Modifies attributes of a running or stopped EC2 instance such as security groups, IAM roles, or source/dest check.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-instance-status

Retrieves status information about EC2 instances including system and instance status checks.

Full documentation coming in next step...

create-image

Creates an AMI (Amazon Machine Image) from an EC2 instance. Used to capture Django application configuration for reuse.

Full documentation coming in next step...

describe-images

Retrieves information about available AMIs (Amazon Machine Images) including publicly available and custom images.

Full documentation coming in next step...

💾 Storage Services

AWS Storage services provide scalable and durable storage for your Django application data. This section covers S3 for object storage, EFS for shared file systems, and EBS for block storage volumes. Learn how to store static files, media uploads, and database backups for your jobpost application.

🛠️ Developer Tools

AWS Developer Tools provide comprehensive CI/CD capabilities for automating your Django application deployment pipeline. This section covers CodePipeline for orchestration, CodeBuild for compilation, CodeDeploy for deployment, and CodeCommit for source control.

⚙️ Management & Governance

AWS Management services help you monitor, manage, and govern your infrastructure. This section covers CloudWatch for monitoring, CloudFormation for infrastructure as code, Systems Manager for operational management, and CloudTrail for auditing.

AWS CloudWatch

Monitoring and observability service for your Django application infrastructure and custom metrics

📋 CloudWatch Overview

Amazon CloudWatch provides monitoring, logging, and alerting for AWS resources and applications. Use it to track custom metrics from your anihpj Django jobpost application, set alarms for critical conditions, visualize performance data in dashboards, and gain operational insights.

Core CloudWatch Operations for Django Monitoring

put-metric-data - Send custom metrics
get-metric-statistics - Retrieve metric data
get-metric-data - Query metrics with math
list-metrics - List available metrics
put-metric-alarm - Create alarms
put-composite-alarm - Combine alarms
describe-alarms - List alarm status
describe-alarm-history - Audit alarm changes
set-alarm-state - Test alarms manually
enable-alarm-actions - Enable notifications
disable-alarm-actions - Suppress alerts
delete-alarms - Remove alarms
put-dashboard - Create dashboard
get-dashboard - View dashboard
list-dashboards - List dashboards
delete-dashboards - Remove dashboards
put-anomaly-detector - ML anomaly detection
describe-anomaly-detectors - List detectors
put-metric-stream - Stream to Firehose
start-metric-streams - Enable streaming
put-insight-rule - Create insights
get-insight-rule-report - View insights
tag-resource - Add resource tags
untag-resource - Remove tags

put-metric-data

Publishes custom metric data points to CloudWatch. Essential for tracking Django application-specific metrics like job posts created, user signups, API response times, and database query performance.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --value {value} \
  --dimensions Name={dimension-name},Value={dimension-value} \
  --timestamp {timestamp} \
  --unit {unit}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string, required): Custom namespace for your metrics. Example: "AnihpjDjango/JobPost" or "Custom/Application"
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric. Example: "JobPostsCreated", "APIResponseTime", "DatabaseQueryTime"
  • --value (double): Metric value to publish. Example: 1 (for count), 245.5 (for milliseconds)
  • --dimensions (list): Key-value pairs to identify metric variants. Example: Name=Environment,Value=production
  • --timestamp (timestamp): Time the metric was recorded (ISO 8601 format). If omitted, current time is used
  • --unit (string): Unit of measurement. Values: Seconds, Microseconds, Milliseconds, Bytes, Kilobytes, Megabytes, Gigabytes, Count, Percent, etc.
  • --metric-data (list): Send multiple metric data points in a single request (batch publishing)
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP response metadata with RequestId and HTTPStatusCode (200 on success)
  • No explicit response body: Success indicated by HTTP 200 status
5 Detailed CloudWatch put-metric-data Examples
Example 1: Track Job Posts Created in Django Application

Send a custom metric to CloudWatch every time a new job post is created in the anihpj jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Track job posts created metric
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/JobPost"
METRIC_NAME="JobPostsCreated"
TIMESTAMP=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

# Send metric when job post is created
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "$METRIC_NAME" \
  --value 1 \
  --unit Count \
  --timestamp "$TIMESTAMP" \
  --dimensions Name=Environment,Value=production Name=Author,Value=user123 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Job post creation metric sent to CloudWatch"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "HTTPStatusCode": 200, "HTTPHeaders": { "x-amzn-requestid": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "content-type": "text/xml", "content-length": "212", "date": "Fri, 17 Jan 2026 10:30:45 GMT" }, "RetryAttempts": 0 } } ✓ Job post creation metric sent to CloudWatch
# Django integration - Track job post creation metrics from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Skills from django.db.models.signals import post_save from django.dispatch import receiver import boto3 from datetime import datetime # Initialize CloudWatch client cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') @receiver(post_save, sender=JobPost) def track_job_post_creation(sender, instance, created, **kwargs): """Send CloudWatch metric when new job post is created""" if created: try: cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/JobPost', MetricData=[ { 'MetricName': 'JobPostsCreated', 'Value': 1, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'}, {'Name': 'Author', 'Value': str(instance.author.id)}, {'Name': 'Location', 'Value': instance.location.city} ] } ] ) print(f"✓ Metric sent: JobPost {instance.id} created") except Exception as e: print(f"✗ CloudWatch error: {e}") # Example usage job_post = JobPost.objects.create( title="Senior Django Developer", author=Author.objects.first(), location=Location.objects.first(), status='published' ) # Output: ✓ Metric sent: JobPost 1 created
Example 2: Monitor API Response Times

Track Django API endpoint response times to monitor application performance and identify slow queries.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor API response time
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/Performance"
METRIC_NAME="APIResponseTime"
RESPONSE_TIME=245.7  # milliseconds

aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "$METRIC_NAME" \
  --value $RESPONSE_TIME \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --dimensions Name=Endpoint,Value=/api/jobposts Name=Method,Value=GET \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ API response time metric sent: ${RESPONSE_TIME}ms"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ API response time metric sent: 245.7ms
# Django middleware to track API response times from django.utils.deprecation import MiddlewareMixin import boto3 import time from datetime import datetime class CloudWatchMetricsMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin): def __init__(self, get_response): self.get_response = get_response self.cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') def process_request(self, request): request.start_time = time.time() def process_response(self, request, response): if hasattr(request, 'start_time'): response_time = (time.time() - request.start_time) * 1000 # Convert to ms # Send metric to CloudWatch try: self.cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Performance', MetricData=[ { 'MetricName': 'APIResponseTime', 'Value': response_time, 'Unit': 'Milliseconds', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'Endpoint', 'Value': request.path}, {'Name': 'Method', 'Value': request.method}, {'Name': 'StatusCode', 'Value': str(response.status_code)} ] } ] ) except Exception as e: print(f"CloudWatch error: {e}") return response # Add to MIDDLEWARE in settings.py: # 'jobpost.middleware.CloudWatchMetricsMiddleware', # Example output when accessing /api/jobposts: # APIResponseTime: 245.7ms for GET /api/jobposts (Status: 200)
Example 3: Batch Send Multiple Metrics

Send multiple metric data points in a single API call for efficiency - track user activity, database queries, and cache hits.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch send multiple metrics
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/Application"

aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-data \
    MetricName=UserSignups,Value=3,Unit=Count,Timestamp=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),Dimensions=[{Name=Environment,Value=production}] \
    MetricName=DatabaseQueries,Value=145,Unit=Count,Timestamp=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),Dimensions=[{Name=Environment,Value=production},{Name=Type,Value=SELECT}] \
    MetricName=CacheHitRate,Value=87.5,Unit=Percent,Timestamp=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),Dimensions=[{Name=CacheType,Value=Redis}] \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Batch metrics sent successfully"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Batch metrics sent successfully Metrics published: - UserSignups: 3 (Count) - DatabaseQueries: 145 (Count) - CacheHitRate: 87.5%
# Django management command to send batch metrics from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.contrib.auth.models import User from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author from django.db import connection from django.core.cache import cache import boto3 from datetime import datetime class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Send batch metrics to CloudWatch' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Gather metrics new_users = User.objects.filter(date_joined__date=datetime.today()).count() total_queries = len(connection.queries) cache_stats = cache.get_stats() if hasattr(cache, 'get_stats') else {} # Batch send metrics metric_data = [ { 'MetricName': 'UserSignups', 'Value': new_users, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [{'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'}] }, { 'MetricName': 'DatabaseQueries', 'Value': total_queries, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'}, {'Name': 'Type', 'Value': 'SELECT'} ] }, { 'MetricName': 'ActiveJobPosts', 'Value': JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count(), 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [{'Name': 'Status', 'Value': 'published'}] } ] cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Application', MetricData=metric_data ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Batch metrics sent')) # Run: python manage.py send_batch_metrics
Example 4: Track Database Query Performance

Monitor Django ORM query execution times to identify slow database operations affecting the jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Track database query performance
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/Database"
METRIC_NAME="QueryExecutionTime"
QUERY_TIME=1250.3  # milliseconds
QUERY_TYPE="SELECT"

aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "$METRIC_NAME" \
  --value $QUERY_TIME \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --dimensions \
    Name=QueryType,Value=$QUERY_TYPE \
    Name=Table,Value=jobpost_jobpost \
    Name=Complexity,Value=Join \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Database query metric sent: ${QUERY_TIME}ms"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "d4e5f6a7-b8c9-0123-def1-234567890123", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Database query metric sent: 1250.3ms
# Django database query performance monitoring from django.db import connection from django.db.backends.utils import CursorWrapper import boto3 import time from datetime import datetime class CloudWatchQueryMonitor: def __init__(self): self.cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') def track_query(self, sql, duration_ms, query_type='SELECT'): """Send query performance metric to CloudWatch""" # Determine table from SQL table = 'unknown' if 'FROM' in sql.upper(): parts = sql.upper().split('FROM')[1].strip().split() if parts: table = parts[0].replace('`', '').replace('"', '') try: self.cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Database', MetricData=[ { 'MetricName': 'QueryExecutionTime', 'Value': duration_ms, 'Unit': 'Milliseconds', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'QueryType', 'Value': query_type}, {'Name': 'Table', 'Value': table}, {'Name': 'Complexity', 'Value': 'Join' if 'JOIN' in sql.upper() else 'Simple'} ] } ] ) except Exception as e: print(f"CloudWatch metric error: {e}") # Example: Monitor complex query from jobpost.models import JobPost monitor = CloudWatchQueryMonitor() start = time.time() # Complex query with joins jobs = JobPost.objects.select_related('author', 'location').prefetch_related('required_skills') jobs_list = list(jobs.filter(status='published')[:100]) duration_ms = (time.time() - start) * 1000 print(f"Query executed in {duration_ms:.2f}ms") # Output: Query executed in 1250.30ms # Metric sent to CloudWatch with table and complexity dimensions
Example 5: Monitor Custom Business Metrics

Track business-specific metrics like skills demand, location popularity, and author activity for the jobpost application.

#!/bin/bash
# Track business metrics
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/Business"

# Skills demand metric
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "SkillDemand" \
  --value 45 \
  --unit Count \
  --dimensions Name=Skill,Value=Python Name=Period,Value=Daily \
  --region us-east-1

# Location popularity
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "LocationPopularity" \
  --value 128 \
  --unit Count \
  --dimensions Name=City,Value=SanFrancisco Name=Period,Value=Weekly \
  --region us-east-1

# Author activity
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "$NAMESPACE" \
  --metric-name "AuthorActivity" \
  --value 23 \
  --unit Count \
  --dimensions Name=AuthorID,Value=author_123 Name=Action,Value=PostsCreated \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Business metrics sent to CloudWatch"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "e5f6a7b8-c9d0-1234-ef12-345678901234", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Business metrics sent to CloudWatch Metrics tracking: - SkillDemand: Python (45 job posts today) - LocationPopularity: San Francisco (128 posts this week) - AuthorActivity: author_123 (23 posts created)
# Django scheduled task to track business metrics from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, Location, Author from django.db.models import Count from datetime import datetime, timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Send business metrics to CloudWatch' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') timestamp = datetime.utcnow() # Track skills demand today = datetime.today().date() skills_demand = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__date=today ).values('required_skills__name').annotate(count=Count('id')) for skill_data in skills_demand: skill_name = skill_data['required_skills__name'] count = skill_data['count'] cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Business', MetricData=[{ 'MetricName': 'SkillDemand', 'Value': count, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': timestamp, 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'Skill', 'Value': skill_name or 'Unknown'}, {'Name': 'Period', 'Value': 'Daily'} ] }] ) # Track location popularity last_week = today - timedelta(days=7) location_stats = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__date__gte=last_week ).values('location__city').annotate(count=Count('id')) for loc_data in location_stats: city = loc_data['location__city'] count = loc_data['count'] cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Business', MetricData=[{ 'MetricName': 'LocationPopularity', 'Value': count, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': timestamp, 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'City', 'Value': city or 'Unknown'}, {'Name': 'Period', 'Value': 'Weekly'} ] }] ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Business metrics sent')) # Schedule with cron: 0 0 * * * python manage.py send_business_metrics

get-metric-statistics

Retrieves statistics for specified metrics over a time period. Essential for analyzing historical performance data, generating reports, and understanding trends in your Django application metrics.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --start-time {start-time} \
  --end-time {end-time} \
  --period {seconds} \
  --statistics {statistic} \
  --dimensions Name={name},Value={value}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric. Example: "AWS/EC2" or "AnihpjDjango/JobPost"
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric. Example: "CPUUtilization", "JobPostsCreated"
  • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start of time range (ISO 8601 or Unix timestamp)
  • --end-time (timestamp, required): End of time range
  • --period (integer, required): Granularity in seconds (60, 300, 3600, etc.). Must be multiple of 60
  • --statistics (list, required): Statistics to retrieve. Values: Average, Sum, Minimum, Maximum, SampleCount
  • --dimensions (list): Dimensions to filter the metric data
  • --unit (string): Unit of the metric to filter results
Return Value
  • Datapoints (Array): Array of metric data points with timestamp and values
  • Timestamp (string): Time of the data point
  • Average/Sum/Min/Max (double): Statistical values based on requested statistics
  • Unit (string): Unit of measurement
  • Label (string): Metric label
5 Detailed CloudWatch get-metric-statistics Examples
Example 1: Retrieve Job Posts Created Statistics for Last 7 Days

Get daily job post creation statistics to analyze posting trends.

#!/bin/bash
# Get job posts created stats for last 7 days
START_TIME=$(date -u -d '7 days ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
END_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/JobPost" \
  --metric-name "JobPostsCreated" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 86400 \
  --statistics Sum Average \
  --dimensions Name=Environment,Value=production \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Retrieved 7-day job post statistics"
{ "Label": "JobPostsCreated", "Datapoints": [ { "Timestamp": "2026-01-10T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 45.0, "Average": 1.875, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-11T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 52.0, "Average": 2.167, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-12T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 38.0, "Average": 1.583, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-13T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 61.0, "Average": 2.542, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-14T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 48.0, "Average": 2.0, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-15T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 55.0, "Average": 2.292, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 42.0, "Average": 1.75, "Unit": "Count" } ] } ✓ Retrieved 7-day job post statistics Total posts last 7 days: 341 Average daily: 48.7 posts
# Django view to display job post trends from django.shortcuts import render from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 def job_post_trends(request): """Display 7-day job posting trends from CloudWatch""" cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') end_time = timezone.now() start_time = end_time - timedelta(days=7) response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/JobPost', MetricName='JobPostsCreated', StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=86400, # Daily Statistics=['Sum', 'Average'], Dimensions=[ {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'} ] ) # Process datapoints datapoints = sorted(response['Datapoints'], key=lambda x: x['Timestamp']) trend_data = [ { 'date': dp['Timestamp'].strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'total': int(dp['Sum']), 'average': round(dp['Average'], 2) } for dp in datapoints ] total_posts = sum(dp['total'] for dp in trend_data) avg_daily = round(total_posts / len(datapoints), 1) if datapoints else 0 return render(request, 'trends.html', { 'trend_data': trend_data, 'total_posts': total_posts, 'avg_daily': avg_daily }) # Template usage: {{ trend_data }}, {{ total_posts }}, {{ avg_daily }}
Example 2: Analyze API Response Time Performance

Get hourly API response time statistics to monitor application performance.

#!/bin/bash
# Analyze API response times for last 24 hours
START_TIME=$(date -u -d '24 hours ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
END_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/API" \
  --metric-name "ResponseTime" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 3600 \
  --statistics Average Minimum Maximum \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Retrieved 24-hour API response time statistics"
{ "Label": "ResponseTime", "Datapoints": [ { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T10:00:00Z", "Average": 245.6, "Minimum": 89.0, "Maximum": 1230.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T11:00:00Z", "Average": 198.3, "Minimum": 95.0, "Maximum": 890.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T12:00:00Z", "Average": 312.7, "Minimum": 102.0, "Maximum": 1450.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T13:00:00Z", "Average": 223.1, "Minimum": 88.0, "Maximum": 980.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" } ] } ✓ Retrieved 24-hour API response time statistics Average response time: 245ms Peak response time: 1450ms (12:00 UTC)
# Django management command for performance analysis from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Analyze API response time performance' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') end_time = timezone.now() start_time = end_time - timedelta(hours=24) response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/API', MetricName='ResponseTime', StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=3600, # Hourly Statistics=['Average', 'Minimum', 'Maximum'], Unit='Milliseconds' ) datapoints = sorted(response['Datapoints'], key=lambda x: x['Timestamp']) # Calculate performance metrics if datapoints: avg_response = sum(dp['Average'] for dp in datapoints) / len(datapoints) max_response = max(dp['Maximum'] for dp in datapoints) min_response = min(dp['Minimum'] for dp in datapoints) self.stdout.write(f"Performance Analysis (Last 24 Hours):") self.stdout.write(f" Average Response Time: {avg_response:.1f}ms") self.stdout.write(f" Best Response Time: {min_response:.1f}ms") self.stdout.write(f" Worst Response Time: {max_response:.1f}ms") # Alert if performance degraded if avg_response > 300: self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( f"⚠️ High average response time: {avg_response:.1f}ms" )) else: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS("✓ Performance within acceptable range"))
Example 3: Monitor Database Query Performance Trends

Retrieve database query execution time statistics to identify performance degradation.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor database query performance for last 6 hours
START_TIME=$(date -u -d '6 hours ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
END_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Database" \
  --metric-name "QueryExecutionTime" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 300 \
  --statistics Average Maximum SampleCount \
  --dimensions Name=QueryType,Value=SELECT \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Retrieved database query performance statistics"
{ "Label": "QueryExecutionTime", "Datapoints": [ { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T08:00:00Z", "Average": 45.2, "Maximum": 189.0, "SampleCount": 342.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T08:05:00Z", "Average": 52.8, "Maximum": 205.0, "SampleCount": 298.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T08:10:00Z", "Average": 48.1, "Maximum": 176.0, "SampleCount": 315.0, "Unit": "Milliseconds" } ] } ✓ Retrieved database query performance statistics Average query time: 48.7ms Peak query time: 205ms Total queries: 955
# Django analytics view for database performance from django.http import JsonResponse from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 def database_performance_api(request): """API endpoint for database performance metrics""" cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') hours = int(request.GET.get('hours', 6)) query_type = request.GET.get('type', 'SELECT') end_time = timezone.now() start_time = end_time - timedelta(hours=hours) response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Database', MetricName='QueryExecutionTime', StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=300, # 5-minute intervals Statistics=['Average', 'Maximum', 'SampleCount'], Dimensions=[ {'Name': 'QueryType', 'Value': query_type} ], Unit='Milliseconds' ) datapoints = sorted(response['Datapoints'], key=lambda x: x['Timestamp']) # Calculate metrics if datapoints: avg_time = sum(dp['Average'] for dp in datapoints) / len(datapoints) max_time = max(dp['Maximum'] for dp in datapoints) total_queries = sum(dp['SampleCount'] for dp in datapoints) # Identify slow periods (avg > 60ms) slow_periods = [ { 'time': dp['Timestamp'].strftime('%H:%M'), 'avg': round(dp['Average'], 1), 'max': round(dp['Maximum'], 1) } for dp in datapoints if dp['Average'] > 60 ] return JsonResponse({ 'query_type': query_type, 'time_range_hours': hours, 'average_query_time': round(avg_time, 2), 'peak_query_time': round(max_time, 2), 'total_queries': int(total_queries), 'slow_periods': slow_periods, 'performance_status': 'good' if avg_time < 50 else 'degraded' }) return JsonResponse({'error': 'No data available'}, status=404)
Example 4: Track User Activity Patterns

Analyze user signup and login statistics to understand usage patterns.

#!/bin/bash
# Track user activity for last 30 days
START_TIME=$(date -u -d '30 days ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")
END_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Users" \
  --metric-name "UserSignups" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 86400 \
  --statistics Sum \
  --dimensions Name=Platform,Value=Web \
  --unit Count \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Retrieved 30-day user signup statistics"
{ "Label": "UserSignups", "Datapoints": [ { "Timestamp": "2025-12-17T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 23.0, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2025-12-18T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 31.0, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2025-12-19T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 28.0, "Unit": "Count" }, ... { "Timestamp": "2026-01-15T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 45.0, "Unit": "Count" }, { "Timestamp": "2026-01-16T00:00:00Z", "Sum": 38.0, "Unit": "Count" } ] } ✓ Retrieved 30-day user signup statistics Total signups last 30 days: 892 Average daily signups: 29.7
# Django dashboard view for user activity analytics from django.shortcuts import render from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 @login_required def user_activity_dashboard(request): """Admin dashboard showing user activity trends""" cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') end_time = timezone.now() start_time = end_time - timedelta(days=30) # Get signup statistics signup_response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Users', MetricName='UserSignups', StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=86400, # Daily Statistics=['Sum'], Dimensions=[{'Name': 'Platform', 'Value': 'Web'}], Unit='Count' ) # Get login statistics login_response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Users', MetricName='UserLogins', StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=86400, Statistics=['Sum'], Dimensions=[{'Name': 'Platform', 'Value': 'Web'}], Unit='Count' ) # Process signup data signup_data = sorted( signup_response['Datapoints'], key=lambda x: x['Timestamp'] ) total_signups = sum(dp['Sum'] for dp in signup_data) avg_daily_signups = round(total_signups / len(signup_data), 1) if signup_data else 0 # Process login data login_data = sorted( login_response['Datapoints'], key=lambda x: x['Timestamp'] ) total_logins = sum(dp['Sum'] for dp in login_data) avg_daily_logins = round(total_logins / len(login_data), 1) if login_data else 0 # Prepare chart data chart_data = { 'labels': [dp['Timestamp'].strftime('%m/%d') for dp in signup_data], 'signups': [int(dp['Sum']) for dp in signup_data], 'logins': [int(dp['Sum']) for dp in login_data] } return render(request, 'admin/user_activity.html', { 'total_signups': int(total_signups), 'avg_daily_signups': avg_daily_signups, 'total_logins': int(total_logins), 'avg_daily_logins': avg_daily_logins, 'chart_data': chart_data })
Example 5: Generate Weekly Performance Report

Retrieve comprehensive metrics to generate a weekly performance report combining multiple metric types.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate weekly performance report
START_TIME=$(date -u -d '7 days ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00")
END_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT23:59:59")
NAMESPACE="AnihpjDjango/Performance"

echo "=== Weekly Performance Report ==="
echo "Period: $START_TIME to $END_TIME"
echo ""

# Job posts created
echo "1. Job Posts Created:"
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/JobPost" \
  --metric-name "JobPostsCreated" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 604800 \
  --statistics Sum \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Datapoints[0].Sum' \
  --output text

# API response time
echo "2. Average API Response Time:"
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/API" \
  --metric-name "ResponseTime" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 604800 \
  --statistics Average \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Datapoints[0].Average' \
  --output text

# Error rate
echo "3. Error Rate:"
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/API" \
  --metric-name "ErrorRate" \
  --start-time "$START_TIME" \
  --end-time "$END_TIME" \
  --period 604800 \
  --statistics Average \
  --unit Percent \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Datapoints[0].Average' \
  --output text

echo ""
echo "✓ Weekly performance report generated"
=== Weekly Performance Report === Period: 2026-01-10T00:00:00 to 2026-01-16T23:59:59 1. Job Posts Created: 341.0 2. Average API Response Time: 234.5 3. Error Rate: 0.42 ✓ Weekly performance report generated Report Summary: - Total Job Posts: 341 (48.7/day average) - Avg Response Time: 234.5ms (within target <300ms) - Error Rate: 0.42% (below threshold <1%)
# Django management command for weekly report generation from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.utils import timezone from django.core.mail import send_mail from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Generate and email weekly performance report' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') end_time = timezone.now() start_time = end_time -
