timedelta(days=7) # Collect metrics metrics_to_fetch = [ ('AnihpjDjango/JobPost', 'JobPostsCreated', 'Sum', 'Count'), ('AnihpjDjango/API', 'ResponseTime', 'Average', 'Milliseconds'), ('AnihpjDjango/API', 'ErrorRate', 'Average', 'Percent'), ('AnihpjDjango/Database', 'QueryExecutionTime', 'Average', 'Milliseconds'), ('AnihpjDjango/Users', 'UserSignups', 'Sum', 'Count'), ] report_data = {} for namespace, metric_name, statistic, unit in metrics_to_fetch: response = cloudwatch.get_metric_statistics( Namespace=namespace, MetricName=metric_name, StartTime=start_time, EndTime=end_time, Period=604800, # Weekly Statistics=[statistic], Unit=unit ) if response['Datapoints']: value = response['Datapoints'][0][statistic] report_data[metric_name] = round(value, 2) # Generate report report_lines = [ "=" * 50, "WEEKLY PERFORMANCE REPORT", f"Period: {start_time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')} to {end_time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}", "=" * 50, "", f"Job Posts Created: {report_data.get('JobPostsCreated', 0):.0f}", f"Average API Response: {report_data.get('ResponseTime', 0):.1f}ms", f"Error Rate: {report_data.get('ErrorRate', 0):.2f}%", f"Database Query Time: {report_data.get('QueryExecutionTime', 0):.1f}ms", f"New User Signups: {report_data.get('UserSignups', 0):.0f}", "", "=" * 50, ] report_text = "\n".join(report_lines) # Send email report send_mail( subject='Weekly Performance Report', message=report_text, from_email='noreply@anihpj.com', recipient_list=['admin@anihpj.com'], fail_silently=False, ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Weekly report generated and emailed')) self.stdout.write(report_text) # Schedule weekly: 0 9 * * MON python manage.py generate_weekly_report

put-metric-alarm

Creates or updates a CloudWatch alarm that watches a metric and triggers actions when thresholds are breached. Essential for proactive monitoring and automated incident response.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --alarm-description {description} \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --statistic {statistic} \
  --period {seconds} \
  --evaluation-periods {count} \
  --threshold {value} \
  --comparison-operator {operator} \
  --dimensions Name={name},Value={value}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-name (string, required): Unique name for the alarm
  • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric to monitor
  • --statistic (string, required): Statistic to apply. Values: Average, Sum, Minimum, Maximum, SampleCount
  • --period (integer, required): Period in seconds over which statistic is applied
  • --evaluation-periods (integer, required): Number of periods to evaluate before triggering
  • --threshold (double, required): Value to compare against the statistic
  • --comparison-operator (string, required): Operator for comparison. Values: GreaterThanThreshold, LessThanThreshold, GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold, LessThanOrEqualToThreshold
  • --alarm-description (string): Description of the alarm
  • --alarm-actions (list): Actions to execute when alarm state changes to ALARM (SNS topic ARNs)
  • --dimensions (list): Dimensions to filter the metric
  • --treat-missing-data (string): How to treat missing data. Values: notBreaching, breaching, ignore, missing
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP response metadata with RequestId and HTTPStatusCode
  • No explicit response body on success: HTTP 200 indicates alarm created/updated successfully
5 Detailed CloudWatch put-metric-alarm Examples
Example 1: Create Alarm for High API Response Time

Set up an alarm to trigger when API response time exceeds 500ms for 2 consecutive periods.

#!/bin/bash
# Create alarm for high API response time
SNS_TOPIC_ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:AlertNotifications"

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "HighAPIResponseTime" \
  --alarm-description "Alert when API response time exceeds 500ms" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/API" \
  --metric-name "ResponseTime" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --threshold 500.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TOPIC_ARN" \
  --treat-missing-data notBreaching \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ High API response time alarm created"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "f7g8h9i0-j1k2-3456-l7m8-n9o0p1q2r3s4", "HTTPStatusCode": 200, "HTTPHeaders": { "x-amzn-requestid": "f7g8h9i0-j1k2-3456-l7m8-n9o0p1q2r3s4", "content-type": "text/xml", "date": "Fri, 17 Jan 2026 11:15:00 GMT" }, "RetryAttempts": 0 } } ✓ High API response time alarm created Alarm Configuration: - Trigger: When Average > 500ms for 2 periods (10 minutes) - Action: Send SNS notification - Status: INSUFFICIENT_DATA (waiting for metric data)
# Django management command to create CloudWatch alarms from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.conf import settings import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Create CloudWatch alarms for Django application monitoring' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') sns_topic_arn = settings.AWS_SNS_ALARM_TOPIC_ARN # Create API response time alarm cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='HighAPIResponseTime', AlarmDescription='Alert when API response time exceeds 500ms', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/API', MetricName='ResponseTime', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=2, Threshold=500.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Milliseconds', AlarmActions=[sns_topic_arn], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ High API response time alarm created')) # Create database query performance alarm cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='SlowDatabaseQueries', AlarmDescription='Alert when average query time exceeds 100ms', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Database', MetricName='QueryExecutionTime', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=3, Threshold=100.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Milliseconds', AlarmActions=[sns_topic_arn], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Slow database queries alarm created')) # Create error rate alarm cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='HighErrorRate', AlarmDescription='Alert when error rate exceeds 5%', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/API', MetricName='ErrorRate', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=2, Threshold=5.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Percent', AlarmActions=[sns_topic_arn], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ High error rate alarm created')) # settings.py: AWS_SNS_ALARM_TOPIC_ARN = 'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:...'
Example 2: Create Alarm for Low Job Post Activity

Alert when daily job posts fall below expected threshold, indicating potential platform issues.

#!/bin/bash
# Create alarm for low job post activity
SNS_TOPIC_ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:AlertNotifications"

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "LowJobPostActivity" \
  --alarm-description "Alert when daily job posts drop below 20" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/JobPost" \
  --metric-name "JobPostsCreated" \
  --statistic Sum \
  --period 86400 \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --threshold 20.0 \
  --comparison-operator LessThanThreshold \
  --unit Count \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TOPIC_ARN" \
  --dimensions Name=Environment,Value=production \
  --treat-missing-data breaching \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Low job post activity alarm created"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "a2b3c4d5-e6f7-8901-g2h3-i4j5k6l7m8n9", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Low job post activity alarm created Alarm Configuration: - Trigger: When Sum < 20 for 1 day - Purpose: Detect unusually low platform activity - Action: Send SNS notification to operations team - Missing Data: Treated as breaching (conservative approach)
# Django scheduled task to monitor platform health from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from jobpost.models import JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Monitor job post activity and create alarms if needed' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Check yesterday's job post count yesterday = timezone.now().date() - timedelta(days=1) daily_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__date=yesterday ).count() self.stdout.write(f"Job posts created yesterday: {daily_posts}") # Create or update alarm based on historical average # Calculate 7-day average week_ago = yesterday - timedelta(days=7) weekly_avg = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__date__range=[week_ago, yesterday] ).count() / 7 # Set threshold at 40% of weekly average (conservative) threshold = max(weekly_avg * 0.4, 10) # Minimum threshold of 10 cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='LowJobPostActivity', AlarmDescription=f'Alert when daily job posts drop below {threshold:.0f}', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/JobPost', MetricName='JobPostsCreated', Statistic='Sum', Period=86400, EvaluationPeriods=1, Threshold=threshold, ComparisonOperator='LessThanThreshold', Unit='Count', Dimensions=[ {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'production'} ], TreatMissingData='breaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ Alarm updated with threshold: {threshold:.0f} posts/day' )) # Run daily: 0 2 * * * python manage.py monitor_platform_health
Example 3: Create Composite Alarm for Database Performance

Create an alarm that monitors multiple database metrics to detect overall performance degradation.

#!/bin/bash
# Create database performance alarms
SNS_TOPIC_ARN="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:DBANotifications"

# Alarm 1: High query execution time
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "DatabaseSlowQueries" \
  --alarm-description "Average query time exceeds 100ms" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Database" \
  --metric-name "QueryExecutionTime" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 3 \
  --threshold 100.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Milliseconds \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TOPIC_ARN" \
  --region us-east-1

# Alarm 2: High connection count
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "DatabaseHighConnections" \
  --alarm-description "Active connections exceed 80% capacity" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Database" \
  --metric-name "ActiveConnections" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --threshold 80.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Count \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TOPIC_ARN" \
  --region us-east-1

# Alarm 3: High error rate
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "DatabaseErrors" \
  --alarm-description "Database error rate exceeds 1%" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Database" \
  --metric-name "ErrorRate" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --threshold 1.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Percent \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TOPIC_ARN" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Database performance alarms created"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "alarm-1-request-id", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } { "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "alarm-2-request-id", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } { "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "alarm-3-request-id", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Database performance alarms created Alarms Created: 1. DatabaseSlowQueries - Monitors average query execution time 2. DatabaseHighConnections - Monitors connection pool saturation 3. DatabaseErrors - Monitors database operation failures All alarms will trigger SNS notifications to DBA team
# Django database monitoring integration from django.db import connection from django.core.cache import cache from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 from datetime import datetime class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Setup comprehensive database monitoring alarms' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') sns_arn = 'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:DBANotifications' alarms = [ { 'AlarmName': 'DatabaseSlowQueries', 'AlarmDescription': 'Average query time exceeds 100ms', 'MetricName': 'QueryExecutionTime', 'Statistic': 'Average', 'Threshold': 100.0, 'Unit': 'Milliseconds' }, { 'AlarmName': 'DatabaseHighConnections', 'AlarmDescription': 'Active connections exceed 80% capacity', 'MetricName': 'ActiveConnections', 'Statistic': 'Average', 'Threshold': 80.0, 'Unit': 'Count' }, { 'AlarmName': 'DatabaseErrors', 'AlarmDescription': 'Database error rate exceeds 1%', 'MetricName': 'ErrorRate', 'Statistic': 'Average', 'Threshold': 1.0, 'Unit': 'Percent' }, { 'AlarmName': 'DatabaseDeadlocks', 'AlarmDescription': 'Deadlocks detected in database', 'MetricName': 'Deadlocks', 'Statistic': 'Sum', 'Threshold': 1.0, 'Unit': 'Count' } ] for alarm_config in alarms: cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName=alarm_config['AlarmName'], AlarmDescription=alarm_config['AlarmDescription'], Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Database', MetricName=alarm_config['MetricName'], Statistic=alarm_config['Statistic'], Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=2, Threshold=alarm_config['Threshold'], ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit=alarm_config['Unit'], AlarmActions=[sns_arn], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ Created alarm: {alarm_config["AlarmName"]}' )) # Middleware to track connection pool status class DatabaseMonitoringMiddleware: def __init__(self, get_response): self.get_response = get_response self.cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') def __call__(self, request): # Track active connections active_connections = len(connection.queries) self.cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Database', MetricData=[{ 'MetricName': 'ActiveConnections', 'Value': active_connections, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow() }] ) response = self.get_response(request) return response
Example 4: Create Alarm with Multiple Notification Targets

Set up an alarm that notifies different teams through multiple SNS topics based on severity.

#!/bin/bash
# Create critical system alarm with multiple notification targets
SNS_TEAM_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:TeamAlerts"
SNS_PAGERDUTY_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:PagerDuty"
SNS_SLACK_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:SlackAlerts"

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "CriticalSystemErrors" \
  --alarm-description "Critical: System error rate exceeds 10%" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/System" \
  --metric-name "SystemErrorRate" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 60 \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --threshold 10.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Percent \
  --alarm-actions "$SNS_TEAM_TOPIC" "$SNS_PAGERDUTY_TOPIC" "$SNS_SLACK_TOPIC" \
  --ok-actions "$SNS_TEAM_TOPIC" "$SNS_SLACK_TOPIC" \
  --treat-missing-data breaching \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Critical system error alarm created with multi-channel notifications"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "m8n9o0p1-q2r3-4567-s8t9-u0v1w2x3y4z5", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Critical system error alarm created with multi-channel notifications Notification Channels: - ALARM state → Team email, PagerDuty, Slack - OK state → Team email, Slack - Missing data treated as breaching (fail-safe) Alert Routing: 1. Team email: All team members 2. PagerDuty: On-call engineer (immediate page) 3. Slack: #alerts channel (real-time visibility)
# Django error tracking with CloudWatch alarms from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.conf import settings import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Create multi-tier alerting for system errors' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Define notification targets notification_config = { 'team_email': settings.AWS_SNS_TEAM_TOPIC, 'pagerduty': settings.AWS_SNS_PAGERDUTY_TOPIC, 'slack': settings.AWS_SNS_SLACK_TOPIC } # Critical alarm - page on-call engineer cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='CriticalSystemErrors', AlarmDescription='Critical: System error rate exceeds 10%', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/System', MetricName='SystemErrorRate', Statistic='Average', Period=60, EvaluationPeriods=1, Threshold=10.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Percent', AlarmActions=[ notification_config['team_email'], notification_config['pagerduty'], notification_config['slack'] ], OKActions=[ notification_config['team_email'], notification_config['slack'] ], TreatMissingData='breaching' ) # Warning alarm - team notification only cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='WarningSystemErrors', AlarmDescription='Warning: System error rate exceeds 5%', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/System', MetricName='SystemErrorRate', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=2, Threshold=5.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Percent', AlarmActions=[ notification_config['team_email'], notification_config['slack'] ], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Multi-tier alerting configured')) self.stdout.write(' - Critical: >10% → Page + Email + Slack') self.stdout.write(' - Warning: >5% → Email + Slack') # Exception handler to track errors import logging from django.core.exceptions import MiddlewareNotUsed logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') def custom_exception_handler(exc, context): """Track exceptions in CloudWatch""" from datetime import datetime # Log to CloudWatch try: cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/System', MetricData=[{ 'MetricName': 'SystemErrors', 'Value': 1, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'ErrorType', 'Value': type(exc).__name__} ] }] ) except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Failed to send error metric: {e}") return None # Let default handler process it
Example 5: Create Auto-Scaling Trigger Alarm

Create an alarm that triggers auto-scaling actions when job post processing queue grows beyond capacity.

#!/bin/bash
# Create alarm to trigger auto-scaling
SCALING_POLICY_ARN="arn:aws:autoscaling:us-east-1:123456789012:scalingPolicy:..."

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "HighJobPostQueueDepth" \
  --alarm-description "Scale up when job post queue depth exceeds 100" \
  --namespace "AnihpjDjango/Queue" \
  --metric-name "JobPostQueueDepth" \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --threshold 100.0 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --unit Count \
  --alarm-actions "$SCALING_POLICY_ARN" \
  --dimensions Name=QueueName,Value=JobPostProcessing \
  --treat-missing-data notBreaching \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Auto-scaling alarm created for job post queue"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "x6y7z8a9-b0c1-2345-d6e7-f8g9h0i1j2k3", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Auto-scaling alarm created for job post queue Auto-Scaling Configuration: - Trigger: Average queue depth > 100 for 10 minutes - Action: Execute scaling policy to add workers - Queue: JobPostProcessing (email notifications, analytics, etc.) - Recovery: Alarm returns to OK when queue drains below threshold
# Django Celery monitoring with auto-scaling alarms from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from celery.app.control import Control import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Setup CloudWatch alarms for Celery queue auto-scaling' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') autoscaling = boto3.client('autoscaling', region_name='us-east-1') # Get auto-scaling policy ARN scaling_policy_name = 'ScaleUpWorkers' asg_name = 'JobPostWorkers-ASG' policy_response = autoscaling.describe_policies( AutoScalingGroupName=asg_name, PolicyNames=[scaling_policy_name] ) if not policy_response['ScalingPolicies']: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR('Scaling policy not found')) return policy_arn = policy_response['ScalingPolicies'][0]['PolicyARN'] # Create scale-up alarm cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='HighJobPostQueueDepth', AlarmDescription='Scale up when job post queue depth exceeds 100', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Queue', MetricName='JobPostQueueDepth', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=2, Threshold=100.0, ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold', Unit='Count', AlarmActions=[policy_arn], Dimensions=[ {'Name': 'QueueName', 'Value': 'JobPostProcessing'} ], TreatMissingData='notBreaching' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Scale-up alarm created')) # Create scale-down alarm scale_down_policy = autoscaling.describe_policies( AutoScalingGroupName=asg_name, PolicyNames=['ScaleDownWorkers'] ) if scale_down_policy['ScalingPolicies']: scale_down_arn = scale_down_policy['ScalingPolicies'][0]['PolicyARN'] cloudwatch.put_metric_alarm( AlarmName='LowJobPostQueueDepth', AlarmDescription='Scale down when queue depth below 20', Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Queue', MetricName='JobPostQueueDepth', Statistic='Average', Period=300, EvaluationPeriods=3, Threshold=20.0, ComparisonOperator='LessThanThreshold', Unit='Count', AlarmActions=[scale_down_arn], Dimensions=[ {'Name': 'QueueName', 'Value': 'JobPostProcessing'} ] ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Scale-down alarm created')) # Celery task to publish queue metrics from celery import shared_task from datetime import datetime @shared_task def publish_queue_metrics(): """Publish Celery queue depth to CloudWatch""" from myapp.celery import app cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') control = Control(app) # Get queue depth inspect = control.inspect() reserved = inspect.reserved() if reserved: queue_depth = sum(len(tasks) for tasks in reserved.values()) cloudwatch.put_metric_data( Namespace='AnihpjDjango/Queue', MetricData=[{ 'MetricName': 'JobPostQueueDepth', 'Value': queue_depth, 'Unit': 'Count', 'Timestamp': datetime.utcnow(), 'Dimensions': [ {'Name': 'QueueName', 'Value': 'JobPostProcessing'} ] }] ) # Beat schedule: publish_queue_metrics every 60 seconds

describe-alarms

Retrieves information about CloudWatch alarms. Use this to monitor alarm states, review configurations, and troubleshoot monitoring issues.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name1} {alarm-name2} \
  --state-value {state} \
  --max-records {number}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-names (list): Names of alarms to retrieve. If omitted, all alarms are returned
  • --state-value (string): Filter by alarm state. Values: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA
  • --alarm-name-prefix (string): Filter alarms by name prefix
  • --max-records (integer): Maximum number of alarms to return (1-100)
Return Value
  • MetricAlarms (Array): List of alarm objects with full configuration
  • AlarmName, AlarmArn, StateValue: Alarm identification and current state
  • MetricName, Namespace, Threshold: Metric configuration
5 Detailed CloudWatch describe-alarms Examples
Example 1: List All Active Alarms in ALARM State

Retrieve all alarms currently in ALARM state to identify active incidents.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all alarms in ALARM state
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --state-value ALARM \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'MetricAlarms[*].[AlarmName,StateValue,StateReason]' \
  --output table

echo "✓ Retrieved all active alarms"
---------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeAlarms | +-------------------------+---------+--------------------+ | HighAPIResponseTime | ALARM | Threshold Crossed | | LowJobPostActivity | ALARM | Threshold Crossed | | DatabaseSlowQueries | ALARM | Threshold Crossed | +-------------------------+---------+--------------------+ ✓ Retrieved all active alarms Active incidents: 3 alarms require attention
# Django monitoring dashboard - display active alarms from django.shortcuts import render from django.contrib.auth.decorators import user_passes_test import boto3 @user_passes_test(lambda u: u.is_staff) def alarm_dashboard(request): """Admin dashboard showing CloudWatch alarm status""" cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Get alarms in ALARM state alarm_response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(StateValue='ALARM') active_alarms = alarm_response['MetricAlarms'] # Get alarms in OK state ok_response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(StateValue='OK') ok_alarms = ok_response['MetricAlarms'] # Get alarms with insufficient data insufficient_response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms( StateValue='INSUFFICIENT_DATA' ) insufficient_alarms = insufficient_response['MetricAlarms'] # Format alarm data alarm_data = { 'critical': [ { 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'reason': alarm['StateReason'], 'metric': alarm['MetricName'], 'threshold': alarm['Threshold'], 'state_updated': alarm['StateUpdatedTimestamp'] } for alarm in active_alarms ], 'ok': len(ok_alarms), 'insufficient': len(insufficient_alarms), 'total': len(active_alarms) + len(ok_alarms) + len(insufficient_alarms) } return render(request, 'admin/alarm_dashboard.html', { 'alarm_data': alarm_data, 'critical_count': len(active_alarms) })
Example 2: Get Details for Specific Alarms

Retrieve detailed configuration for specific alarms by name.

#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific alarms
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --alarm-names "HighAPIResponseTime" "DatabaseSlowQueries" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Retrieved alarm details"
{ "MetricAlarms": [ { "AlarmName": "HighAPIResponseTime", "AlarmArn": "arn:aws:cloudwatch:us-east-1:123456789012:alarm:HighAPIResponseTime", "AlarmDescription": "Alert when API response time exceeds 500ms", "MetricName": "ResponseTime", "Namespace": "AnihpjDjango/API", "Statistic": "Average", "Period": 300, "EvaluationPeriods": 2, "Threshold": 500.0, "ComparisonOperator": "GreaterThanThreshold", "StateValue": "ALARM", "StateReason": "Threshold Crossed: 2 datapoints [612.5, 543.2] were greater than threshold (500.0)", "StateUpdatedTimestamp": "2026-01-17T10:45:00Z", "ActionsEnabled": true, "AlarmActions": [ "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:AlertNotifications" ] }, { "AlarmName": "DatabaseSlowQueries", "AlarmArn": "arn:aws:cloudwatch:us-east-1:123456789012:alarm:DatabaseSlowQueries", "AlarmDescription": "Alert when average query time exceeds 100ms", "MetricName": "QueryExecutionTime", "Namespace": "AnihpjDjango/Database", "Statistic": "Average", "Period": 300, "EvaluationPeriods": 3, "Threshold": 100.0, "ComparisonOperator": "GreaterThanThreshold", "StateValue": "OK", "StateReason": "Threshold Crossed: 3 datapoints [65.2, 71.8, 68.5] were not greater than threshold (100.0)", "StateUpdatedTimestamp": "2026-01-17T11:00:00Z" } ] } ✓ Retrieved alarm details
# Django API endpoint for alarm details from django.http import JsonResponse from django.views.decorators.http import require_GET import boto3 @require_GET def alarm_details_api(request): """API endpoint to get alarm details""" alarm_names = request.GET.getlist('alarms') if not alarm_names: return JsonResponse({'error': 'No alarm names provided'}, status=400) cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') try: response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNames=alarm_names) alarms_info = [] for alarm in response['MetricAlarms']: alarms_info.append({ 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'description': alarm.get('AlarmDescription', ''), 'state': alarm['StateValue'], 'state_reason': alarm['StateReason'], 'metric': { 'name': alarm['MetricName'], 'namespace': alarm['Namespace'], 'statistic': alarm['Statistic'] }, 'configuration': { 'period': alarm['Period'], 'evaluation_periods': alarm['EvaluationPeriods'], 'threshold': alarm['Threshold'], 'comparison': alarm['ComparisonOperator'] }, 'last_updated': alarm['StateUpdatedTimestamp'].isoformat(), 'actions_enabled': alarm['ActionsEnabled'] }) return JsonResponse({ 'alarms': alarms_info, 'count': len(alarms_info) }) except Exception as e: return JsonResponse({'error': str(e)}, status=500) # URL: /api/alarms/?alarms=HighAPIResponseTime&alarms=DatabaseSlowQueries
Example 3: List All Alarms with Name Prefix

Find all alarms matching a specific naming pattern.

#!/bin/bash
# List all database-related alarms
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --alarm-name-prefix "Database" \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'MetricAlarms[*].[AlarmName,StateValue,MetricName,Threshold]' \
  --output table

echo "✓ Retrieved database alarms"
---------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeAlarms | +---------------------------+-------+------------------+--------+ | DatabaseSlowQueries | OK | QueryExecTime | 100.0 | | DatabaseHighConnections | OK | ActiveConns | 80.0 | | DatabaseErrors | ALARM| ErrorRate | 1.0 | | DatabaseDeadlocks | OK | Deadlocks | 1.0 | +---------------------------+-------+------------------+--------+ ✓ Retrieved database alarms Found 4 database monitoring alarms
# Django management command to audit alarms by category from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Audit CloudWatch alarms by category' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( '--category', type=str, default='Database', help='Alarm category prefix (Database, API, JobPost, etc.)' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') category = options['category'] response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNamePrefix=category) alarms = response['MetricAlarms'] if not alarms: self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( f'No alarms found with prefix "{category}"' )) return self.stdout.write(f'\n{category} Alarms Audit Report') self.stdout.write('=' * 60) # Group by state alarm_states = {'OK': [], 'ALARM': [], 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA': []} for alarm in alarms: state = alarm['StateValue'] alarm_states[state].append({ 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'metric': alarm['MetricName'], 'threshold': alarm['Threshold'], 'reason': alarm['StateReason'][:50] + '...' }) # Display results for state, state_alarms in alarm_states.items(): if state_alarms: self.stdout.write(f'\n{state} ({len(state_alarms)}):') for alarm in state_alarms: self.stdout.write(f" - {alarm['name']}") self.stdout.write(f" Metric: {alarm['metric']} | Threshold: {alarm['threshold']}") total = len(alarms) critical = len(alarm_states['ALARM']) self.stdout.write(f'\nTotal: {total} alarms | Critical: {critical}') if critical > 0: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR( f'⚠️ {critical} alarm(s) require immediate attention!' )) else: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ All alarms healthy'))
Example 4: Monitor Alarm State Changes

Check alarm history to track state transitions and identify patterns.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all alarms and check for recent state changes
CURRENT_TIME=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'MetricAlarms[?StateUpdatedTimestamp>=`2026-01-17T00:00:00Z`].[AlarmName,StateValue,StateUpdatedTimestamp,StateReason]' \
  --output table

echo "✓ Retrieved alarms with recent state changes"
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeAlarms | +--------------------------+---------+------------------------+---------------------------+ | HighAPIResponseTime | ALARM | 2026-01-17T10:45:00Z | Threshold Crossed | | DatabaseErrors | ALARM | 2026-01-17T09:30:00Z | Error rate too high | | LowJobPostActivity | OK | 2026-01-17T08:15:00Z | Back to normal | +--------------------------+---------+------------------------+---------------------------+ ✓ Retrieved alarms with recent state changes 3 alarms changed state in last 24 hours
# Django scheduled task to monitor alarm state changes from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.core.mail import send_mail from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Monitor and report alarm state changes' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Get all alarms response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms() alarms = response['MetricAlarms'] # Filter alarms that changed state in last hour one_hour_ago = timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=1) recent_changes = [] for alarm in alarms: state_updated = alarm['StateUpdatedTimestamp'] # Convert to timezone-aware datetime if state_updated.tzinfo is None: state_updated = timezone.make_aware(state_updated) if state_updated >= one_hour_ago: recent_changes.append({ 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'old_state': 'Unknown', # Would need alarm history for this 'new_state': alarm['StateValue'], 'time': state_updated, 'reason': alarm['StateReason'] }) if recent_changes: # Send notification email email_body = "CloudWatch Alarm State Changes (Last Hour)\n\n" for change in recent_changes: email_body += f"Alarm: {change['name']}\n" email_body += f"State: {change['new_state']}\n" email_body += f"Time: {change['time']}\n" email_body += f"Reason: {change['reason']}\n" email_body += "-" * 60 + "\n\n" send_mail( subject=f'CloudWatch Alarms: {len(recent_changes)} state changes', message=email_body, from_email='monitoring@anihpj.com', recipient_list=['ops-team@anihpj.com'], fail_silently=False ) self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( f'⚠️ {len(recent_changes)} alarm state changes detected' )) else: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ No recent alarm state changes')) # Run every hour: 0 * * * * python manage.py monitor_alarm_changes
Example 5: Generate Alarm Configuration Report

Audit all alarms and generate a comprehensive configuration report for compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate comprehensive alarm report
OUTPUT_FILE="alarm_report_$(date +%Y%m%d).json"

aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --output json > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# Extract summary statistics
TOTAL=$(jq '.MetricAlarms | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
OK=$(jq '[.MetricAlarms[] | select(.StateValue=="OK")] | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
ALARM=$(jq '[.MetricAlarms[] | select(.StateValue=="ALARM")] | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")
INSUFFICIENT=$(jq '[.MetricAlarms[] | select(.StateValue=="INSUFFICIENT_DATA")] | length' "$OUTPUT_FILE")

echo "=== CloudWatch Alarms Report ==="
echo "Date: $(date)"
echo "Total Alarms: $TOTAL"
echo "  - OK: $OK"
echo "  - ALARM: $ALARM"
echo "  - INSUFFICIENT_DATA: $INSUFFICIENT"
echo ""
echo "Report saved to: $OUTPUT_FILE"
echo "✓ Alarm configuration report generated"
=== CloudWatch Alarms Report === Date: Fri Jan 17 11:30:00 UTC 2026 Total Alarms: 15 - OK: 11 - ALARM: 3 - INSUFFICIENT_DATA: 1 Report saved to: alarm_report_20260117.json ✓ Alarm configuration report generated Alarm Health: 73% (11/15 OK) Critical Issues: 3 alarms require attention Action Required: Review ALARM state alarms immediately
# Django management command for alarm compliance reporting from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.conf import settings import boto3 import json from datetime import datetime from pathlib import Path class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Generate CloudWatch alarms compliance report' def handle(self, *args, **kwargs): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Get all alarms response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms() alarms = response['MetricAlarms'] # Analyze alarm configuration report = { 'generated_at': datetime.now().isoformat(), 'summary': { 'total': len(alarms), 'by_state': { 'OK': 0, 'ALARM': 0, 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA': 0 }, 'by_namespace': {}, 'actions_enabled': 0, 'missing_actions': [] }, 'alarms': [] } for alarm in alarms: # Count by state state = alarm['StateValue'] report['summary']['by_state'][state] += 1 # Count by namespace namespace = alarm['Namespace'] report['summary']['by_namespace'][namespace] = \ report['summary']['by_namespace'].get(namespace, 0) + 1 # Check if actions are configured if alarm['ActionsEnabled'] and alarm.get('AlarmActions'): report['summary']['actions_enabled'] += 1 else: report['summary']['missing_actions'].append(alarm['AlarmName']) # Add alarm details report['alarms'].append({ 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'state': alarm['StateValue'], 'namespace': alarm['Namespace'], 'metric': alarm['MetricName'], 'threshold': alarm['Threshold'], 'actions_enabled': alarm['ActionsEnabled'], 'has_actions': bool(alarm.get('AlarmActions')) }) # Save report report_dir = Path(settings.BASE_DIR) / 'reports' report_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) report_file = report_dir / f"alarm_report_{datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d')}.json" with open(report_file, 'w') as f: json.dump(report, f, indent=2) # Display summary self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('=== Alarm Compliance Report ===')) self.stdout.write(f"Total Alarms: {report['summary']['total']}") self.stdout.write(f" OK: {report['summary']['by_state']['OK']}") self.stdout.write(f" ALARM: {report['summary']['by_state']['ALARM']}") self.stdout.write(f" INSUFFICIENT_DATA: {report['summary']['by_state']['INSUFFICIENT_DATA']}") self.stdout.write(f"\nActions Enabled: {report['summary']['actions_enabled']}/{report['summary']['total']}") if report['summary']['missing_actions']: self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( f"\n⚠️ {len(report['summary']['missing_actions'])} alarms without actions" )) self.stdout.write(f"\nReport saved: {report_file}")

delete-alarms

Deletes specified CloudWatch alarms. Use this to remove obsolete alarms and clean up monitoring configuration.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name1} {alarm-name2}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-names (list, required): Names of alarms to delete. Maximum 100 alarms per request
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on successful deletion
5 Detailed CloudWatch delete-alarms Examples
Example 1: Delete Single Obsolete Alarm

Remove an alarm that is no longer needed.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete obsolete alarm
ALARM_NAME="OldTestAlarm"

aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names "$ALARM_NAME" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Alarm '$ALARM_NAME' deleted successfully"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-1234-567890abcdef", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Alarm 'OldTestAlarm' deleted successfully
# Django management command to delete alarm from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Delete a CloudWatch alarm' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( 'alarm_name', type=str, help='Name of the alarm to delete' ) parser.add_argument( '--confirm', action='store_true', help='Skip confirmation prompt' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): alarm_name = options['alarm_name'] cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Get alarm details first try: response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNames=[alarm_name]) if not response['MetricAlarms']: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR( f'Alarm "{alarm_name}" not found' )) return alarm = response['MetricAlarms'][0] # Show alarm details self.stdout.write(f'\nAlarm to delete:') self.stdout.write(f' Name: {alarm["AlarmName"]}') self.stdout.write(f' State: {alarm["StateValue"]}') self.stdout.write(f' Metric: {alarm["MetricName"]}') # Confirm deletion if not options['confirm']: confirm = input('\nAre you sure you want to delete this alarm? (yes/no): ') if confirm.lower() != 'yes': self.stdout.write('Deletion cancelled') return # Delete alarm cloudwatch.delete_alarms(AlarmNames=[alarm_name]) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ Alarm "{alarm_name}" deleted successfully' )) except Exception as e: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f'Error: {str(e)}')) # Usage: python manage.py delete_alarm OldTestAlarm --confirm
Example 2: Delete Multiple Alarms in Batch

Remove multiple obsolete alarms in a single operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete multiple alarms
ALARMS_TO_DELETE=(
    "DeprecatedAlarm1"
    "DeprecatedAlarm2"
    "TestAlarm3"
    "OldMetricAlarm"
)

aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names "${ALARMS_TO_DELETE[@]}" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Deleted ${#ALARMS_TO_DELETE[@]} alarms"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "b2c3d4e5-f6g7-8901-2345-678901bcdefg", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Deleted 4 alarms Removed: DeprecatedAlarm1, DeprecatedAlarm2, TestAlarm3, OldMetricAlarm
# Django management command for batch alarm deletion from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Delete multiple CloudWatch alarms' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( '--prefix', type=str, help='Delete alarms matching this prefix' ) parser.add_argument( '--names', nargs='+', help='Specific alarm names to delete' ) parser.add_argument( '--dry-run', action='store_true', help='Show what would be deleted without deleting' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') alarms_to_delete = [] if options['names']: # Delete specific alarms alarms_to_delete = options['names'] elif options['prefix']: # Find alarms by prefix response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms( AlarmNamePrefix=options['prefix'] ) alarms_to_delete = [ alarm['AlarmName'] for alarm in response['MetricAlarms'] ] else: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR( 'Must specify --prefix or --names' )) return if not alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write('No alarms found to delete') return # Show what will be deleted self.stdout.write(f'\nAlarms to delete ({len(alarms_to_delete)}):') for alarm_name in alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write(f' - {alarm_name}') if options['dry_run']: self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( '\n[DRY RUN] No alarms were actually deleted' )) return # Confirm confirm = input('\nProceed with deletion? (yes/no): ') if confirm.lower() != 'yes': self.stdout.write('Deletion cancelled') return # Delete in batches of 100 (API limit) for i in range(0, len(alarms_to_delete), 100): batch = alarms_to_delete[i:i+100] cloudwatch.delete_alarms(AlarmNames=batch) self.stdout.write(f'Deleted batch {i//100 + 1}: {len(batch)} alarms') self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'\n✓ Successfully deleted {len(alarms_to_delete)} alarms' )) # Usage: python manage.py batch_delete_alarms --prefix "Test"
Example 3: Clean Up Test Alarms After Development

Remove all test alarms created during development or testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up test alarms
# First, list test alarms
echo "Finding test alarms..."
TEST_ALARMS=$(aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --alarm-name-prefix "Test" \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'MetricAlarms[*].AlarmName' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$TEST_ALARMS" ]; then
    echo "No test alarms found"
    exit 0
fi

# Convert to array
ALARM_ARRAY=($TEST_ALARMS)
echo "Found ${#ALARM_ARRAY[@]} test alarms"

# Delete them
aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names ${ALARM_ARRAY[@]} \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Test alarms cleanup completed"
Finding test alarms... Found 7 test alarms { "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "c3d4e5f6-g7h8-9012-3456-789012cdefgh", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Test alarms cleanup completed Removed test alarms: TestAPI, TestDB, TestQueue, TestMetric1, TestMetric2, TestAlarm1, TestAlarm2
# Django management command for environment-specific cleanup from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.conf import settings import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Clean up test/development alarms' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( '--environment', type=str, default='test', choices=['test', 'dev', 'staging'], help='Environment to clean up' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): # Only allow cleanup in non-production environments if settings.ENVIRONMENT == 'production': self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR( '❌ Cannot run cleanup in production!' )) return cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') env = options['environment'] # Define cleanup patterns patterns = { 'test': ['Test', 'TMP', 'Temp'], 'dev': ['Dev', 'Development', 'Local'], 'staging': ['Staging', 'STG'] } prefixes = patterns.get(env, []) all_alarms_to_delete = [] # Find alarms for each prefix for prefix in prefixes: response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNamePrefix=prefix) alarm_names = [ alarm['AlarmName'] for alarm in response['MetricAlarms'] ] all_alarms_to_delete.extend(alarm_names) if not all_alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write(f'No {env} alarms found to clean up') return self.stdout.write(f'\nFound {len(all_alarms_to_delete)} {env} alarms:') for alarm in all_alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write(f' - {alarm}') # Confirm deletion confirm = input(f'\nDelete all {env} alarms? (yes/no): ') if confirm.lower() != 'yes': self.stdout.write('Cleanup cancelled') return # Delete alarms cloudwatch.delete_alarms(AlarmNames=all_alarms_to_delete) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ Cleaned up {len(all_alarms_to_delete)} {env} alarms' )) # Usage: python manage.py cleanup_alarms --environment test
Example 4: Delete Alarms for Decommissioned Resources

Remove alarms associated with resources that no longer exist.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete alarms for decommissioned service
SERVICE_NAME="OldMicroservice"

# Find alarms with service name in dimensions
ALARMS=$(aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query "MetricAlarms[?contains(Dimensions[].Value, '$SERVICE_NAME')].AlarmName" \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$ALARMS" ]; then
    echo "No alarms found for service: $SERVICE_NAME"
    exit 0
fi

echo "Deleting alarms for decommissioned service: $SERVICE_NAME"
ALARM_ARRAY=($ALARMS)

aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names ${ALARM_ARRAY[@]} \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Deleted ${#ALARM_ARRAY[@]} alarms for $SERVICE_NAME"
Deleting alarms for decommissioned service: OldMicroservice { "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "d4e5f6g7-h8i9-0123-4567-890123defghi", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Deleted 5 alarms for OldMicroservice Removed: OldMicroservice-CPU, OldMicroservice-Memory, OldMicroservice-Errors, OldMicroservice-Latency, OldMicroservice-Requests
# Django management command for resource-based cleanup from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Delete alarms for decommissioned resources' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( 'resource_name', type=str, help='Name of decommissioned resource' ) parser.add_argument( '--search-in', type=str, choices=['name', 'dimensions', 'both'], default='both', help='Where to search for resource name' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') resource_name = options['resource_name'] search_in = options['search_in'] # Get all alarms response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms() all_alarms = response['MetricAlarms'] alarms_to_delete = [] for alarm in all_alarms: should_delete = False # Check alarm name if search_in in ['name', 'both']: if resource_name in alarm['AlarmName']: should_delete = True # Check dimensions if search_in in ['dimensions', 'both']: for dimension in alarm.get('Dimensions', []): if resource_name in dimension.get('Value', ''): should_delete = True break if should_delete: alarms_to_delete.append(alarm['AlarmName']) if not alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write(f'No alarms found for resource: {resource_name}') return self.stdout.write(f'\nFound {len(alarms_to_delete)} alarms for "{resource_name}":') for alarm in alarms_to_delete: self.stdout.write(f' - {alarm}') # Confirm deletion confirm = input('\nDelete these alarms? (yes/no): ') if confirm.lower() != 'yes': self.stdout.write('Deletion cancelled') return # Delete alarms cloudwatch.delete_alarms(AlarmNames=alarms_to_delete) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ Deleted {len(alarms_to_delete)} alarms for {resource_name}' )) # Log cleanup action from django.contrib.admin.models import LogEntry, DELETION from django.contrib.contenttypes.models import ContentType from django.contrib.auth import get_user_model User = get_user_model() admin_user = User.objects.filter(is_superuser=True).first() if admin_user: LogEntry.objects.create( user=admin_user, content_type=None, object_id=resource_name, object_repr=f'CloudWatch Alarms for {resource_name}', action_flag=DELETION, change_message=f'Deleted {len(alarms_to_delete)} alarms' ) # Usage: python manage.py delete_resource_alarms OldMicroservice
Example 5: Automated Alarm Lifecycle Management

Implement automated cleanup of alarms based on age and activity.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete alarms that haven't been updated in 90 days
NINETY_DAYS_AGO=$(date -u -d '90 days ago' +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

# Get all alarms
ALL_ALARMS=$(aws cloudwatch describe-alarms --region us-east-1 --output json)

# Find stale alarms (not updated in 90 days)
STALE_ALARMS=$(echo "$ALL_ALARMS" | jq -r \
  --arg cutoff "$NINETY_DAYS_AGO" \
  '.MetricAlarms[] | select(.StateUpdatedTimestamp < $cutoff) | .AlarmName')

if [ -z "$STALE_ALARMS" ]; then
    echo "No stale alarms found"
    exit 0
fi

# Convert to array
STALE_ARRAY=($STALE_ALARMS)
echo "Found ${#STALE_ARRAY[@]} stale alarms (>90 days inactive)"

# Delete stale alarms
aws cloudwatch delete-alarms \
  --alarm-names ${STALE_ARRAY[@]} \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Cleaned up ${#STALE_ARRAY[@]} stale alarms"
Found 3 stale alarms (>90 days inactive) { "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "e5f6g7h8-i9j0-1234-5678-901234efghij", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Cleaned up 3 stale alarms Removed inactive alarms: LegacyMetric1, OldServiceCheck, DeprecatedAlert
# Django management command for alarm lifecycle management from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Automated alarm lifecycle management' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( '--days-inactive', type=int, default=90, help='Delete alarms inactive for this many days' ) parser.add_argument( '--dry-run', action='store_true', help='Show what would be deleted' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') days = options['days_inactive'] cutoff_date = timezone.now() - timedelta(days=days) # Get all alarms response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms() all_alarms = response['MetricAlarms'] stale_alarms = [] alarm_details = [] for alarm in all_alarms: state_updated = alarm['StateUpdatedTimestamp'] # Make timezone-aware if needed if state_updated.tzinfo is None: state_updated = timezone.make_aware(state_updated) if state_updated < cutoff_date: stale_alarms.append(alarm['AlarmName']) days_inactive = (timezone.now() - state_updated).days alarm_details.append({ 'name': alarm['AlarmName'], 'last_update': state_updated, 'days_inactive': days_inactive, 'state': alarm['StateValue'] }) if not stale_alarms: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'✓ No stale alarms found (inactive >{days} days)' )) return # Display stale alarms self.stdout.write(f'\nStale Alarms (inactive >{days} days):') self.stdout.write('-' * 60) for detail in sorted(alarm_details, key=lambda x: x['days_inactive'], reverse=True): self.stdout.write( f" {detail['name']:<40} {detail['days_inactive']:>3} days | {detail['state']}" ) self.stdout.write(f'\nTotal stale alarms: {len(stale_alarms)}') if options['dry_run']: self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( '\n[DRY RUN] No alarms were deleted' )) return # Confirm deletion confirm = input(f'\nDelete {len(stale_alarms)} stale alarms? (yes/no): ') if confirm.lower() != 'yes': self.stdout.write('Cleanup cancelled') return # Delete stale alarms cloudwatch.delete_alarms(AlarmNames=stale_alarms) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS( f'\n✓ Deleted {len(stale_alarms)} stale alarms' )) # Send notification email from django.core.mail import send_mail email_body = f"CloudWatch Alarm Lifecycle Cleanup Report\n\n" email_body += f"Deleted {len(stale_alarms)} alarms inactive for >{days} days\n\n" for detail in alarm_details: email_body += f"- {detail['name']} ({detail['days_inactive']} days inactive)\n" send_mail( subject=f'CloudWatch: Cleaned up {len(stale_alarms)} stale alarms', message=email_body, from_email='monitoring@anihpj.com', recipient_list=['ops-team@anihpj.com'], fail_silently=True ) # Schedule monthly: 0 0 1 * * python manage.py alarm_lifecycle --days-inactive 90

set-alarm-state

Temporarily sets the state of an alarm for testing purposes. Essential for validating alarm actions and notification workflows.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --state-value {state} \
  --state-reason {reason}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the alarm
  • --state-value (string, required): State to set. Values: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA
  • --state-reason (string, required): Reason for state change (human-readable description)
  • --state-reason-data (string): Machine-readable JSON data about the state change
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success
5 Detailed CloudWatch set-alarm-state Examples
Example 1: Test Alarm Notification Workflow

Manually trigger an alarm to test SNS notifications and alert workflows.

#!/bin/bash
# Test alarm notification by setting state to ALARM
aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
  --alarm-name "HighAPIResponseTime" \
  --state-value ALARM \
  --state-reason "Manual test of alarm notification workflow" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Alarm state set to ALARM for testing"
echo "Check SNS notifications, PagerDuty, Slack, etc."
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "f6g7h8i9-j0k1-2345-6789-012345fghijk", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Alarm state set to ALARM for testing Check SNS notifications, PagerDuty, Slack, etc. Notification channels should receive test alert within 1-2 minutes
# Django management command to test alarm notifications from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 import time class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Test CloudWatch alarm notification workflow' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( 'alarm_name', type=str, help='Name of alarm to test' ) parser.add_argument( '--duration', type=int, default=60, help='Duration to keep alarm in ALARM state (seconds)' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') alarm_name = options['alarm_name'] duration = options['duration'] # Get current alarm state response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNames=[alarm_name]) if not response['MetricAlarms']: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f'Alarm "{alarm_name}" not found')) return alarm = response['MetricAlarms'][0] original_state = alarm['StateValue'] self.stdout.write(f'\nTesting alarm: {alarm_name}') self.stdout.write(f'Original state: {original_state}') # Set to ALARM state self.stdout.write('\nSetting alarm to ALARM state...') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='ALARM', StateReason='Testing alarm notification workflow from Django management command' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING('✓ Alarm triggered')) self.stdout.write(f'Waiting {duration} seconds for notifications...') # Wait for notifications time.sleep(duration) # Reset to original state self.stdout.write('\nResetting alarm to original state...') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue=original_state, StateReason='Test completed - returning to original state' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS(f'✓ Test completed')) self.stdout.write(f'Alarm returned to {original_state} state') self.stdout.write('\nVerify that you received notifications through all configured channels') # Usage: python manage.py test_alarm_notifications HighAPIResponseTime --duration 60
Example 2: Simulate Alarm Recovery

Test alarm recovery by setting state back to OK after an incident.

#!/bin/bash
# Simulate alarm recovery
aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
  --alarm-name "DatabaseSlowQueries" \
  --state-value OK \
  --state-reason "Simulating recovery after performance optimization" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Alarm state set to OK (recovery simulation)"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "g7h8i9j0-k1l2-3456-7890-123456ghijkl", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Alarm state set to OK (recovery simulation) Test recovery notifications and auto-remediation workflows
# Django utility for alarm state simulation from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Simulate alarm state transitions for testing' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument('alarm_name', type=str) parser.add_argument( '--scenario', type=str, choices=['incident', 'recovery', 'flapping'], default='incident', help='Scenario to simulate' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') alarm_name = options['alarm_name'] scenario = options['scenario'] if scenario == 'incident': # Simulate incident self.stdout.write('Simulating incident...') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='ALARM', StateReason='[SIMULATION] Threshold exceeded - incident detected' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR('✓ Incident simulated (ALARM)')) elif scenario == 'recovery': # Simulate recovery self.stdout.write('Simulating recovery...') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='OK', StateReason='[SIMULATION] Metrics returned to normal levels' ) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('✓ Recovery simulated (OK)')) elif scenario == 'flapping': # Simulate flapping alarm import time self.stdout.write('Simulating flapping alarm...') for i in range(3): # Set to ALARM cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='ALARM', StateReason=f'[SIMULATION] Flapping cycle {i+1} - ALARM' ) self.stdout.write(f' Cycle {i+1}: ALARM') time.sleep(2) # Set to OK cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='OK', StateReason=f'[SIMULATION] Flapping cycle {i+1} - OK' ) self.stdout.write(f' Cycle {i+1}: OK') time.sleep(2) self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING( '✓ Flapping simulation complete (3 cycles)' )) self.stdout.write('\nCheck monitoring systems for notifications') # Usage: python manage.py simulate_alarm DatabaseSlowQueries --scenario recovery
Example 3: Test Insufficient Data State Handling

Set alarm to INSUFFICIENT_DATA state to test handling of missing metrics.

#!/bin/bash
# Test insufficient data handling
aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
  --alarm-name "JobPostQueueDepth" \
  --state-value INSUFFICIENT_DATA \
  --state-reason "Testing handling of missing metric data" \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Alarm state set to INSUFFICIENT_DATA"
echo "Verify dashboard displays and alerting behavior"
{ "ResponseMetadata": { "RequestId": "h8i9j0k1-l2m3-4567-8901-234567hijklm", "HTTPStatusCode": 200 } } ✓ Alarm state set to INSUFFICIENT_DATA Verify dashboard displays and alerting behavior Check that alarm doesn't trigger false alerts for missing data
# Django testing utility for alarm edge cases from django.test import TestCase from django.core.management import call_command import boto3 from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock class AlarmStateTestCase(TestCase): """Test alarm behavior with different states""" def setUp(self): self.cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') self.test_alarm = 'TestAlarm' @patch('boto3.client') def test_insufficient_data_state(self, mock_boto): """Test alarm behavior when data is missing""" mock_cloudwatch = MagicMock() mock_boto.return_value = mock_cloudwatch # Set alarm to INSUFFICIENT_DATA mock_cloudwatch.set_alarm_state.return_value = { 'ResponseMetadata': {'HTTPStatusCode': 200} } cloudwatch = mock_boto('cloudwatch') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=self.test_alarm, StateValue='INSUFFICIENT_DATA', StateReason='Testing missing data handling' ) # Verify call was made mock_cloudwatch.set_alarm_state.assert_called_once() # Verify no false alarms triggered # (In real implementation, check notification system) def test_alarm_state_transitions(self): """Test complete alarm lifecycle""" states = ['OK', 'ALARM', 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA', 'OK'] for state in states: self.cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=self.test_alarm, StateValue=state, StateReason=f'Testing transition to {state}' ) # Verify state was set response = self.cloudwatch.describe_alarms( AlarmNames=[self.test_alarm] ) if response['MetricAlarms']: current_state = response['MetricAlarms'][0]['StateValue'] self.assertEqual(current_state, state, f'Expected {state}, got {current_state}') # Run: python manage.py test jobpost.tests.AlarmStateTestCase
Example 4: Automated Alarm Testing During Deployment

Test alarm configuration as part of CI/CD pipeline before production deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Pre-deployment alarm testing script
ALARMS_TO_TEST=(
    "HighAPIResponseTime"
    "DatabaseSlowQueries"
    "LowJobPostActivity"
)

echo "=== Pre-Deployment Alarm Testing ==="
for ALARM in "${ALARMS_TO_TEST[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "Testing: $ALARM"
    
    # Set to ALARM
    aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
      --alarm-name "$ALARM" \
      --state-value ALARM \
      --state-reason "[CI/CD] Pre-deployment alarm test" \
      --region us-east-1
    
    echo "  ✓ Triggered ALARM state"
    sleep 5
    
    # Reset to OK
    aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
      --alarm-name "$ALARM" \
      --state-value OK \
      --state-reason "[CI/CD] Test completed - resetting" \
      --region us-east-1
    
    echo "  ✓ Reset to OK state"
done

echo ""
echo "✓ All alarm tests completed"
echo "Verify notifications were received for all alarms"
=== Pre-Deployment Alarm Testing === Testing: HighAPIResponseTime ✓ Triggered ALARM state ✓ Reset to OK state Testing: DatabaseSlowQueries ✓ Triggered ALARM state ✓ Reset to OK state Testing: LowJobPostActivity ✓ Triggered ALARM state ✓ Reset to OK state ✓ All alarm tests completed Verify notifications were received for all alarms Test Results: - 3 alarms tested - 6 state transitions successful - All notifications delivered - Ready for production deployment
# Django management command for CI/CD alarm testing from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand import boto3 import time import requests class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Test alarm notifications in CI/CD pipeline' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( '--webhook-url', type=str, help='Webhook URL to report test results' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') # Define critical alarms to test critical_alarms = [ 'HighAPIResponseTime', 'DatabaseSlowQueries', 'CriticalSystemErrors', 'LowJobPostActivity' ] test_results = { 'passed': [], 'failed': [], 'total': len(critical_alarms) } self.stdout.write('=== CI/CD Alarm Testing ===\n') for alarm_name in critical_alarms: self.stdout.write(f'Testing: {alarm_name}') try: # Get original state response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNames=[alarm_name]) if not response['MetricAlarms']: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f' ✗ Alarm not found')) test_results['failed'].append(alarm_name) continue original_state = response['MetricAlarms'][0]['StateValue'] # Test ALARM state cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='ALARM', StateReason='[CI/CD] Automated alarm functionality test' ) self.stdout.write(' ✓ ALARM state triggered') time.sleep(3) # Reset to original state cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue=original_state, StateReason='[CI/CD] Test completed - reset to original state' ) self.stdout.write(f' ✓ Reset to {original_state}') test_results['passed'].append(alarm_name) except Exception as e: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f' ✗ Error: {str(e)}')) test_results['failed'].append(alarm_name) # Report results self.stdout.write('\n=== Test Results ===') self.stdout.write(f"Passed: {len(test_results['passed'])}/{test_results['total']}") self.stdout.write(f"Failed: {len(test_results['failed'])}/{test_results['total']}") # Send webhook notification if options['webhook_url']: payload = { 'test_type': 'cloudwatch_alarm_testing', 'results': test_results, 'status': 'success' if not test_results['failed'] else 'failed' } try: requests.post(options['webhook_url'], json=payload, timeout=10) self.stdout.write('\n✓ Results sent to webhook') except Exception as e: self.stdout.write(f'\n✗ Webhook failed: {e}') # Exit with error code if tests failed if test_results['failed']: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR('\n✗ Some alarm tests failed!')) exit(1) else: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('\n✓ All alarm tests passed!')) # CI/CD usage: python manage.py test_alarms_cicd --webhook-url https://ci.example.com/hooks
Example 5: Maintenance Mode - Suppress Alarms Temporarily

Set alarms to OK state during planned maintenance to avoid false alerts.

#!/bin/bash
# Enter maintenance mode - suppress alarms
MAINTENANCE_START=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC")

# List of alarms to suppress
ALARMS=(
    "HighAPIResponseTime"
    "DatabaseSlowQueries"
    "LowJobPostActivity"
)

echo "=== Entering Maintenance Mode ==="
echo "Start time: $MAINTENANCE_START"
echo ""

for ALARM in "${ALARMS[@]}"; do
    aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
      --alarm-name "$ALARM" \
      --state-value OK \
      --state-reason "Maintenance Mode: Suppressing alerts during scheduled maintenance starting $MAINTENANCE_START" \
      --region us-east-1
    
    echo "✓ Suppressed: $ALARM"
done

echo ""
echo "✓ Maintenance mode activated"
echo "Remember to exit maintenance mode after work is complete!"
=== Entering Maintenance Mode === Start time: 2026-01-17 14:00:00 UTC ✓ Suppressed: HighAPIResponseTime ✓ Suppressed: DatabaseSlowQueries ✓ Suppressed: LowJobPostActivity ✓ Maintenance mode activated Remember to exit maintenance mode after work is complete! All specified alarms set to OK state Monitoring notifications suppressed during maintenance window
# Django maintenance mode management from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand from django.core.cache import cache from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import boto3 class Command(BaseCommand): help = 'Manage CloudWatch alarm maintenance mode' def add_arguments(self, parser): parser.add_argument( 'action', type=str, choices=['enter', 'exit', 'status'], help='Maintenance mode action' ) parser.add_argument( '--duration', type=int, default=60, help='Maintenance duration in minutes' ) parser.add_argument( '--alarms', nargs='+', help='Specific alarms to suppress (default: all critical alarms)' ) def handle(self, *args, **options): cloudwatch = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name='us-east-1') action = options['action'] # Default critical alarms default_alarms = [ 'HighAPIResponseTime', 'DatabaseSlowQueries', 'CriticalSystemErrors', 'LowJobPostActivity', 'HighErrorRate' ] alarms = options['alarms'] or default_alarms if action == 'enter': # Enter maintenance mode start_time = timezone.now() end_time = start_time + timedelta(minutes=options['duration']) self.stdout.write('=== Entering Maintenance Mode ===') self.stdout.write(f'Start: {start_time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")}') self.stdout.write(f'End: {end_time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")}') self.stdout.write(f'Duration: {options["duration"]} minutes\n') # Store original states original_states = {} for alarm_name in alarms: try: # Get current state response = cloudwatch.describe_alarms(AlarmNames=[alarm_name]) if response['MetricAlarms']: original_state = response['MetricAlarms'][0]['StateValue'] original_states[alarm_name] = original_state # Set to OK cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue='OK', StateReason=f'Maintenance Mode: Scheduled maintenance until {end_time.strftime("%H:%M:%S UTC")}' ) self.stdout.write(f'✓ Suppressed: {alarm_name} (was {original_state})') except Exception as e: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f'✗ Failed: {alarm_name} - {e}')) # Store maintenance info in cache cache.set('maintenance_mode', { 'active': True, 'start_time': start_time.isoformat(), 'end_time': end_time.isoformat(), 'original_states': original_states, 'alarms': alarms }, timeout=options['duration'] * 60) self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('\n✓ Maintenance mode activated')) elif action == 'exit': # Exit maintenance mode maintenance_info = cache.get('maintenance_mode') if not maintenance_info or not maintenance_info.get('active'): self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING('No active maintenance mode')) return self.stdout.write('=== Exiting Maintenance Mode ===\n') original_states = maintenance_info.get('original_states', {}) for alarm_name in maintenance_info['alarms']: try: original_state = original_states.get(alarm_name, 'INSUFFICIENT_DATA') cloudwatch.set_alarm_state( AlarmName=alarm_name, StateValue=original_state, StateReason='Maintenance Mode: Scheduled maintenance completed - restoring normal monitoring' ) self.stdout.write(f'✓ Restored: {alarm_name} to {original_state}') except Exception as e: self.stdout.write(self.style.ERROR(f'✗ Failed: {alarm_name} - {e}')) # Clear maintenance mode cache.delete('maintenance_mode') self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('\n✓ Maintenance mode deactivated')) elif action == 'status': # Check maintenance status maintenance_info = cache.get('maintenance_mode') if maintenance_info and maintenance_info.get('active'): self.stdout.write(self.style.WARNING('=== Maintenance Mode ACTIVE ===')) self.stdout.write(f"Start: {maintenance_info['start_time']}") self.stdout.write(f"End: {maintenance_info['end_time']}") self.stdout.write(f"Suppressed alarms: {len(maintenance_info['alarms'])}") else: self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS('Maintenance mode: INACTIVE')) # Usage: # python manage.py maintenance_mode enter --duration 120 # python manage.py maintenance_mode status # python manage.py maintenance_mode exit

list-metrics

Lists available metrics in CloudWatch. Use this to discover what metrics are being published and explore metric dimensions.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch list-metrics \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --dimensions Name={name},Value={value}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string): Filter metrics by namespace
  • --metric-name (string): Filter by specific metric name
  • --dimensions (list): Filter by dimension key-value pairs
Return Value
  • Metrics (Array): List of metrics matching filters
  • Namespace, MetricName, Dimensions: Metric identification

get-metric-data

Retrieves metric data using metric queries. More powerful than get-metric-statistics with support for math expressions and multiple metrics.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-metric-data \
  --metric-data-queries file://queries.json \
  --start-time {start} \
  --end-time {end}
Parameters Description
  • --metric-data-queries (list, required): Array of metric query specifications
  • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start of time range
  • --end-time (timestamp, required): End of time range
  • --scan-by (string): Order of data points. Values: TimestampDescending, TimestampAscending
Return Value
  • MetricDataResults (Array): Results for each query with Id, Label, Timestamps, Values

put-dashboard

Creates or updates a CloudWatch dashboard with custom visualizations. Essential for creating operational dashboards and monitoring views.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name {name} \
  --dashboard-body file://dashboard.json
Parameters Description
  • --dashboard-name (string, required): Name of the dashboard
  • --dashboard-body (string, required): JSON specification of dashboard widgets
Return Value
  • DashboardValidationMessages (Array): Validation warnings if any

get-dashboard

Retrieves dashboard configuration. Use this to export dashboards or review widget definitions.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name {name}
Parameters Description
  • --dashboard-name (string, required): Name of the dashboard to retrieve
Return Value
  • DashboardArn (string): ARN of the dashboard
  • DashboardBody (string): JSON specification of dashboard
  • DashboardName (string): Name of the dashboard

list-dashboards

Lists all CloudWatch dashboards in the current account and region.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch list-dashboards \
  --dashboard-name-prefix {prefix}
Parameters Description
  • --dashboard-name-prefix (string): Filter dashboards by name prefix
Return Value
  • DashboardEntries (Array): List of dashboards with DashboardName, DashboardArn, LastModified, Size

delete-dashboards

Deletes specified CloudWatch dashboards.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch delete-dashboards \
  --dashboard-names {name1} {name2}
Parameters Description
  • --dashboard-names (list, required): Names of dashboards to delete
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on successful deletion

get-metric-widget-image

Returns a snapshot graph for one or more metrics as a PNG image. Useful for embedding metrics in reports or dashboards.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-metric-widget-image \
  --metric-widget '{MetricWidget JSON}' \
  --output-format png
Parameters Description
  • --metric-widget (string, required): JSON specification of the metric widget
  • --output-format (string): Format of the image. Default: png
Return Value
  • MetricWidgetImage (blob): Base64-encoded PNG image

put-composite-alarm

Creates or updates a composite alarm that aggregates the states of multiple alarms using boolean logic.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-composite-alarm \
  --alarm-name {name} \
  --alarm-rule {rule} \
  --actions-enabled
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the composite alarm
  • --alarm-rule (string, required): Expression combining other alarms (e.g., "ALARM(alarm1) OR ALARM(alarm2)")
  • --actions-enabled (boolean): Whether actions should be executed when alarm state changes
  • --alarm-actions (list): Actions to execute when alarm enters ALARM state
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

describe-alarms-for-metric

Retrieves all alarms for a specified metric. Useful for finding what alarms monitor a particular metric.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms-for-metric \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --dimensions Name={name},Value={value}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric
  • --dimensions (list): Dimensions of the metric
Return Value
  • MetricAlarms (Array): List of alarms monitoring this metric

describe-alarm-history

Retrieves the history of alarm state changes, providing audit trail of when alarms triggered.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch describe-alarm-history \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --start-date {timestamp} \
  --end-date {timestamp}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-name (string): Name of the alarm to retrieve history for
  • --start-date (timestamp): Start date for history retrieval
  • --end-date (timestamp): End date for history retrieval
  • --history-item-type (string): Type of history items. Values: ConfigurationUpdate, StateUpdate, Action
Return Value
  • AlarmHistoryItems (Array): List of alarm history entries with timestamps

enable-alarm-actions

Enables actions for specified alarms. Use this to resume alarm notifications after maintenance.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch enable-alarm-actions \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name1} {alarm-name2}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-names (list, required): Names of alarms to enable actions for
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

disable-alarm-actions

Disables actions for specified alarms. Use this during maintenance to suppress alarm notifications.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch disable-alarm-actions \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name1} {alarm-name2}
Parameters Description
  • --alarm-names (list, required): Names of alarms to disable actions for
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

put-anomaly-detector

Creates an anomaly detector that uses machine learning to detect anomalous metric behavior.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-anomaly-detector \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --stat {statistic}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric
  • --stat (string, required): Statistic to apply (Average, Sum, etc.)
  • --dimensions (list): Dimensions of the metric
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

describe-anomaly-detectors

Lists anomaly detectors in your account and their configurations.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch describe-anomaly-detectors \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string): Filter by namespace
  • --metric-name (string): Filter by metric name
Return Value
  • AnomalyDetectors (Array): List of anomaly detector configurations

delete-anomaly-detector

Deletes an anomaly detector.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch delete-anomaly-detector \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --stat {statistic}
Parameters Description
  • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric
  • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric
  • --stat (string, required): Statistic used by the detector
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

put-metric-stream

Creates or updates a metric stream that sends CloudWatch metrics to a destination like Kinesis Firehose.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name} \
  --firehose-arn {firehose-arn} \
  --role-arn {role-arn} \
  --output-format json
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream
  • --firehose-arn (string, required): ARN of Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream
  • --role-arn (string, required): IAM role ARN for CloudWatch to assume
  • --output-format (string, required): Format for streamed metrics (json or opentelemetry0.7)
Return Value
  • Arn (string): ARN of the metric stream

list-metric-streams

Lists metric streams in your account.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch list-metric-streams
Return Value
  • Entries (Array): List of metric streams with Name, Arn, State, CreationDate

get-metric-stream

Retrieves details about a specific metric stream.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name}
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream
Return Value
  • Arn, Name, State, FirehoseArn, RoleArn, OutputFormat: Stream configuration details

start-metric-streams

Starts streaming metrics for specified metric streams.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch start-metric-streams \
  --names {stream-name1} {stream-name2}
Parameters Description
  • --names (list, required): Names of metric streams to start
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

stop-metric-streams

Stops streaming metrics for specified metric streams.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch stop-metric-streams \
  --names {stream-name1} {stream-name2}
Parameters Description
  • --names (list, required): Names of metric streams to stop
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

delete-metric-stream

Deletes a metric stream permanently.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch delete-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name}
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream to delete
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

put-insight-rule

Creates a Contributor Insights rule that analyzes log data to identify top contributors to system issues.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch put-insight-rule \
  --rule-name {rule-name} \
  --rule-state ENABLED \
  --rule-definition '{Rule JSON}'
Parameters Description
  • --rule-name (string, required): Name of the Contributor Insights rule
  • --rule-state (string, required): State of the rule (ENABLED or DISABLED)
  • --rule-definition (string, required): JSON defining the rule logic
Return Value
  • ResponseMetadata (Object): HTTP 200 on success

describe-insight-rules

Lists Contributor Insights rules in your account.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch describe-insight-rules
Return Value
  • InsightRules (Array): List of insight rules with Name, State, Schema

get-insight-rule-report

Retrieves the report generated by a Contributor Insights rule.

Syntax
aws cloudwatch get-insight-rule-report \
