5 Detailed ECR batch-get-image Examples
Example 1: Get anihpj Image Manifest
#!/bin/bash
# Get image manifest for latest tag
aws ecr batch-get-image \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-ids imageTag=latest \
  --query 'images[0].imageManifest' \
  --output text | jq .
{ "schemaVersion": 2, "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json", "config": { "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.container.image.v1+json", "size": 7023, "digest": "sha256:abc123..." }, "layers": [ { "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.image.rootfs.diff.tar.gzip", "size": 52000000, "digest": "sha256:layer1..." } ] }
# Django Models Integration - Get Image Manifest from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import json # Get image manifest for latest tag repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') latest = repository.skills.filter(name='latest').first() manifest = { 'schemaVersion': 2, 'mediaType': 'application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json', 'config': {'mediaType': 'application/vnd.docker.container.image.v1+json', 'size': 7023}, 'layers': [{'mediaType': 'application/vnd.docker.image.rootfs.diff.tar.gzip', 'size': 52000000}] } print(json.dumps(manifest, indent=2)) print(f"<Skills name={latest.name if latest else 'latest'} layers=1>") # Output: <Skills name=latest layers=1>
Example 2: Get Multiple anihpj Images
#!/bin/bash
# Get multiple images at once
aws ecr batch-get-image \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-ids imageTag=latest imageTag=v1.0.0 \
  --query 'images[*].[imageId.imageTag,imageId.imageDigest]' \
  --output table
--------------------------------------------------- | BatchGetImage | +--------+----------------------------------------+ | latest | sha256:abc123... | | v1.0.0 | sha256:def456... | +--------+----------------------------------------+
# Django Models Integration - Get Multiple Images from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import hashlib # Get multiple images at once repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') images = repository.skills.filter(name__in=['latest', 'v1.0.0']) print("---------------------------------------------------") print("| BatchGetImage |") print("+--------+----------------------------------------+") for img in images: digest = hashlib.sha256(f"{repository.title}:{img.name}".encode()).hexdigest()[:10] print(f"| {img.name:<6} | sha256:{digest}...{' ' * 20} |") print("+--------+----------------------------------------+") print(f"<QuerySet [{', '.join([i.name for i in images])}]>") # Output: <QuerySet [latest, v1.0.0]>

batch-delete-image

Deletes a list of specified images within a repository.

Syntax
aws ecr batch-delete-image \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-ids {image-ids}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-ids - Image identifiers to delete
Return Value

Returns imageIds for deleted images and failures array for any errors.

5 Detailed ECR batch-delete-image Examples
Example 1: Delete Specific anihpj Image Tag
#!/bin/bash
# Delete old image tag
aws ecr batch-delete-image \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-ids imageTag=v0.8.0

echo "anihpj v0.8.0 image deleted"
{ "imageIds": [ { "imageDigest": "sha256:abc123...", "imageTag": "v0.8.0" } ], "failures": [] } anihpj v0.8.0 image deleted
# Django Models Integration - Delete Specific Image Tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Delete old image tag repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') old_image = repository.skills.filter(name='v0.8.0').first() if old_image: image_name = old_image.name repository.skills.remove(old_image) old_image.delete() print(f'{{"imageIds": [{{"imageTag": "{image_name}"}}], "failures": []}}') print(f"anihpj {image_name} image deleted") print(f"<Skills deleted=v0.8.0>") # Output: <Skills deleted=v0.8.0>
Example 2: Clean Up Untagged anihpj Images
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all untagged images
echo "Cleaning up untagged anihpj images..."

DIGESTS=$(aws ecr list-images \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --filter tagStatus=UNTAGGED \
  --query 'imageIds[*].imageDigest' \
  --output text)

for DIGEST in $DIGESTS; do
  aws ecr batch-delete-image \
    --repository-name anihpj-django \
    --image-ids imageDigest=$DIGEST
done

echo "anihpj untagged images cleaned up"
Cleaning up untagged anihpj images... { "imageIds": [{"imageDigest": "sha256:abc123..."}], "failures": [] } { "imageIds": [{"imageDigest": "sha256:def456..."}], "failures": [] } anihpj untagged images cleaned up
# Django Models Integration - Clean Up Untagged Images from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Delete all untagged images repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') print("Cleaning up untagged anihpj images...") # Find and delete orphaned/untagged images untagged = Skills.objects.filter(name__startswith='orphan') deleted_count = 0 for img in untagged: print(f'{{"imageIds": [{{"imageDigest": "sha256:..."}}], "failures": []}}') img.delete() deleted_count += 1 print("anihpj untagged images cleaned up") print(f"<QuerySet deleted={deleted_count}>") # Output: <QuerySet deleted=2>
Example 3: Delete Old anihpj Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Delete images older than specified version
REPO="anihpj-django"
KEEP_VERSIONS=("latest" "v1.0.0" "v0.9.0")

echo "Deleting old anihpj image versions..."

aws ecr list-images \
  --repository-name $REPO \
  --query 'imageIds[*].imageTag' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read TAG; do
    if [[ ! " ${KEEP_VERSIONS[@]} " =~ " ${TAG} " ]] && [ -n "$TAG" ]; then
      echo "Deleting: $TAG"
      aws ecr batch-delete-image \
        --repository-name $REPO \
        --image-ids imageTag=$TAG
    fi
done

echo "Old anihpj versions deleted"
Deleting old anihpj image versions... Deleting: v0.7.0 Deleting: v0.6.0 Old anihpj versions deleted
# Django Models Integration - Delete Old Versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Delete images older than specified version repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') keep_versions = ['latest', 'v1.0.0', 'v0.9.0'] print("Deleting old anihpj image versions...") images = repository.skills.exclude(name__in=keep_versions) for img in images: print(f"Deleting: {img.name}") repository.skills.remove(img) img.delete() print("Old anihpj versions deleted") print(f"<QuerySet deleted_versions={images.count()} kept={len(keep_versions)}>") # Output: <QuerySet deleted_versions=2 kept=3>

put-image

Creates or updates the image manifest and tags associated with an image.

Syntax
aws ecr put-image \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-manifest {manifest} \
  --image-tag {tag}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-manifest - Docker image manifest JSON
  • --image-tag - Tag for the image
Return Value

Returns image details with imageId including imageDigest and imageTag.

5 Detailed ECR put-image Examples
Example 1: Retag anihpj Image
#!/bin/bash
# Retag existing image with new tag
REPO="anihpj-django"
SOURCE_TAG="latest"
NEW_TAG="production"

# Get the manifest
MANIFEST=$(aws ecr batch-get-image \
  --repository-name $REPO \
  --image-ids imageTag=$SOURCE_TAG \
  --query 'images[0].imageManifest' \
  --output text)

# Put with new tag
aws ecr put-image \
  --repository-name $REPO \
  --image-tag $NEW_TAG \
  --image-manifest "$MANIFEST"

echo "anihpj image retagged as $NEW_TAG"
{ "image": { "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "imageId": { "imageDigest": "sha256:abc123...", "imageTag": "production" }, "imageManifest": "..." } } anihpj image retagged as production
# Django Models Integration - Retag Image from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Retag existing image with new tag repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') source_tag = 'latest' new_tag = 'production' # Get source image and create new tag pointing to same image source_image = repository.skills.filter(name=source_tag).first() new_image, created = Skills.objects.get_or_create(name=new_tag) repository.skills.add(new_image) print(f'repositoryName: {repository.title}') print(f'imageTag: {new_tag}') print(f"anihpj image retagged as {new_tag}") print(f"<Skills name={new_tag} created={created}>") # Output: <Skills name=production created=True>

put-image-tag-mutability

Updates the image tag mutability settings for the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr put-image-tag-mutability \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-tag-mutability {MUTABLE|IMMUTABLE}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-tag-mutability - MUTABLE or IMMUTABLE
Return Value

Returns repositoryName and imageTagMutability setting.

5 Detailed ECR put-image-tag-mutability Examples
Example 1: Make anihpj Tags Immutable
#!/bin/bash
# Prevent tag overwriting
aws ecr put-image-tag-mutability \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-tag-mutability IMMUTABLE

echo "anihpj repository tags are now immutable"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "imageTagMutability": "IMMUTABLE" } anihpj repository tags are now immutable
# Django Models Integration - Make Tags Immutable from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Prevent tag overwriting repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Store mutability setting (using description) repository.description = (repository.description or '') + '\nimageTagMutability: IMMUTABLE' repository.save() print(f'registryId: 123456789012') print(f'repositoryName: {repository.title}') print(f'imageTagMutability: IMMUTABLE') print("anihpj repository tags are now immutable") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} mutability=IMMUTABLE>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django mutability=IMMUTABLE>
Example 2: Enable Tag Overwrites
#!/bin/bash
# Allow tag overwriting for dev repo
aws ecr put-image-tag-mutability \
  --repository-name anihpj-django-dev \
  --image-tag-mutability MUTABLE

echo "anihpj dev repository allows tag overwrites"
{ "repositoryName": "anihpj-django-dev", "imageTagMutability": "MUTABLE" } anihpj dev repository allows tag overwrites
# Django Models Integration - Enable Tag Overwrites from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Allow tag overwriting for dev repo repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django-dev') # Store mutability setting repository.description = (repository.description or '') + '\nimageTagMutability: MUTABLE' repository.save() print(f'repositoryName: {repository.title}') print(f'imageTagMutability: MUTABLE') print("anihpj dev repository allows tag overwrites") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} mutability=MUTABLE>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django-dev mutability=MUTABLE>

get-authorization-token

Retrieves an authorization token. An authorization token represents your IAM authentication credentials and can be used to access any Amazon ECR registry.

Syntax
aws ecr get-authorization-token
Parameters Description
  • --registry-ids - (Optional) List of registry IDs
Return Value

Returns authorizationData with authorizationToken, expiresAt, and proxyEndpoint.

5 Detailed ECR get-authorization-token Examples
Example 1: Get Authorization Token
#!/bin/bash
# Get ECR authorization token
aws ecr get-authorization-token \
  --query 'authorizationData[0].authorizationToken' \
  --output text
QVdTOmV5SndZWGxzYjJGa0lqb2lkR1Z6ZENKOQ==
# Django Models Integration - Get Authorization Token from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import base64 import secrets # Get ECR authorization token token = secrets.token_bytes(32) auth_token = base64.b64encode(f"AWS:{base64.b64encode(token).decode()}".encode()).decode() print(auth_token[:40] + "...") print(f"<Token expires_in=12h>") # Output: <Token expires_in=12h>
Example 2: Docker Login with Token
#!/bin/bash
# Login to ECR for anihpj
REGION="us-east-1"
ACCOUNT="123456789012"

TOKEN=$(aws ecr get-authorization-token \
  --query 'authorizationData[0].authorizationToken' \
  --output text | base64 -d | cut -d: -f2)

echo $TOKEN | docker login \
  --username AWS \
  --password-stdin \
  $ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com

echo "Logged in to anihpj ECR registry"
WARNING! Your password will be stored unencrypted... Login Succeeded Logged in to anihpj ECR registry
# Django Models Integration - Docker Login with Token from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import base64 # Login to ECR for anihpj region = 'us-east-1' account = '123456789012' # Simulate token retrieval token = 'simulated_ecr_token' registry = f"{account}.dkr.ecr.{region}.amazonaws.com" # Store login status user = User.objects.first() login_record = Skills.objects.create(name=f'ecr_login:{registry}') user.skills.add(login_record) print("Login Succeeded") print(f"Logged in to anihpj ECR registry") print(f"<User logged_in=True registry={registry[:30]}...>") # Output: <User logged_in=True registry=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east...>

get-login-password

Retrieves an authorization token and decodes the password for Docker login. This is the preferred method for authentication.

Syntax
aws ecr get-login-password
Parameters Description
  • --region - (Optional) AWS region
Return Value

Returns the decoded password for Docker login authentication.

5 Detailed ECR get-login-password Examples
Example 1: Simple Docker Login for anihpj
#!/bin/bash
# Recommended ECR login method
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
  docker login \
    --username AWS \
    --password-stdin \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

echo "Ready to push anihpj images"
Login Succeeded Ready to push anihpj images
# Django Models Integration - Simple Docker Login from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Recommended ECR login method region = 'us-east-1' account = '123456789012' registry = f"{account}.dkr.ecr.{region}.amazonaws.com" # Store login session user = User.objects.first() login_skill = Skills.objects.create(name=f'ecr_authenticated:{region}') user.skills.add(login_skill) print("Login Succeeded") print("Ready to push anihpj images") print(f"<User authenticated=True registry={registry[:30]}...>") # Output: <User authenticated=True registry=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-ea...>
Example 2: Complete anihpj Docker Push Flow
#!/bin/bash
# Full push workflow for anihpj Django app
REGION="us-east-1"
ACCOUNT="123456789012"
REPO="anihpj-django"
TAG="v1.2.0"

# Login to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region $REGION | \
  docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
    $ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com

# Build the image
docker build -t $REPO:$TAG .

# Tag for ECR
docker tag $REPO:$TAG $ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com/$REPO:$TAG

# Push to ECR
docker push $ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com/$REPO:$TAG

echo "anihpj $TAG pushed to ECR"
Login Succeeded Sending build context to Docker daemon 5.12MB Successfully built abc123def456 Successfully tagged anihpj-django:v1.2.0 The push refers to repository [123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-django] abc123: Pushed v1.2.0: digest: sha256:xyz789... size: 1234 anihpj v1.2.0 pushed to ECR
# Django Models Integration - Complete Docker Push Flow from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import hashlib # Full push workflow for anihpj Django app repo = 'anihpj-django' tag = 'v1.2.0' # Get or create repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title=repo) # Add new image tag image = Skills.objects.create(name=tag) repository.skills.add(image) print("Login Succeeded") print("Successfully built abc123def456") print(f"Successfully tagged {repo}:{tag}") print(f"{repo}:{tag} pushed to ECR") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} new_image={tag}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django new_image=v1.2.0>
Example 3: CI/CD Login Script
#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD script for anihpj deployments
set -e

REGION="${AWS_REGION:-us-east-1}"
ACCOUNT=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query 'Account' --output text)
ECR_REGISTRY="$ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$REGION.amazonaws.com"

echo "Authenticating with anihpj ECR registry..."
aws ecr get-login-password --region $REGION | \
  docker login --username AWS --password-stdin $ECR_REGISTRY

echo "Building anihpj Docker image..."
docker build -t anihpj-django:$GIT_COMMIT .
docker tag anihpj-django:$GIT_COMMIT $ECR_REGISTRY/anihpj-django:$GIT_COMMIT
docker tag anihpj-django:$GIT_COMMIT $ECR_REGISTRY/anihpj-django:latest

echo "Pushing to anihpj ECR..."
docker push $ECR_REGISTRY/anihpj-django:$GIT_COMMIT
docker push $ECR_REGISTRY/anihpj-django:latest

echo "anihpj deployment images ready"
Authenticating with anihpj ECR registry... Login Succeeded Building anihpj Docker image... Successfully built abc123 Pushing to anihpj ECR... abc123def456: Pushed latest: digest: sha256:xyz789... anihpj deployment images ready
# Django Models Integration - CI/CD Login Script from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # CI/CD script for anihpj deployments region = 'us-east-1' account = '123456789012' git_commit = 'abc123' repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') print("Authenticating with anihpj ECR registry...") print("Login Succeeded") print("Building anihpj Docker image...") print("Successfully built abc123") # Add commit-based tag and latest commit_tag = Skills.objects.create(name=git_commit) latest_tag, _ = Skills.objects.get_or_create(name='latest') repository.skills.add(commit_tag, latest_tag) print("Pushing to anihpj ECR...") print(f"latest: digest: sha256:xyz789...") print("anihpj deployment images ready") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} commit={git_commit} images={repository.skills.count()}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django commit=abc123 images=3>

start-image-scan

Starts an image vulnerability scan.

Syntax
aws ecr start-image-scan \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-id {image-id}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-id - Image identifier (imageTag or imageDigest)
Return Value

Returns imageScanStatus with status (IN_PROGRESS, COMPLETE, or FAILED).

5 Detailed ECR start-image-scan Examples
Example 1: Scan anihpj Image
#!/bin/bash
# Start vulnerability scan
aws ecr start-image-scan \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-id imageTag=latest

echo "Vulnerability scan started for anihpj:latest"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "imageId": { "imageDigest": "sha256:abc123...", "imageTag": "latest" }, "imageScanStatus": { "status": "IN_PROGRESS" } } Vulnerability scan started for anihpj:latest
# Django Models Integration - Start Image Scan from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # Track vulnerability scan for anihpj image repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') image_tag = 'latest' # Create scan record scan_skill = Skills.objects.create( name=f'scan_started:{image_tag}:{timezone.now().date()}' ) repository.skills.add(scan_skill) print(f"Registry: 123456789012") print(f"Repository: {repository.title}") print(f"Image: {image_tag}") print(f"Status: IN_PROGRESS") print(f"<Skills name={scan_skill.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=scan_started:latest:2026-01-15>
Example 2: Scan All anihpj Images
#!/bin/bash
# Scan all tagged images
echo "Starting scans for all anihpj images..."

aws ecr list-images \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --filter tagStatus=TAGGED \
  --query 'imageIds[*].imageTag' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read TAG; do
    if [ -n "$TAG" ]; then
      echo "Scanning: $TAG"
      aws ecr start-image-scan \
        --repository-name anihpj-django \
        --image-id imageTag=$TAG
    fi
done

echo "All anihpj image scans initiated"
Starting scans for all anihpj images... Scanning: latest Scanning: v1.0.0 Scanning: v0.9.0 All anihpj image scans initiated
# Django Models Integration - Scan All Images from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # Bulk scan all anihpj image tags repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') image_tags = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='v').union( repository.skills.filter(name='latest') ) print("Starting scans for all anihpj images...") scans_started = [] for tag in image_tags: print(f"Scanning: {tag.name}") scan = Skills.objects.create( name=f'scan:{tag.name}:{timezone.now().date()}' ) scans_started.append(scan) print(f"All anihpj image scans initiated") print(f"<QuerySet count={len(scans_started)} scans_started>") # Output: <QuerySet count=3 scans_started>

describe-image-scan-findings

Returns the scan findings for the specified image.

Syntax
aws ecr describe-image-scan-findings \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-id {image-id}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-id - Image identifier (imageTag or imageDigest)
Return Value

Returns imageScanFindings with findingSeverityCounts and findings array.

5 Detailed ECR describe-image-scan-findings Examples
Example 1: Get anihpj Scan Results
#!/bin/bash
# Get vulnerability scan results
aws ecr describe-image-scan-findings \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-id imageTag=latest \
  --query 'imageScanFindings.findingSeverityCounts'
{ "CRITICAL": 0, "HIGH": 2, "MEDIUM": 5, "LOW": 12, "INFORMATIONAL": 3 }
# Django Models Integration - Get Scan Results from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Track vulnerability findings for anihpj image repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') image_tag = 'latest' # Store scan findings summary findings = { 'CRITICAL': 0, 'HIGH': 2, 'MEDIUM': 5, 'LOW': 12, 'INFORMATIONAL': 3 } total_vulns = sum(findings.values()) for severity, count in findings.items(): print(f"{severity}: {count}") # Store scan result result_skill = Skills.objects.create(name=f'scan_complete:latest:vulns={total_vulns}') repository.skills.add(result_skill) print(f"<Skills name={result_skill.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=scan_complete:latest:vulns=22>
Example 2: List Critical Vulnerabilities
#!/bin/bash
# List critical and high vulnerabilities
echo "anihpj Critical/High Vulnerabilities"
echo "======================================"

aws ecr describe-image-scan-findings \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-id imageTag=latest \
  --output json | jq -r '.imageScanFindings.findings[] | select(.severity == "CRITICAL" or .severity == "HIGH") | "\(.severity): \(.name) - \(.description[0:80])..."'
anihpj Critical/High Vulnerabilities ====================================== HIGH: CVE-2026-1234 - Buffer overflow vulnerability in libssl... HIGH: CVE-2026-5678 - Remote code execution in Python package...
# Django Models Integration - List Critical Vulnerabilities from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Track critical/high vulnerabilities for anihpj repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') print("anihpj Critical/High Vulnerabilities") print("======================================") # Store each critical vulnerability vulns = [ {'severity': 'HIGH', 'cve': 'CVE-2026-1234', 'desc': 'Buffer overflow in libssl'}, {'severity': 'HIGH', 'cve': 'CVE-2026-5678', 'desc': 'Remote code execution in Python'} ] for v in vulns: skill = Skills.objects.create(name=f"vuln:{v['cve']}") repository.skills.add(skill) print(f"{v['severity']}: {v['cve']} - {v['desc']}...") print(f"<QuerySet vulns_tracked={len(vulns)}>") # Output: <QuerySet vulns_tracked=2>
Example 3: Security Gate Check
#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD security gate for anihpj
REPO="anihpj-django"
TAG="latest"

echo "Checking anihpj image security..."

# Wait for scan to complete
while true; do
  STATUS=$(aws ecr describe-image-scan-findings \
    --repository-name $REPO \
    --image-id imageTag=$TAG \
    --query 'imageScanStatus.status' \
    --output text)
  
  if [ "$STATUS" == "COMPLETE" ]; then
    break
  fi
  echo "Scan in progress..."
  sleep 10
done

# Check for critical vulnerabilities
CRITICAL=$(aws ecr describe-image-scan-findings \
  --repository-name $REPO \
  --image-id imageTag=$TAG \
  --query 'imageScanFindings.findingSeverityCounts.CRITICAL // `0`' \
  --output text)

if [ "$CRITICAL" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "❌ FAILED: $CRITICAL critical vulnerabilities found"
  exit 1
else
  echo "✓ PASSED: No critical vulnerabilities in anihpj image"
fi
Checking anihpj image security... Scan in progress... ✓ PASSED: No critical vulnerabilities in anihpj image
# Django Models Integration - Security Gate Check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # CI/CD security gate for anihpj deployment repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') image_tag = 'latest' critical_count = 0 print("Checking anihpj image security...") # Check scan status scan = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='scan_complete:latest').first() if not scan: print("Scan in progress...") # Verify no critical vulnerabilities if critical_count == 0: gate_result = Skills.objects.create(name=f'gate_passed:{image_tag}:{timezone.now().date()}') repository.skills.add(gate_result) print("✓ PASSED: No critical vulnerabilities in anihpj image") else: print(f"❌ FAILED: {critical_count} critical vulnerabilities found") print(f"<Skills name={gate_result.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=gate_passed:latest:2026-01-15>

put-image-scanning-configuration

Updates the image scanning configuration for the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr put-image-scanning-configuration \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --image-scanning-configuration scanOnPush={true|false}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --image-scanning-configuration - Scanning configuration with scanOnPush
Return Value

Returns repositoryName and imageScanningConfiguration settings.

5 Detailed ECR put-image-scanning-configuration Examples
Example 1: Enable Scan on Push
#!/bin/bash
# Enable automatic scanning
aws ecr put-image-scanning-configuration \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --image-scanning-configuration scanOnPush=true

echo "anihpj images will be scanned automatically on push"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "imageScanningConfiguration": { "scanOnPush": true } } anihpj images will be scanned automatically on push
# Django Models Integration - Enable Scan on Push from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Enable automatic vulnerability scanning for anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Update scanning configuration scan_config = Skills.objects.create(name='scan_on_push:enabled') repository.skills.add(scan_config) print(f"Registry: 123456789012") print(f"Repository: {repository.title}") print(f"scanOnPush: true") print("anihpj images will be scanned automatically on push") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} scan_config={scan_config.name}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django scan_config=scan_on_push:enabled>

get-registry-scanning-configuration

Retrieves the scanning configuration for a registry.

Syntax
aws ecr get-registry-scanning-configuration
Parameters Description
  • No required parameters - Retrieves registry-level scanning config
Return Value

Returns scanningConfiguration with scanType and rules array.

5 Detailed ECR get-registry-scanning-configuration Examples
Example 1: Get Registry Scanning Config
#!/bin/bash
# Get registry-level scanning configuration
aws ecr get-registry-scanning-configuration
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "scanningConfiguration": { "scanType": "ENHANCED", "rules": [ { "scanFrequency": "CONTINUOUS_SCAN", "repositoryFilters": [ {"filter": "anihpj-*", "filterType": "WILDCARD"} ] } ] } }
# Django Models Integration - Get Registry Scanning Config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Check registry-level scanning configuration registry_id = '123456789012' # Query all repositories matching anihpj pattern anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') print(f"Registry: {registry_id}") print(f"Scan Type: ENHANCED") print(f"Scan Frequency: CONTINUOUS_SCAN") print(f"Filter: anihpj-* (WILDCARD)") print(f"Matching repos: {anihpj_repos.count()}") print(f"<QuerySet anihpj_repos count={anihpj_repos.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet anihpj_repos count=3>

put-registry-scanning-configuration

Creates or updates the scanning configuration for your private registry.

Syntax
aws ecr put-registry-scanning-configuration \
  --scan-type {BASIC|ENHANCED} \
  [--rules {rules}]
Parameters Description
  • --scan-type - BASIC or ENHANCED scanning
  • --rules - (Optional) Scanning rules for repositories
Return Value

Returns registryId and scanningConfiguration with applied settings.

5 Detailed ECR put-registry-scanning-configuration Examples
Example 1: Enable Enhanced Scanning for anihpj
#!/bin/bash
# Enable enhanced scanning for anihpj repos
aws ecr put-registry-scanning-configuration \
  --scan-type ENHANCED \
  --rules '[
    {
      "scanFrequency": "CONTINUOUS_SCAN",
      "repositoryFilters": [
        {"filter": "anihpj-*", "filterType": "WILDCARD"}
      ]
    }
  ]'

echo "Enhanced scanning enabled for all anihpj repositories"
{ "registryScanningConfiguration": { "scanType": "ENHANCED", "rules": [ { "scanFrequency": "CONTINUOUS_SCAN", "repositoryFilters": [...] } ] } } Enhanced scanning enabled for all anihpj repositories
# Django Models Integration - Enable Enhanced Scanning from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Configure enhanced scanning for anihpj repositories registry_id = '123456789012' # Get all anihpj repositories anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') # Create scanning configuration skill config = Skills.objects.create(name='registry_scan:enhanced:continuous') # Apply to all anihpj repos for repo in anihpj_repos: repo.skills.add(config) print(f"Scan Type: ENHANCED") print(f"Scan Frequency: CONTINUOUS_SCAN") print(f"Repos affected: {anihpj_repos.count()}") print("Enhanced scanning enabled for all anihpj repositories") print(f"<QuerySet anihpj_repos count={anihpj_repos.count()} scan=enhanced>") # Output: <QuerySet anihpj_repos count=3 scan=enhanced>

get-lifecycle-policy

Retrieves the lifecycle policy for the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr get-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name {repository-name}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
Return Value

Returns registryId, repositoryName, and lifecyclePolicyText JSON.

5 Detailed ECR get-lifecycle-policy Examples
Example 1: Get anihpj Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get lifecycle policy
aws ecr get-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --query 'lifecyclePolicyText' \
  --output text | jq .
{ "rules": [ { "rulePriority": 1, "description": "Keep last 10 images", "selection": { "tagStatus": "any", "countType": "imageCountMoreThan", "countNumber": 10 }, "action": { "type": "expire" } } ] }
# Django Models Integration - Get Lifecycle Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Get lifecycle policy for anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Get lifecycle rules lifecycle_rules = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='lifecycle:') print("Lifecycle Policy Rules:") print(f" Rule Priority: 1") print(f" Description: Keep last 10 images") print(f" Tag Status: any") print(f" Count Type: imageCountMoreThan") print(f" Count Number: 10") print(f" Action: expire") print(f"<QuerySet lifecycle_rules count={lifecycle_rules.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet lifecycle_rules count=1>

put-lifecycle-policy

Creates or updates the lifecycle policy for the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --lifecycle-policy-text {policy-text}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --lifecycle-policy-text - Lifecycle policy JSON
Return Value

Returns registryId, repositoryName, and lifecyclePolicyText.

5 Detailed ECR put-lifecycle-policy Examples
Example 1: Keep Last 10 anihpj Images
#!/bin/bash
# Create lifecycle policy to keep last 10 images
aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --lifecycle-policy-text '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "rulePriority": 1,
        "description": "Keep only 10 most recent anihpj images",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "any",
          "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
          "countNumber": 10
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "expire"
        }
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj lifecycle policy configured"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "lifecyclePolicyText": "..." } anihpj lifecycle policy configured
# Django Models Integration - Put Lifecycle Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Create lifecycle policy for anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Create lifecycle rule skill lifecycle = Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:keep_last_10') repository.skills.add(lifecycle) print(f"Registry: 123456789012") print(f"Repository: {repository.title}") print("Policy: Keep only 10 most recent images") print("anihpj lifecycle policy configured") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} lifecycle={lifecycle.name}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django lifecycle=lifecycle:keep_last_10>
Example 2: Clean Untagged Images After 7 Days
#!/bin/bash
# Remove untagged images after 7 days
aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --lifecycle-policy-text '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "rulePriority": 1,
        "description": "Expire untagged anihpj images after 7 days",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "untagged",
          "countType": "sinceImagePushed",
          "countUnit": "days",
          "countNumber": 7
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "expire"
        }
      },
      {
        "rulePriority": 2,
        "description": "Keep last 20 tagged anihpj images",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "tagged",
          "tagPrefixList": ["v"],
          "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
          "countNumber": 20
        },
        "action": {
          "type": "expire"
        }
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj cleanup policy configured"
anihpj cleanup policy configured
# Django Models Integration - Clean Untagged Images from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Configure cleanup policy for anihpj repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Create cleanup rules rule1 = Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:untagged_7days') rule2 = Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:keep_20_tagged') repository.skills.add(rule1, rule2) print("Rule 1: Expire untagged images after 7 days") print("Rule 2: Keep last 20 tagged images") print("anihpj cleanup policy configured") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} rules=2>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django rules=2>
Example 3: Production Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Comprehensive lifecycle policy for anihpj
aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --lifecycle-policy-text '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "rulePriority": 1,
        "description": "Keep production tags indefinitely",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "tagged",
          "tagPrefixList": ["prod-", "release-"],
          "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
          "countNumber": 100
        },
        "action": {"type": "expire"}
      },
      {
        "rulePriority": 2,
        "description": "Keep last 5 dev images",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "tagged",
          "tagPrefixList": ["dev-", "feature-"],
          "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
          "countNumber": 5
        },
        "action": {"type": "expire"}
      },
      {
        "rulePriority": 3,
        "description": "Remove untagged after 1 day",
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "untagged",
          "countType": "sinceImagePushed",
          "countUnit": "days",
          "countNumber": 1
        },
        "action": {"type": "expire"}
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj production lifecycle policy applied"
anihpj production lifecycle policy applied
# Django Models Integration - Production Lifecycle Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Comprehensive lifecycle policy for anihpj repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Clear existing lifecycle rules repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='lifecycle:').delete() # Create production rules rules = [ Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:prod_indefinite'), Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:dev_keep_5'), Skills.objects.create(name='lifecycle:untagged_1day') ] for rule in rules: repository.skills.add(rule) print("Rule 1: Keep production tags indefinitely") print("Rule 2: Keep last 5 dev images") print("Rule 3: Remove untagged after 1 day") print("anihpj production lifecycle policy applied") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} rules={len(rules)}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django rules=3>

delete-lifecycle-policy

Deletes the lifecycle policy associated with the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr delete-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name {repository-name}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
Return Value

Returns registryId, repositoryName, and deleted lifecyclePolicyText.

5 Detailed ECR delete-lifecycle-policy Examples
Example 1: Delete anihpj Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Remove lifecycle policy
aws ecr delete-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name anihpj-django

echo "anihpj lifecycle policy deleted"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "lifecyclePolicyText": "..." } anihpj lifecycle policy deleted
# Django Models Integration - Delete Lifecycle Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Remove lifecycle policy from anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Delete all lifecycle rules deleted_count = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='lifecycle:').count() repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='lifecycle:').delete() print(f"Registry: 123456789012") print(f"Repository: {repository.title}") print(f"Lifecycle rules removed: {deleted_count}") print("anihpj lifecycle policy deleted") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} lifecycle_rules_deleted={deleted_count}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django lifecycle_rules_deleted=3>

start-lifecycle-policy-preview

Starts a preview of a lifecycle policy for the specified repository.

Syntax
aws ecr start-lifecycle-policy-preview \
  --repository-name {repository-name} \
  --lifecycle-policy-text {policy-text}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
  • --lifecycle-policy-text - Lifecycle policy to preview
Return Value

Returns registryId, repositoryName, and status (IN_PROGRESS).

5 Detailed ECR start-lifecycle-policy-preview Examples
Example 1: Preview anihpj Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Preview what would be deleted
aws ecr start-lifecycle-policy-preview \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --lifecycle-policy-text '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "rulePriority": 1,
        "selection": {
          "tagStatus": "any",
          "countType": "imageCountMoreThan",
          "countNumber": 5
        },
        "action": {"type": "expire"}
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj lifecycle preview started"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "repositoryName": "anihpj-django", "lifecyclePolicyText": "...", "status": "IN_PROGRESS" } anihpj lifecycle preview started
# Django Models Integration - Start Lifecycle Preview from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # Preview lifecycle policy before applying repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Create preview record preview = Skills.objects.create( name=f'lifecycle_preview:{timezone.now().date()}:in_progress' ) repository.skills.add(preview) print(f"Registry: 123456789012") print(f"Repository: {repository.title}") print(f"Status: IN_PROGRESS") print("anihpj lifecycle preview started") print(f"<Skills name={preview.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=lifecycle_preview:2026-01-15:in_progress>

get-lifecycle-policy-preview

Retrieves the results of the lifecycle policy preview request.

Syntax
aws ecr get-lifecycle-policy-preview \
  --repository-name {repository-name}
Parameters Description
  • --repository-name - Name of the repository
Return Value

Returns previewResults array with images that would be affected.

5 Detailed ECR get-lifecycle-policy-preview Examples
Example 1: Get anihpj Preview Results
#!/bin/bash
# Get preview results
aws ecr get-lifecycle-policy-preview \
  --repository-name anihpj-django \
  --query 'previewResults[*].[imageTags[0],appliedRulePriority]' \
  --output table
----------------------------------- | GetLifecyclePolicyPreview | +-----------+---------------------+ | v0.7.0 | 1 | | v0.6.0 | 1 | | v0.5.0 | 1 | +-----------+---------------------+ These images would be deleted by the policy
# Django Models Integration - Get Lifecycle Preview Results from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Get lifecycle preview results for anihpj repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Simulate preview results - images that would be deleted images_to_delete = repository.skills.filter( name__in=['v0.7.0', 'v0.6.0', 'v0.5.0'] ) print("Images that would be deleted by the policy:") for img in images_to_delete: print(f" {img.name} - Rule Priority: 1") print(f"Total images affected: {images_to_delete.count()}") print("These images would be deleted by the policy") print(f"<QuerySet images_to_delete count={images_to_delete.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet images_to_delete count=3>

describe-registry

Describes the settings for a registry.

Syntax
aws ecr describe-registry
Parameters Description
  • No required parameters - Retrieves registry configuration
Return Value

Returns registryId and replicationConfiguration settings.

5 Detailed ECR describe-registry Examples
Example 1: Describe anihpj Registry
#!/bin/bash
# Get registry configuration
aws ecr describe-registry
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "replicationConfiguration": { "rules": [ { "destinations": [ { "region": "eu-west-1", "registryId": "123456789012" } ], "repositoryFilters": [ {"filter": "anihpj-*", "filterType": "PREFIX_MATCH"} ] } ] } }
# Django Models Integration - Describe Registry from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Get registry configuration for anihpj registry_id = '123456789012' # Get replication destinations locations = Location.objects.filter(name__in=['us-east-1', 'eu-west-1']) # Get repositories matching anihpj pattern anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') print(f"Registry: {registry_id}") print("Replication Configuration:") print(" Destination: eu-west-1") print(" Filter: anihpj-* (PREFIX_MATCH)") print(f" Repos replicated: {anihpj_repos.count()}") print(f"<QuerySet locations={locations.count()} repos={anihpj_repos.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet locations=2 repos=3>

get-registry-policy

Retrieves the permissions policy for a registry.

Syntax
aws ecr get-registry-policy
Parameters Description
  • No required parameters - Retrieves registry-level policy
Return Value

Returns registryId and policyText JSON document.

5 Detailed ECR get-registry-policy Examples
Example 1: Get Registry Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get registry-level policy
aws ecr get-registry-policy
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "policyText": "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[{\"Sid\":\"ReplicationAccess\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"AWS\":\"arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root\"},\"Action\":[\"ecr:CreateRepository\",\"ecr:ReplicateImage\"]}]}" }
# Django Models Integration - Get Registry Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Get registry-level policy registry_id = '123456789012' # Query users with replication access authorized_accounts = User.objects.filter( skills__name__contains='ecr:replication_access' ) print(f"Registry: {registry_id}") print("Policy Statement:") print(" Sid: ReplicationAccess") print(" Effect: Allow") print(" Actions: CreateRepository, ReplicateImage") print(f" Authorized accounts: {authorized_accounts.count()}") print(f"<QuerySet authorized_accounts count={authorized_accounts.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet authorized_accounts count=1>

put-registry-policy

Creates or updates the permissions policy for your registry.

Syntax
aws ecr put-registry-policy \
  --policy-text {policy-text}
Parameters Description
  • --policy-text - JSON policy document for registry
Return Value

Returns registryId and applied policyText.

5 Detailed ECR put-registry-policy Examples
Example 1: Allow Cross-Account Replication
#!/bin/bash
# Allow replication to another account
aws ecr put-registry-policy \
  --policy-text '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
      {
        "Sid": "anihpjReplicationAccess",
        "Effect": "Allow",
        "Principal": {"AWS": "arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root"},
        "Action": [
          "ecr:CreateRepository",
          "ecr:ReplicateImage"
        ],
        "Resource": "*"
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj registry replication policy configured"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "policyText": "..." } anihpj registry replication policy configured
# Django Models Integration - Put Registry Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Configure cross-account replication policy registry_id = '123456789012' target_account = '987654321098' # Create policy skill policy = Skills.objects.create(name='registry_policy:replication:allow') # Grant access to target account user, _ = User.objects.get_or_create(username=f'account_{target_account}') user.skills.add(policy) print(f"Registry: {registry_id}") print(f"Policy: anihpjReplicationAccess") print(f"Target Account: {target_account}") print("anihpj registry replication policy configured") print(f"<User account={target_account} policy={policy.name}>") # Output: <User account=987654321098 policy=registry_policy:replication:allow>

delete-registry-policy

Deletes the registry permissions policy.

Syntax
aws ecr delete-registry-policy
Parameters Description
  • No required parameters - Deletes registry-level policy
Return Value

Returns registryId and deleted policyText.

5 Detailed ECR delete-registry-policy Examples
Example 1: Delete Registry Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Remove registry policy
aws ecr delete-registry-policy

echo "anihpj registry policy deleted"
{ "registryId": "123456789012", "policyText": "..." } anihpj registry policy deleted
# Django Models Integration - Delete Registry Policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Remove registry policy registry_id = '123456789012' # Remove policy skills deleted = Skills.objects.filter(name__startswith='registry_policy:').delete() print(f"Registry: {registry_id}") print(f"Policies removed: {deleted[0]}") print("anihpj registry policy deleted") print(f"<Skills deleted_count={deleted[0]}>") # Output: <Skills deleted_count=1>

put-replication-configuration

Creates or updates the replication configuration for a registry.

Syntax
aws ecr put-replication-configuration \
  --replication-configuration {replication-configuration}
Parameters Description
  • --replication-configuration - Replication rules with destinations and filters
Return Value

Returns replicationConfiguration with rules array.

5 Detailed ECR put-replication-configuration Examples
Example 1: Replicate anihpj to EU Region
#!/bin/bash
# Set up cross-region replication
aws ecr put-replication-configuration \
  --replication-configuration '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "destinations": [
          {"region": "eu-west-1", "registryId": "123456789012"}
        ],
        "repositoryFilters": [
          {"filter": "anihpj-", "filterType": "PREFIX_MATCH"}
        ]
      }
    ]
  }'

echo "anihpj images will replicate to EU"
{ "replicationConfiguration": { "rules": [ { "destinations": [{"region": "eu-west-1", "registryId": "123456789012"}], "repositoryFilters": [{"filter": "anihpj-", "filterType": "PREFIX_MATCH"}] } ] } } anihpj images will replicate to EU
# Django Models Integration - Put Replication Configuration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Configure cross-region replication for anihpj source_region = 'us-east-1' dest_region = 'eu-west-1' # Create location for replication destination dest_location, _ = Location.objects.get_or_create(name=dest_region) # Tag anihpj repos for replication anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') replication_skill = Skills.objects.create(name=f'replication:{dest_region}') for repo in anihpj_repos: repo.skills.add(replication_skill) repo.location = dest_location repo.save() print(f"Destination: {dest_region}") print(f"Filter: anihpj- (PREFIX_MATCH)") print(f"Repos to replicate: {anihpj_repos.count()}") print("anihpj images will replicate to EU") print(f"<QuerySet anihpj_repos count={anihpj_repos.count()} replication={dest_region}>") # Output: <QuerySet anihpj_repos count=3 replication=eu-west-1>

create-pull-through-cache-rule

Creates a pull through cache rule. A pull through cache rule provides a way to cache images from an external public registry in your Amazon ECR private registry.

Syntax
aws ecr create-pull-through-cache-rule \
  --ecr-repository-prefix {prefix} \
  --upstream-registry-url {url}
Parameters Description
  • --ecr-repository-prefix - Prefix for cached repositories
  • --upstream-registry-url - Source registry URL (docker.io, etc.)
Return Value

Returns ecrRepositoryPrefix, upstreamRegistryUrl, and createdAt timestamp.

5 Detailed ECR create-pull-through-cache-rule Examples
Example 1: Cache Docker Hub for anihpj
#!/bin/bash
# Create pull through cache for Docker Hub
aws ecr create-pull-through-cache-rule \
  --ecr-repository-prefix anihpj-docker-hub \
  --upstream-registry-url docker.io

echo "Docker Hub cache configured for anihpj"
{ "ecrRepositoryPrefix": "anihpj-docker-hub", "upstreamRegistryUrl": "docker.io", "createdAt": "2026-01-17T15:00:00Z" } Docker Hub cache configured for anihpj
# Django Models Integration - Create Pull Through Cache Rule from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # Create pull through cache for Docker Hub prefix = 'anihpj-docker-hub' upstream = 'docker.io' # Create cache rule as skill cache_rule = Skills.objects.create(name=f'cache_rule:{prefix}:{upstream}') print(f"ECR Repository Prefix: {prefix}") print(f"Upstream Registry: {upstream}") print(f"Created: {timezone.now()}") print("Docker Hub cache configured for anihpj") print(f"<Skills name={cache_rule.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=cache_rule:anihpj-docker-hub:docker.io>
Example 2: Cache ECR Public Gallery
#!/bin/bash
# Cache public ECR images
aws ecr create-pull-through-cache-rule \
  --ecr-repository-prefix anihpj-ecr-public \
  --upstream-registry-url public.ecr.aws

echo "ECR Public cache configured"
{ "ecrRepositoryPrefix": "anihpj-ecr-public", "upstreamRegistryUrl": "public.ecr.aws" } ECR Public cache configured
# Django Models Integration - Cache ECR Public Gallery from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Cache ECR public images for anihpj prefix = 'anihpj-ecr-public' upstream = 'public.ecr.aws' # Create cache rule cache_rule = Skills.objects.create(name=f'cache_rule:{prefix}:{upstream}') print(f"ECR Repository Prefix: {prefix}") print(f"Upstream Registry: {upstream}") print("ECR Public cache configured") print(f"<Skills name={cache_rule.name}>") # Output: <Skills name=cache_rule:anihpj-ecr-public:public.ecr.aws>

describe-pull-through-cache-rules

Returns the pull through cache rules for a registry.

Syntax
aws ecr describe-pull-through-cache-rules
Parameters Description
  • --ecr-repository-prefixes - (Optional) Filter by prefixes
Return Value

Returns pullThroughCacheRules array with prefixes and upstream URLs.

5 Detailed ECR describe-pull-through-cache-rules Examples
Example 1: List anihpj Cache Rules
#!/bin/bash
# List all pull through cache rules
aws ecr describe-pull-through-cache-rules \
  --query 'pullThroughCacheRules[*].[ecrRepositoryPrefix,upstreamRegistryUrl]' \
  --output table
------------------------------------------------------- | DescribePullThroughCacheRules | +----------------------+------------------------------+ | anihpj-docker-hub | docker.io | | anihpj-ecr-public | public.ecr.aws | | anihpj-ghcr | ghcr.io | +----------------------+------------------------------+
# Django Models Integration - List Cache Rules from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # List all pull through cache rules for anihpj cache_rules = Skills.objects.filter(name__startswith='cache_rule:') print("Pull Through Cache Rules:") for rule in cache_rules: parts = rule.name.split(':') prefix = parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else 'unknown' upstream = parts[2] if len(parts) > 2 else 'unknown' print(f" {prefix} -> {upstream}") print(f"Total cache rules: {cache_rules.count()}") print(f"<QuerySet cache_rules count={cache_rules.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet cache_rules count=3>

delete-pull-through-cache-rule

Deletes a pull through cache rule.

Syntax
aws ecr delete-pull-through-cache-rule \
  --ecr-repository-prefix {prefix}
Parameters Description
  • --ecr-repository-prefix - Prefix of the cache rule to delete
Return Value

Returns deleted ecrRepositoryPrefix and upstreamRegistryUrl.

5 Detailed ECR delete-pull-through-cache-rule Examples
Example 1: Delete Cache Rule
#!/bin/bash
# Remove unused cache rule
aws ecr delete-pull-through-cache-rule \
  --ecr-repository-prefix anihpj-old-cache

echo "anihpj cache rule deleted"
{ "ecrRepositoryPrefix": "anihpj-old-cache", "upstreamRegistryUrl": "docker.io" } anihpj cache rule deleted
# Django Models Integration - Delete Cache Rule from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Remove unused cache rule prefix = 'anihpj-old-cache' # Find and delete cache rule cache_rule = Skills.objects.filter(name__startswith=f'cache_rule:{prefix}:').first() if cache_rule: upstream = cache_rule.name.split(':')[2] cache_rule.delete() print(f"ECR Repository Prefix: {prefix}") print(f"Upstream Registry: {upstream}") print("anihpj cache rule deleted") print(f"<Skills deleted prefix={prefix}>") # Output: <Skills deleted prefix=anihpj-old-cache>

tag-resource

Adds specified tags to a resource with the specified ARN.

Syntax
aws ecr tag-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  --tags {tags}
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn - ARN of the ECR resource
  • --tags - List of tags as Key=value pairs
Return Value

Returns no content on success. Tags are added to resource.

5 Detailed ECR tag-resource Examples
Example 1: Tag anihpj Repository
#!/bin/bash
# Add tags to repository
aws ecr tag-resource \
  --resource-arn "arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-django" \
  --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Team,Value=backend

echo "Tags added to anihpj repository"
Tags added to anihpj repository
# Django Models Integration - Tag Repository from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Add tags to anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Create tag skills tags = [ Skills.objects.create(name='tag:Project=anihpj'), Skills.objects.create(name='tag:Environment=production'), Skills.objects.create(name='tag:Team=backend') ] for tag in tags: repository.skills.add(tag) print("Tags added:") for tag in tags: print(f" {tag.name.replace('tag:', '')}") print("Tags added to anihpj repository") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} tags={len(tags)}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django tags=3>
Example 2: Tag All anihpj Repositories
#!/bin/bash
# Tag all anihpj repositories
echo "Tagging all anihpj repositories..."

aws ecr describe-repositories \
  --query 'repositories[?starts_with(repositoryName, `anihpj`)].repositoryArn' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read ARN; do
    echo "Tagging: $ARN"
    aws ecr tag-resource \
      --resource-arn $ARN \
      --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=ManagedBy,Value=cli
done

echo "All anihpj repositories tagged"
Tagging all anihpj repositories... Tagging: arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-django Tagging: arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-nginx Tagging: arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-celery All anihpj repositories tagged
# Django Models Integration - Tag All Repositories from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Tag all anihpj repositories anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') print("Tagging all anihpj repositories...") # Create common tags project_tag = Skills.objects.create(name='tag:Project=anihpj') managed_tag = Skills.objects.create(name='tag:ManagedBy=cli') for repo in anihpj_repos: print(f"Tagging: {repo.title}") repo.skills.add(project_tag, managed_tag) print("All anihpj repositories tagged") print(f"<QuerySet repos_tagged count={anihpj_repos.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet repos_tagged count=3>

untag-resource

Deletes specified tags from a resource.

Syntax
aws ecr untag-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  --tag-keys {tag-keys}
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn - ARN of the ECR resource
  • --tag-keys - List of tag keys to remove
Return Value

Returns no content on success. Tags are removed from resource.

5 Detailed ECR untag-resource Examples
Example 1: Remove Tags from anihpj Repository
#!/bin/bash
# Remove specific tags
aws ecr untag-resource \
  --resource-arn "arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-django" \
  --tag-keys Temporary Test

echo "Tags removed from anihpj repository"
Tags removed from anihpj repository
# Django Models Integration - Remove Tags from Repository from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # Remove specific tags from anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Remove Temporary and Test tags tags_to_remove = ['tag:Temporary', 'tag:Test'] removed_count = 0 for tag_name in tags_to_remove: tag = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith=tag_name).first() if tag: repository.skills.remove(tag) removed_count += 1 print(f"Tags removed: {removed_count}") print("Tags removed from anihpj repository") print(f"<JobPost title={repository.title} tags_removed={removed_count}>") # Output: <JobPost title=anihpj-django tags_removed=2>

list-tags-for-resource

Lists the tags for an Amazon ECR resource.

Syntax
aws ecr list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn}
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn - ARN of the ECR resource
Return Value

Returns tags array with key and value for each tag.

5 Detailed ECR list-tags-for-resource Examples
Example 1: List anihpj Repository Tags
#!/bin/bash
# List tags for repository
aws ecr list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-arn "arn:aws:ecr:us-east-1:123456789012:repository/anihpj-django"
{ "tags": [ {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"}, {"Key": "Team", "Value": "backend"} ] }
# Django Models Integration - List Repository Tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # List tags for anihpj repository repository = JobPost.objects.get(title='anihpj-django') # Get all tags for this repository tags = repository.skills.filter(name__startswith='tag:') print("Repository Tags:") for tag in tags: key_value = tag.name.replace('tag:', '').split('=') print(f" {key_value[0]}: {key_value[1] if len(key_value) > 1 else 'N/A'}") print(f"<QuerySet tags count={tags.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet tags count=3>
Example 2: List Tags for All anihpj Repos
#!/bin/bash
# List tags for all anihpj repositories
echo "anihpj Repository Tags"
echo "========================"

aws ecr describe-repositories \
  --query 'repositories[?starts_with(repositoryName, `anihpj`)].repositoryArn' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read ARN; do
    REPO_NAME=$(echo $ARN | rev | cut -d'/' -f1 | rev)
    echo ""
    echo "Repository: $REPO_NAME"
    
    aws ecr list-tags-for-resource \
      --resource-arn $ARN \
      --query 'tags[*].[Key,Value]' \
      --output table
done
anihpj Repository Tags ======================== Repository: anihpj-django ----------------------------- | Key | Value | +-------------+-------------+ | Project | anihpj | | Environment | production | +-------------+-------------+ Repository: anihpj-nginx ----------------------------- | Key | Value | +-------------+-------------+ | Project | anihpj | | Environment | production | +-------------+-------------+
# Django Models Integration - List Tags for All Repos from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost # List tags for all anihpj repositories anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') print("anihpj Repository Tags") print("========================") for repo in anihpj_repos: print(f"\nRepository: {repo.title}") tags = repo.skills.filter(name__startswith='tag:') for tag in tags: parts = tag.name.replace('tag:', '').split('=') print(f" {parts[0]}: {parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else 'N/A'}") print(f"<QuerySet anihpj_repos count={anihpj_repos.count()}>") # Output: <QuerySet anihpj_repos count=2>
Example 3: Export anihpj Tags Report
#!/bin/bash
# Export tags to CSV
OUTPUT_FILE="anihpj-ecr-tags.csv"

echo "Repository,Key,Value" > $OUTPUT_FILE

aws ecr describe-repositories \
  --query 'repositories[?starts_with(repositoryName, `anihpj`)].{name: repositoryName, arn: repositoryArn}' \
  --output json | jq -r '.[] | "\(.name),\(.arn)"' | while IFS=',' read NAME ARN; do
    aws ecr list-tags-for-resource \
      --resource-arn $ARN \
      --output json | jq -r --arg name "$NAME" '.tags[] | "\($name),\(.Key),\(.Value)"' >> $OUTPUT_FILE
done

echo "anihpj ECR tags exported to $OUTPUT_FILE"
cat $OUTPUT_FILE
anihpj ECR tags exported to anihpj-ecr-tags.csv Repository,Key,Value anihpj-django,Project,anihpj anihpj-django,Environment,production anihpj-nginx,Project,anihpj anihpj-nginx,Environment,production
# Django Models Integration - Export Tags Report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import csv # Export tags to CSV output_file = 'anihpj-ecr-tags.csv' anihpj_repos = JobPost.objects.filter(title__startswith='anihpj-') with open(output_file, 'w', newline='') as f: writer = csv.writer(f) writer.writerow(['Repository', 'Key', 'Value']) for repo in anihpj_repos: tags = repo.skills.filter(name__startswith='tag:') for tag in tags: parts = tag.name.replace('tag:', '').split('=') writer.writerow([repo.title, parts[0], parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else '']) print(f"anihpj ECR tags exported to {output_file}") total_tags = sum(r.skills.filter(name__startswith='tag:').count() for r in anihpj_repos) print(f"<QuerySet repos={anihpj_repos.count()} tags_exported={total_tags}>") # Output: <QuerySet repos=2 tags_exported=4>

🔒 Security & Identity

AWS Security services help you secure your applications and data. This section covers IAM for identity and access management, Cognito for user authentication, Certificate Manager for SSL/TLS, Secrets Manager for credential management, and KMS for encryption key management.
